column4: Love comes to us as input: we don't initiate it or make it up; it's all given to us and received by us; all Grace. It comes to us as an answer. But eventually we notice that if it were not the answer, if something else were the answer, it would be the wrong answer. If the god we worshipped were an asshole, then we would be, too: we are responsible for our conception of the universe, beginning especially with respect to what's worth doing, worth living for. If God were a tyrant--reigning over the order that so much of religion and government and advertising uses to manipulate and control human beings--we ought to resist that god, rather than capitulate and obey. (Happily, God is not.) Viewed from this perspective, this stance, love--our love--is an output. It is our calculated answer at the most fundamental level to the question of what the hell is going on, and what we should do about it.* Viewed this way, we ourselves are the source of love. We insist on it; we'll take a stand for it. In that regard, we participate in love-motivated creation, in the sourcing and disseminating of the laws (the purpose, and the chosen means) of the universe. We are right in the beginning with the Father, and without us is not anything made that gets made. That word of love--to which we are first converted in time, but from whom that word authoritatively emmenates--is our word (and in that respect the Father and we are one.) From another perspective, we are that spoken word. (We are Christ, begotten, not made.) Both of these complementary perspectives remain affirmed: the one calculates, expressed in space-time, and affirms what the other timelessly sees and shares. The created universe is where God does their thinking in such a way--bit by bit, or qbit by qbit, or entangled cluster by entangled cluster thereof--that we participate in it as our own thought.
column11: If humankind is to thrive, we need to let go of any religion that wounds and kills. Some of what we believe about God is actually about us; at times we create God in our own image. In other words, some of us imagine God as punitive, angry, and vengeful because these are aspects of ourselves that make us feel powerful and protected, rather than vulnerable. But we need to exercise a spiritual imagination free of fear and shed the constraints of unhealthy religion. Hate-filled religion needs an exorcism!  From Rev. Dr. Jacqui Lewis in CAC newsletter Dec 2, 2021.

column5: Intractable Disputes about the Natural Law (IDNL) p. 21: MacIntyre on Aquinas:
column6: "Truth,that is,is a relationship to be achieved between a particular intellect and some object about which it judges,a relationship that satisfies two conditions: first,that how the thinker conceives of the object has become identical with how that object is and, secondly, that the thinker conceives of the object as she or he does, because and only because that is how the object is"
column8: Aq has a broader notion of T than this, which won't as stated (here, by M) cover God's relationship to things known. They are in some way identical with God's knowledge, but they are how they are because of how God conceives of them, not the other way around. So also for products of art, craft, any creative enterprise. The central question in an incarnational and christic universe is how much of the divine in us is creative with respect to the balance of creation. In raising children, very much so (raise up a child...). In setting examples, interceding, even reconceiving: these involve free acts; are creative; are emergent properties; not "localized" in the objects of cognition but relational, creative, unfolding. Perhaps we are inescabably entangled with all things known and knowable, bringing the world to pass as we relate to it. So "Truth is a maximally stable relationship between the universe as knowing and the universe as known." Insofar as there are multiple ways to conceive of itself in stable ways, so far is truth relative to conceptual schemes. The realist hope as embodied in science and more fundamentally in rational inquiry is that the universe (we) may become one in knowing, with multiple approaches to knowing achieving a kind of unity that rests on mutually recognized complementarity; in short, that the universe is coherent, in both theoretical and practical orders.
column11: so "a house is midway between the knowledge of the builder who made it, and the knowledge of the one who gathers his knowledge of the house from the house already built." Ia Q. 14 a.8 ad 3. We created knowers are thus co-creators. In that sense, without us (in Christ, in mature humanity, as a whole) is not anything made, that is made. If we would agree, in full K circumstances, as to how to behold a given object, then there is a fact, and truth, about that object.

column8: IDM p.. 23: If I am to engage with you in shared rational enquiry,we must both be assured that we can expect the other to speak the truth, as she or he understands it. There must be no deceptive or intentionally misleading speech.
column9: TL: 'Not intentionally misleading', all-things-considered. Some casuistry is called for here, Jesuitical or otherwise. Speech has multiple effects; among these may be roads that lead to truth in various ways. One of those may be through consideration of a foreseen mistake, not intended but permitted, to enable fruitful exploration of a familiar intellectual trap, say. Plato's Republic is full of such multi-impactful speech. Such foreseen and beneficial rethinking is entirely relevant as to the moral status of the utterance; there is no intent to mislead, but there may be a foreseen mistake, whose uncovering is optimized even at the point of introducing the circumstances in which the mistake is likely to be made. This is not intentional misleading, but it may seem to be to some under some circumstances, and not without evidence for their perceptions.

And what is the nature, the metaphysical basis, that picks some of these trajectories as "worthwhile" and fullfillling? What is the basis for worth, here, for valuation? The ends? My nature? Nature? Isn't this a negotiated settlement in which my say--not without information, but neither without me--is definititve? Here's an answer: love is the metaphysical foundation, the gift of being and of freedom to me, of by me, to others, a lot or a little. Who is to say? In the end, me. And you.
column11: Way later (1/16/21) on rereading: M's point is that ends don't just describe what is aimed at, but what should be, and thus cannot be psychological states. (A real problem for pCRm, since everything is a psychological state.) So there must be some distinction: not my present psych states, not simply that for anyone at any time, except perhaps for those states constituting the divine mind, the whole, maximally aware of the optimal interconnectedness not of its parts--it has none--but of its perspectives, of which it has many, and which perhaps I and you and we all are one (the p in pCRm.)
column12: Counterfactuals about the impact of additional information on psychological states have the remove from current states required by M's critique of the simple identification of psychological states. They are more than present motives, the end of the line for chains of current reasoning. They are normative (if incrementally and tentatively); claims about sufficiency (as end of the line not for current reasons but for any defensible reason) can be made for reasons and can be empirically justified as the best accounts so far, aiming at accounts which cannot be overturned in any stable way.
column13: If these features of counterfactual claims about the impact of information--this is good, and no more information will show it not to be--are sufficient to ground all the features which present psychological states cannot provide, then the claim that ends are not psychological states is too fast. Indeed they are not and cannot be identified with just any psychological state. But there are some psychological states which can align in content with proper claims about ends; these are the psychological states of virtuous (well-formed, in contact with information through formation) and adequately-informed members of stable communites. And if they align in content, why not suppose also that they align in responsibility: that these are the psychological states of persons so configured is precisely the reason why the reasons they give, the ends they cite, are normative. Persons ignorant of those ends are, as Aristotle says, not ignorant but wicked. It is their character that is flawed, not their current understanding of the facts. So this appeal to counterfactuals about information is a sufficient grounds for their normative role as ends.
column15: Also, there is a reductio argument available: suppose such counterfactuals obtained, but ends were aligned with some other standard. It would follow that optimally-informed persons would deviate from those ends. So it would be contrary to practical reason to pursue those ends. The resulting standards would be both irrational and unnecessary (doing no useful work, and in fact, only harm.)

column5: does the whole have a precise interpretation, such as a deductive argument does? Perhaps not
column6: perhaps the whole, the song that dialectic sings, is a dynamic entanglement of elements, mutually enlivening and limiting one another in an illumnating/guiding, beneficial/hopeful, beautiful/tantalizing way. The right side of these marks of the true,  the good and the beautiful are dynamic elements. There is no fixed account of them, and the first principles from which we argue deductively are held in place and thereby justified by the very promise of this very emerging coherence. So to interpret a whole system of thought and practice, such as St. T's, is to embrace a tradition of inquiry, as much a direction a current destination. To interprent is to lock in the direction, coherent with but surpassing the answers provided.

column8: eg: for Utilitarians, see them as guardians of respect for universality that rightly emerges from respect (whether or not adequately articulated by Utils) for equal dignity of all persons, drawn to consistent commitment (think Peter Singer), following the arguments where they lead and living as reason seems to require. Invite them to lead the way by considering for the sake of its utilitarian value a tradition that grounds that equal dignity in an account of human prosperity (the great-making feature of any such acct on Util grounds)
column10: a Tradition proves itself by providing evidence of deep immersion into not just achievements but promise of its rivals..."persuading them, while hiding from them that they are not healthy"...or rather, not at all hiding, but neither emphasizing. From Philpott's essay in IDM: "Rather,as the philosopher Charles Taylor argues,an overlapping consensus can and ought to arise from a convergence among interlocutors arguing from their tradition’s deepest warrants. 31"
column11: note a parallel: seeing the promise in a tradition, and in a person, are both requirements for imaginately inhabiting their perspectives; both involve dialectical grasp of their whole perspectives, or rather glimpses of the power that keeps them attracted and held together around a perspective: largely the power of promise. Unfulfilled and inarticulate recognition of promise is part of the attractive feature of a whole system of thought and practice. In it lies the key to persuasion, or more fundamentally the sharing of insight, the loving conferal of being.

The union of elements is not accidental in the Thomistic/Aristotelian sense, not a "mere heap" but a being, a theory, a unity of elements whose coherence testifies to the existence of a pattern, a form (recalling Plato's explorations in the dialogies, or Aristotle in the Metaphysics.)

A perspective worth considering. From General Smedley Butler, once the most decorated Marine in US history:

I spent 33 years and four months in active military service and during that period I spent most of my time as a high class muscle man for Big Business, for Wall Street and the bankers. In short, I was a racketeer, a gangster for capitalism. I helped make Mexico and especially Tampico safe for American oil interests in 1914. I helped make Haiti and Cuba a decent place for the National City Bank boys to collect revenues in. I helped in the raping of half a dozen Central American republics for the benefit of Wall Street. I helped purify Nicaragua for the International Banking House of Brown Brothers in 1902–1912. I brought light to the Dominican Republic for the American sugar interests in 1916. I helped make Honduras right for the American fruit companies in 1903. In China in 1927 I helped see to it that Standard Oil went on its way unmolested. Looking back on it, I might have given Al Capone a few hints. The best he could do was to operate his racket in three districts. I operated on three continents. (Wikipedia:Smedley_Butler)

column8: What exactly is love? Active favor toward the active favor of the beloved. (Favors many worlds hypothesis in creation?) Love requires a beloved and active favor in both lover and beloved. Active favor: blowing on the coals of being, an active inclination. The activity of the active favor implies action at some point in time: whenever such activity would be actively favored by the beloved. (Help when needed. Action when appreciated.) The out of active favor is life; in spaciotemporal situatedness, it is growth. So active favor toward active favor of another is giving life, promoting growth, advancing powers, empowering, assisting. In the abstract, love (active favor) hovers over the possibilities of life, of growth.

Awareness of the cost (wages) of sin is the mark of a culture of holiness (fluidity, gracefulness with respect to love, not just love given but love first and profoundly received, and given in kind.)

column4: //// more and more, the SC position seems at odds with MacIntyre: it is an expressivist postion: it denies that the standard of moral expressions are independent of human choices (of course from the immediacy of the choices of young persons, but not from the choices of those persons on adequate reflection, which requires adequate formation, which requires immersion in the right kind of social life, but all of this sources information about her choices, and the resulting choice is good for her precisely because, and not independently of, her choice under such conditions.. Were to to choose otherwise in such fully-informed conditions, the motivational and rational link between the description of the object of choice and her own choices would be broken: she would HAVE no reason to choose it if that description did not move her to so choose; there would BE no reason to choose it; there would be no further evidence on which to appeal. Promotes human flourishing? Not enough said (unless it happens to actually be, and she is moved.) In harmony with God's plan for the flourishing of human kind, all creation, ... and this doesn't move her? Then why should it? The link is cut. If in fact she is moved in another direction under such fully informed circumstances, why shouldn't she, and indeed, shouldn't she not pass up that rival choice; wouldn't she be rationally committed to that rival choice, and thus to the rejection of the putative good that meets every other criteria of human flourishing, divine apprroval, ... perhaps love drives her in a different direction? Then wouldn't reason, too: this is in fact her informed inclination. She'd be untrue to herself, acting contrary to her own reason, not to pursue it, if it survives after she has fully, maturely, explored, deliberated, consulted, weighed, and yet so inclines. This is an expressivist argument. What resources does M's Thomist have against it? (But why suppose M's Thomist is the Thomist, here?) Perhaps HF considerations in the end always do pursuade anyone who grasps them (as a 1st or 2nd-first language speaker of the emerging victorious tradition...). But if so, they are the standard precisely because they pursuade, not indepenently of whether they pursuade, and thus not independently of her (and each agent's) choices.

MacIntyre, end of 3RV: It is at this point that the Thomist has to resist the temptation to premature self-congratulation . . . . For it is not so much that Thomism has emerged unscathed from two serious philosophical encounters [with Enlightenment rationalism and Nietzschean emotivism] as that no serious philosophical encounter has as yet taken place. The Thomist conception . . . is untouched by contemporary radical critiques in key part because the cultural, linguistic, and philosophical distance between it and them is now so great that they are no longer able seriously to envisage the possibility of . . . [a] serious encounter.

column11: Objection: science uncovers fact. (Not history? Not through narrative?) Response: that's because we are alike in choosing to relate to the world precisely, with some measure of control; we are drawn (eventually) to clusters of solved problems with prediction of that set growing. Crafts emerge that are powerful in this way, and we choose to incoorporate the communities in which those crafts reside. We choose those communities, and those communities develop and retain a precise narrative of prediction and control of certain aspects of our interactions with the world. In epistemological crises, you see the plurality of narraitve; in the history of change (retrospectively, in a new and settled community, "progress") we see the communal and volitional, the social shaping, of the narratives built around what we accept as fact (as for memory, and experiment, and philosophy/theology alike.)

Rohr, on reading scripture - What Do We Do with the Bible?
column5: 1/6/2019 Epiphany For all its inspiration, for all the lives it has changed, the Bible is undeniably problematic. Put in the hands of egocentric, unloving, or power-hungry people or those who have never learned how to read spiritually inspired literature, it is almost always a disaster. History has demonstrated this, century after century, so this is not an unwarranted, disrespectful, or biased conclusion. The burning of heretics, the Crusades, slavery, apartheid, homophobia, and the genocide and oppression of native peoples were all justified through the selective use of Scripture quotes. So, what are we supposed to do with the Bible? Today’s meditation will be a bit longer than usual to begin addressing this question. And we’ll spend the rest of the week unpacking what Jesus did with the Hebrew Scriptures—the only Bible he knew. My general approach is to change the seer and not to change the text. Only transformed people can be entrusted with inspired writings. They can operate in a symbiotic(“shared life”) relationship with words and are unlikely to use the Bible to exclude and shame others or as a rationale for their bad behavior.

column4: Rohr, on the interplay between the beginner and the advanced practitioner: what it is to conform in part before understanding as a whole (re M's 3RV discussion): Beginner’s Mind 1/11/2019
column5: I’d like to offer some spiritual advice so that you can read Scripture the way that Jesus did and use it for good purposes. Offer a prayer for guidance from the Holy Spirit before you make your interpretation of an important text. With an open heart and mind, seek the attitude of a beginner and learner. Pray as long as it takes to feel any certitudes loosen. Once you have attained some degree of openness, try to move to a position of detachment from your own egoic will and its goals and desires—to be correct, to be secure, to stay with the familiar. This might take some time, but without such freedom from your own need for control, you will invariably make a text say what you need and want it to say. Then you must listen for a deeper voice than your own, which you will know because it will never shame or frighten you, but rather strengthen you, even when it is challenging you. If it is God’s voice, it will take away your illusions and your violence so completely and so naturally that you can barely identify with such previous feelings! I call this God’s replacement therapy. God does not ask and expect you to do anything new until God has first made it desirable and possible for you to do it. Grace cannot easily operate under coercion, duress, shame, or guilt. If your understanding of Scripture leads you to experience any or several of the fruits of the Spirit—love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, trustfulness, gentleness, and self-control (Galatians 5:22-23)—I think you can trust that this interpretation is from the Spirit, from the deeper stream of wisdom. As you read, if you sense any negative or punitive emotions like morose delight, feelings of superiority, self-satisfaction, arrogant dualistic certitude, desire for revenge, need for victory, or a spirit of dismissal or exclusion, you must trust that this is not Jesus’ hermeneutic at work, but your own ego still steering the ship. Remember the temptation of Jesus in the desert (Matthew 4:3-10). Three temptations to the misuse of power are listed: economic, religious, and political. Even Jesus must face these subtle disguises before he begins his public ministry. Only when he has found freedom from his own egoic need for power can Jesus teach with true inner authority and speak truth to the oppressive powers of his time.

column4: from https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/cosmology/ (Smeenk and Ellis) Ellis (2017) makes a distinction between Cosmology, which is the physically based subject dealt with in the textbooks listed in this article, dealing with the expansion of the universe, galaxies, number counts, background radiation, and so on, and Cosmologia, where one takes all that as given but adds in consideration about the meaning this all has for life.
column5: If one is going to consider Cosmologia seriously, it is incumbent on one to take seriously the full range of data appropriate to that enterprise. That is, the data needed for the attempted scope of such a theory must include data to do with the meaning of life as well as data derived from telescopes, laboratory experiments, and particle colliders. It must thus include data about good and evil, life and death, fear and hope, love and pain, writings from the great philosophers and writers and artists who have lived in human history and pondered the meaning of life on the basis of their life experiences. This is all of great meaning to those who live on Earth (and hence in the Universe).

column4: AP's call for Human AI is about "building human systems where facts matter",  where relevant data is made available and processed with the best currently-accepted ML techniques, applying the same power of AI to human systems that is routinely deployed in other areas of science. In that sense, "Human AI is an aspirational analogy". https://www.itu.int/en/journal/002/Documents/ITU2018-15.pdf
column5: But AP's call goes farther than an aspirational call. As is already being done in a variety of cases from corporate and global developmental contexts, insights from preliminary investigations where contemporary data, analysis tools and ML techniques are in fact being used to suggest new patterns of human organization. Further, these suggested patterns are being implemented, and predicted results measured and confirmed (or disconfirmed.)  In Pentland's view, these interventions--really, these social experiments--confirm an important underlying and surprising set of insights into human behavioral systems, namely, that they depend on social connections far more heavily than either one might antecedently expect, or would be explained by the influence of rational choices of individuals. We evolve socially, even in very short order, in surprising ways, and we need to assemble and respond to data revealilng how these patterns really work if we are to understand and manipulate human social systems toward beneficial outcomes.
column8: But there's the rub: what are these beneficial outcomes, and according to whom? According to Pentland, much of this is common sense, a "don't spit into the wind" calculation of experienced rational individuals. To his credit, he sees deeper than this appealing but misleadingly simple level. Recognizing that more complex social questions require more sophisticated analysis, he acknowledges the need to appeal to Rawls, Sen, or Nussbaum (but not to MacIntyre.) Somehow such resources of the classical liberal tradition will have to guide us on the tougher questions, even while there are plenty of clear cases around which we can get to work. But it is essential that we are "fully informed", which a human AI system would paradigmatically be.

column7: This is obviously a parable of resource sharing--economy, energy in this case. Some of us have grabbed a large share by force, used up some of the resources (air capacity for carbon at sustainable levels), and set the rules for the rest of the world to merely subsist.
column8: The way back to a good outcome...a just outcome...is for the have-lots kids to give back to the have-little crowd (with apologies.) And to clean up the air, at their (have-lots) expense. This situation calls for responsible wealth transfer. Here's what responsible wealth tranfer looks like in the energy space in Northern Uganda.
column9: 100K gets dropped into a 30kW energy system, together with a social/financial sustainability model. 100K initially buys a system, that is operated and sustained by a partnership between the local site and the local utility. At current energy expenditure rates, the site pays off a mortgage on the system, on a use-it-or-lose-it basis (as with a car or a home.) Over a period of 8 years or so, these mortgage payments provide sustainability for the system, and both repay the initial 100K (to the local utility, who can reinvest it in another site) and in effect buys a second (replacement) system for itself, which it owns outright. So the initial set of social, financial and energy arrangements. The money for this whole exchange comes half from donor wealth transfer, and half from the sun, leaving behind a community that provides its own clean energy with sustainable habits. (A closing contract with the local site can agree that the “second system” (never entirely new, just replaced with new parts as they age out) is paid out at a new market rate through to a full sustainability amount (in effect, for the “third system”) with a minimal service fee to the local utility. That local utility can nonetheless recapture and replant the system with expenses saved by the site in its jointly-managed sustainability account, if the site defaults on payments (which are now almost entirely payments to itself, to sustain its own wholly-owned system.) So the local site owns the system, under a covenant (as is sometimes done with housing): they keep it, as long as they sustain it, and cooperate with the local utility to ensure that they sustain it, all as part of the “responsibility to sustain” component of the initial donor wealth transfer. The money for this whole exchange comes half from donor wealth transfer, and half from the sun, leaving behind a community that provides its own clean energy with sustainable habits. (A closing contract with the local site can agree that the “second system” (never entirely new, just replaced with new parts as they age out) is paid out at a new market rate through to a full sustainability amount (in effect, for the “third system”) with a minimal service fee to the local utility. That local utility can nonetheless recapture and replant the system with expenses saved by the site in its jointly-managed sustainability account, if the site defaults on payments (which are now almost entirely payments to itself, to sustain its own wholly-owned system.) So the local site owns the system, under a covenant (as is sometimes done with housing): they keep it, as long as they sustain it, and cooperate with the local utility to ensure that they sustain it, all as part of the “responsibility to sustain” component of the initial donor wealth transfer. In this way, both the donor, and eventually only the sun, sustain both the energy system at the site, and the local utility who implements the responsible transfer of wealth into a sustainable energy system. BOSCO has told this story twice: once, with continued 3 years of success, at JPII; once, with ongoing successful response to initial failures (once, during installation that resulted in a move to a second site in LIra; and once, after installation that resulted in movement to a third site.

column6: information connects with our choices across a broad spectrum of mechanisms. At the molecular level, interaction with information over millions of years has shaped the structure and function of our DNA through natural selection, and at much higher frequencies with CRISPR and epigenetics (perhaps even heritable epigentics); at a cellular level we respond to information through our immune system. Over the past 150 years or so, all these molecular and biological mechanisms of information capture have been modeled and intentionally applied by human beings to achieve more efficient and stable outcomes. At a social level, information connects to our choices through direct and indirect, linguistic and nonlinguistic interaction with peers, and through direct and indirect interaction with culture. At the social level, just as with molecular and cellular biology, these patterns of information interaction, which Richard Dawkings has called memes, also admit of natural selection and intelligent design, as Daniel Dennett has described in an illuminating account.

Exploration through weak ties can bring in both useful and subversive ideas; which are which is not a neutral question. Information might well subvert a shared narrative...but information, according to whom? Information can be rejected as false since it threatens a narrative, or twisted into conformity with a narrative. So whose interpretation, which filter, should be applied to such information? Ideally, according to those who shared the narrative--fully grasped it, in full contact with information processed to form that narrative, that model--but who also grasp the subversive information and see a better (subverted) way forward to retain maximal contact with past and (potentially) future information. (The unit of progress through such contact is the solved problem, as Kuhn taught us.)

column6: Information is a set of states or probabilities of states, and a set of probabilities about how these states might (possibly) change through kinds of operations on them. (This is a set of actuals, and possibles.)
column7:
information is conserved...it's just everthing that is, and how it might change...any changes that occur don't change the number of possibilities, only which ones are actual. So the total number of actuals and possibles remains constant; that is, information is conserved. ("Quantum determinism means that given a present wave function, its future changes are uniquely determined by the evolution operator.
Reversibility refers to the fact that the evolution operator has an inverse, meaning that the past wave functions are similarly unique. The combination of the two means that information must always be preserved."

column6: Here's an answer: IF we could define a computable (an operationalized, measurable) account of what is true, and what is good, and what is right, THEN it would be quite interesting that the universe as a whole is a computation. For we might be able to compute an answer, a solution, to questions about what is true, good, and right. (One could add beauty, perhaps later.)
column7: But on MacIntyre's account, truth is computable: the truth is a stable relationship between the mind of a person formed to maturity (with full information contact, enough so that no additional contact impacts h/h judgements) in an information-rich and agonistically stable community, with the world, expressed as judgements about that world . But there is an algorithm to run which can generate such communities and such judgements. So Truth is socially computable.
column8: And so too for Goodness (stable relationships of inclination/preference formed in mature members of such communities) and right (stable outcomes of justificatory exchanges). (Roughly, the set of all questions in the domain of Truth, Goodness, Right, is hereafter TGR.)

column4: Intro to "Toward what end?" conference submission: Why the revival of the craft-tradition falls short of a craft, and what to do about it
column5: Alasdair MacIntyre’s rationality of craft-tradition has drawn a great deal of attention since its articulation throughout the 1980s and again most recently in 2016. His work opens up intriguing possibilities for better ways to live together: accumulating a store of theoretical and practical wisdom through a network of crafts; nurturing those crafts in communities coordinated by a master-craft, a new science of politics, operating under a unifying narrative; enriching and preserving the emerging store of practical wisdom across generations through tradition: refining and proving the attractiveness and stability of its store of wisdom through informed disputation with mature members of rival traditions. Especially against the current backdrop of increasingly antagonistic efforts to organize information about our world that is emerging at an ever-accelerating rate, MacIntyre’s craft-tradition prescription for building and profitably engaging a diverse assembly of rich traditions—his algorithm for rational inquiry—seems needed now more than ever. Yet in spite of both the promise of MacIntyre’s work and the severity of our need, it seems somehow that this algorithm has been neither coded nor compiled, and thus no program run. His work has been cited over 25,000 times across half a century. Yet successful efforts to build rich communities, or promote dialogue among them, or restructure institutions to encourage both under the guidance of a new science of politics, all seem lacking. What few calls for community have been issued seem to focus on retreat and isolation, rather than on advancement and engagement. The development of a robust science of communities, practices, narratives, and institutions for which MacIntyre has argued represents a paradigm shift in a Kuhnian sense, requiring identification of a full-bodied set of solvable problems, built around some clear cases of success that adoption of the new paradigm makes possible. In this essay I will propose three very different kinds of reasons why MacIntyre’s account has thus far not inspired such a shift in paradigm. These amount to 1) loading up too rich an account to be digested all at once; 2) pitching that account primarily at universities, and thus to the wrong initial audience; and 3) leaving some powerful features of the account unarticulated. Each of these shortcomings can be remedied. When they are, problems will be identified seem to admit of solution only after the implementation of each remedy, together with corresponding paradigmatic examples of successful efforts to solve them. This set of remedies and their preliminary successful applications are offered as preliminary indication of how an emerging craft of craft-tradition transformation should operate.

column4: MacIntyre What the Natural Sciences Do Not Explain. column5: First, that there are no lawlike generaliations between the intentions of a scientist and the experimental outcomes. On the surface, this seems false; we rely on them all the time. On the edge of discovery, that seems more plausible. On the path of verification and workup to next steps, that seems false. In particular, it seems false for the relationship between the intention of an experimenter and the displayed behavior of a rat whose protein for nurture has been either knocked out or turned off via epigenetics...this is a controlled outcome, seemingly as lawlike as anything else we explain.

column15: M is open to turing maching that thinks, but asserts that if it does, its thoughts will not be susciptible to explanation on natural scientific terms. column17: Increasing likelihood of conceptual innovation (raising children in scientifically wonderous conditions, etc) can be done, but "takes us no nearer" explaining why particular innovations occur. // TL: but this is just the same as why particular collisions produce particular products. We can increase chances of collisions and thus of productions of a given sort, but never pin down an explantion for a particular outcome. Why is thought-in--discovery any different?
column19: Because there is a history of inquiry behind Hamilton's discovery, and no such analogue for collision products. But is does this difference also sit on a spectrum? Doesn't different reaction to collison for our 32 neuron friend have an explanation in terms of perception of endomic vs exomic collision trigger (a start of consciousness)? A monkey hand colliding with rotted berries vs fresh, and a resulting open-hand withdrawal vs. a gathering fist, when reaching around a cliff top to an unseen bush? Isn't the explanation a more complex history of experience with nuts, but indeterminate with respect to how many bunches are sampled before the monkey moves on? A spectrum. Does the monkey need to know the explanation? No...but we can see it, in the monkey and ourselves. We are further along the spectrum of complex input. No qualitative jump seems called for here...
column22: In the end, all these modes of explanation, each in their particular domain, those domains changing over time as crafts advance: all these, taken together, contribute to the judgments of mature individuals. Among these are persons formed to maturing in one rich tradition, and to 2nd-1st-language competency in a rival. The judgements of these persons have complex elements, but in the end are wholistic, embracing one tradition over another, or unifying them in a particular way, or rejecting both in light of one another, etc. It is these global judgements which are the evidence of the rational superiority of one tradition over another, that is, of rational progress. These judgments are made in circumstances that can be specified and quantified, and the results of such judgements can be quantified. It is precisely the stability of such judgements that is the evidence of truth--as best we have so far--for each tradition's claims. And so stability of those very (fully informed) judgements is the very definition of truth. Otherwise, something might be true, but you shouldn't believe it precisely when maximally informed. And this is not what all people call truth.

the AI alignment problem breaks down into a few interrelated subproblems: "1) Have a satisfactory definition of human values.
2) Gather data about human values, in a manner compatible with the definition. 3) Find reliable ML algorithms that can learn and generalize from this data."
column7: "Properly aligning advanced AI systems with human values will require resolving many uncertainties related to the psychology of human rationality, emotion, and biases. These can only be resolved empirically through experimentation — if we want to train AI to do what humans want, we need to study humans." The intro of the Open AI paper linked at https://distill.pub/2019/safety-needs-social-scientists/, by Irving and Askell.
column8: "The goal of long-term artificial intelligence (AI) safety is to ensure that advanced AI systems are reliably aligned with human values — that they reliably do things that people want them to do. Since it is difficult to write down precise rules describing human values, one approach is to treat aligning with human values as another learning problem. We ask humans a large number of questions about what they want, train an ML model of their values, and optimize the AI system to do well according to the learned values."  This approach would just magnify the current state pursuit of what people currently want...the worst form of a desire-based account of wellbeing. What we need instead is a study of informed desire, the outcome of a HIFNRC computation. Why suppose that to study the properties of anything, you just explore it in just any arrangement? We explore properties of the Z boson by constructing an LEP with total energy of collision set to the mass of the Z, rather than looking around at Zs as they exist in nature. Their behavior under certain conditions (coming into being, on average, at rest at the LEP) is the way to optimize information capture from the system. So too in a HIFNRCs.

column6: Quantum information theory, related to Quantum reconstruction...one approach, built on just three postulates (of information capacity (bit as basic unit) localtity (no hidden variables), and reversibility...up to down and back again...) about what is and is not permitted with information (Decket et al Univ of Vienna) Classical Physics comes from 1 and 0 bit values; quantum rules, from the full clock dial)
column7: Answers or measurement outcomes are contingent on questions asked, like the cheating verison of 20 questions (John Wheeler, in teaching line between Bohr to Feinman. articulated this)...no fact to the matter of the world exists in this game; only the still-permitted possible outcomes based on questions already asked come out, and if many remain, then according to the probability of each remaining.
column8: TL: on this model, there is no "is"';  if you ask such and such a series of questions, then the remaining possibilities are these. This is a limitation on how we can interact with what we can know, not on what reality is independently of our capacity to know. Truth is stability of such interactions (put this better on this line...); it is a way of living in what we can know, i.e., what we can relate to consciously, which, so far as we could ever know, is all that there is. We are computing stable ways of being conscious, which (happily for us) are loving ways of being conscious, together. We experience plurality in our consciousness, and we experience our consciousness as dependent on what we experience as other than ourselves. Though sympathetic dialogue, we overcome that "otherness" and become one with the other, recognizing that oneness already present, or so that is the stable outcome of our conscious interaction as we calculate it (at a conscious level) personally, communally, socially. (The communal/social distinction, never sharp or univocal, seems to be related to "otherness" as overcome (in some measure), or as not yet so.

column4: // a story line...
column5: Once upon a time, people used to live in big, imperfect but much happier, tribes. They were big enough to develop promising answers to all the questions they had, and reasonable solutions to all the problems they had. These famlies had their ups and downs, but on the whole, they lived together in very rewarding relationships sharing very helpful common answers and solutions to all of life's challenges. And it was very good.
column7: One day, though, these families grew big enough and moved far enough to encounter other tribes. When they did, it took a long time to exchange their answers and solutions, especially because they understood the questions and problems differently.  Sometimes, these meetings went well, and both tribes learned a lot. When they did, their set of answers and solutions grew even greater, enabling them to travel farther and wider.

column9: from Urban, direct quote, Ch 6, on political leadeship;
column10: If you live in a democracy, and you’re not zoomed out far enough, you might look at the politicians running your government and mistake them for your leaders. In the short term, sure, they jostle with each other over the country’s policies and steer the country on the international stage. But with a step back, the real long-term leader of a democracy is the giant communal brain of the citizen body. Politicians are all about principles…as long as those principles fit on the top of the country’s Thought Pile, safely inside the Overton window. But when that Thought Pile moves, like it did on the topic of gay marriage between 2008 and 2012, politicians drop everything and start swimming.
column14: With the right idea and a lot of courage, a single person can spark a mind-changing movement that gains so much momentum, it moves our beliefs and our cultural norms, which in turn moves the Overton window, which moves policy, which moves law....Whenever a citizen of a democracy speaks up in a classroom or town hall, writes an op-ed, makes a movie, tweets a tweet, or yells something out on the street, they’re sending an idea out into the great network—firing a little neural impulse into the workings of the larger system. Every citizen, whether they realize it or not, is a neuron in the mind of a giant organism (emph mine), and what they do and say in their lifetime contributes to who that organism is, even if only a little bit.

column5: Starting with a beef with Donald Hoffman. (Here, based on his public lectures/interviews; reaffirm all that follows after reading his 2019 Case Against Reality, CAR.) On his view, evolution provably produces illusory perception, not veridical. (Back of mind: does his argument undercut itself, like any strong skeptical argument seems easily accused of doing?) Evolution favors fitness, which only under very specialized and not-so-often circumstances maps onto...what? Accuracy? Truth? He uses these loosely, at least in public discussion.
column6: What is "veridical", in his studies (evolutionary game theory, graph theory, and genetic algorith approaches.) Maximally aware of/in contact with information about the world? Got it: no xray vision: we operate under (what: the illusion of?) a narrow band of electromagnetic wavelengths. So we think two things are the same, when they're not (eg flower with and without UV stripes, which bees can distinguish but we cannot.) This is illusory? Seems odd to say so. So get the details from his CAR
column7: So that's one easy complaint (or required clarification, at least) from Hoffman. But my main suspicion is this: if it is possible that a rhetorically attractive minimum--analogous to a maximally gravitationally attractive mass moving through a finite physical system--could emerge, attractive from every perspective on full information exchanges, then the way the mature members of that emerging tradition see the world seems to meet both fitness and stability of interaction standards; that is, fitness and truth combine, and not accidentally, but essentially. Fitness in that specialized information-responsiveness arena is truthfulness; fitness is another way to express stability. (Not dead yet, and pretty damn unlikely to be anytime soon: a bamf expression of fitness, of stability of existence, and it can apply to ideas/memes, too.) Truthfulness (information used in stable patterns) is expressed in technology (exterior world as useful), disposition (interior world ...?), culture (exterior world as ends?), and narrative (spoken and written organizational pattern encompassing all else, like an OS for which software is written?)
column9: So perception is veridical isfa it is fit under such full-information probing. Veridical is not the same as universal; of course there are other ways to look at it (a bee's way), but not our way, except also indirectly (through representational coloring of multiwavelength astroimages, for example.) But our image of the moon in visible light is not false (ust as we've dropped "false coloring" for "representational coloring" in astroimage rendering), not substandard, except insofar as it would limit our proper (information-processing, telos-computational) functioning to see it that way. (Is our new/crescent moon image somehow substandard to a full moon image, b/c it contains less information? No. Nor is our image of the full moon, because it isn't transparent, mostly empty space, obviously3d, etc.) So from this stable-information-processing model of truth, fitness does not diverge from truthfulness in perception. Of course it does in instances: ignorance is bliss, we say, but not permanent ignorance. This is why failing to notice the faults of others is a fit-making characteristic, not short-term always but long-term, when properly developed in a fully mature human being.
column11: That gets at the nagging suspicion re/ skepticism of perception, from SIP truth. It applies further, though, to skepticism of reality. What is realistic perception, other than SIP (stable, under stable information processing) perception? There may be many ways to achieve this, and all of them may be equally insights into reality--information-processing instances--except insofar as they lose stability under full information exchange conditions (if there are any; otherwise, perceptival relativism is still on the table.) For a perception to lose stability under a FI exchange condition is for mature members to no longer trust it; it remains a veridical candidate (so far) isfa they do continue to trust it under such conditions. Something along these lines is how we might proceed to verify perceptions, and establish that they are in some way real, in some way revealing what is real, constituting information about what is real.
column13: But there seems to be another nagging argument in the neighborhood, not about the process of verifying perception, but of the meaning of reality. Suppose that SIP truth is obtainable, replete with (improbably) all the simplifying distinctions: objective (surviving full information inspection from all rival perspectives) and universal (and by all human beings or all consious, human-communicative agents.) Some big tradition would be on the move, a supermassive black hole of justification, pulling in everyone under full information processing conditions: suppose SIP were achieved robustly (under this simplifying hypothetical set of conditions.) Now take any rival set of claims from any other tradition, with any measure of justification from the standpoint of that tradition. (I have in mind, Thomistic natural law claims from those who challenge their tradition-constitutedness; they are universal, T-independent claims, some say...) If it is possible that some supermassive T overcomes this rival T in full-information exchange, then it is false that these natural law claims are tradition-independent; no mature members of the tradition who asserts that they are tradition-independent hold on to such assertions, after FI encounter with our supermassive tradition, on this supposition. So if this supposition is even possibly true, it follows that all claims to T-independentness of any kind are subject, for their justification, to the outcome of interactions between traditions. But this is just what the proponents of T-independent-claims deny is the case. So such proponents must be committed to the impossibility of an emergence of a supermassive tradition in FI encounters. That seems an implausible commitment to have to make. They could justify it by argument from T-independent premises, but would have to admit that they might all have to drop such premises under possible historical future conditions, which is just what the T-dependent advocate asserts and the T-independent advocates deny.   This gets close, as a first attempt, to articulating my nagging suspicion. T-independent claims couldn't possibly be true, unless they necessarily were true, and thus the existence of a supermassive SIP tradition were impossible. But no one knows such a T is impossible; we can specify conditions for testing the claim that any such tradtion is such a supermassive SIP T, and it the claim that a tradition is such a tradition might amass as much empirical evidence, possibly, as we have for any other kind of claim. (That may be too quick...) So it isn't obviously false that some such T could exist; and so no one should believe that it is necessarily impossible now, as adherents of T-independent truth claims are committed to doing. So no one should hold such claims, at least not at this state of the experiment.
column18: That's getting close, as an articulation of this nagging suspicion. Find a way to put this more clearly. column4: // so let's take these information processing thoughts to a considation of facts, or rather the meaning of "facts". These are thought to be objective, perhaps the outcomes of observation, through some clever philosophers of science talk about theory-laden facts, as if our theories are built into our observations. So, for example, particles like the Higgs Boson are observed, indirecly, via statistical analysis of a great many collisions at the LHC, recorded in one of its general purpose detectors, CMS or ATLAS; that statistical analysis builds on recontruction of many individual events, each of which is reconstructed from some behavior in the detector, and some basic information about the energy of the colliding particles. The reconstruction of the daughter particles emerging from the collision of parents is tough, because in advance you can't know what the nature of the parent particles are: protons are not fundamental, and the number and nature of the constituent particles--quarks and gluons--from each proton are numerous, and can only be identified through reconstructions, solutions that are consistent with the laws of nature and all of the more basic observations in the detector (such as the number of electrons flowing out of a photomultiplier tube (that is, the current) of a known voltage and a feed of photons from a scintillator of known properties, between layers n and n+1 of brass of known thickness and properties. In the end, we know what daughter particles, and in what directions they traveled with what speed, emerged from each initial collision. To find the Higgs, we need to interpret that data for each collision as whole, and find patterns that emerge in the production of many such events that are consistent with the known laws of nature probably (at a 99.99999% confidence level) only if the Higgs Boson exists under such laws. But to do even a single reconstruction of a single collision, we need to know what all the emergent (daughter) particles are; we need to detect them, and that's what the detector (CMS or ATLAS) is for. But it turns out that there is a type of particle--neutrinos--which we can detect, but only rarely and with much more detector than we could afford to build around the LHC. But we can apply some pretty basic physical theory (convervation of momentum) to the problem, infer the existence and kind of neutrinos which must have been produced, and use these to complete the picture of all the collision products, reason back to the nature of the colliding parent particles, and use the many emerging complete accounts of individual collisions to find patterns (perhaps predicted by theory, perhaps not) that emerge through many such events. In such a way, human kind discovered the Higgs Boson. (A tiny bit deeper dive into the way conservation of momentum is used to find neutrinos is good for the soul. So imagine this thought experiment. Two couples are ice skating. Rather than standard skates, they wear roller skates on the ice, but with only a single ball bearing in the center front and center rear of each skate, each ball enclosed in a frictionless cup attached to the bottom of the skates. These ball bearings are what contact the ice, so the skates can get no traction in any direction from them. (To get the skaters started, we might build a figure-skater's toe pick into the skate in front of the front-most of the two ball bearings.) If we put set our skaters on the ice and pushed, they could not turn in any way. If we separated any two of them by some distance, we could push them at one another, and they would collide if our aim was good. Now let's give each pair of skaters one more piece of equipment: a smooth steel rod, about the thinkness of rebar (say, 1/2 inch); each rod is some three feet in length, enabling one skater to place hands roughly shoulder-length apart, and the second skater her own pair of hands comfortably inside the first skater's hands. Suppose we put two pairs of skaters, thus equipped, on the ice, one at each side of (say) a hockey rink, game-ready, zambonied. Grasping the rod held vertically between them, and using their toe picks just initiallty for traction, they could induce a spin around the rod, much as pairs skaters sometimes do, with no rod. But with the rod, we might use our own rod, capped as a short "T" at one end, with the crossbar turned up into a half-circle. With such a tool, we could reach quickly between the spinning skaters (digging in our own toe picks, or from side of the ice), connect with the center of their verical rod, and push the pair into a linear trajectory, even while they spin around the center of that linear motion, across the ice. Now if we were clever enough, some large enough team of us could do the same thing on the other end of the rink, pushing a 2nd spinning couple across the ice toward the first. Now, on this simple model, you can see that we might view each couple as a particle, consisting of some number of parts (perhaps three: two skaters and one rod holding them together), but moving as a whole in a single direction. If we succeed in our (maybe cruel) attempt at directing the spinning couples directly toward one another from across the rink, we'll have a collision: otherwise, not. The one thing we know about any colliding pair is that they were moving directly along the path of the collision, or it wouldn't have happened. Now imaging pushing bundles of spinning particles down a beam line, toward the center of a detector's collision hall...the one thing we know, if there is a collision, is that the sum of the weighted movements (technically, the momentum) of all the parts is all pointed right down the teamline. There is no curving away from that line--technically, momentum in a direction transverse to the beam line--if there is to be a collision:  transverse momentum of any collision is zero. Armed with that knowlege, we can reconstruct all the detected elements ...

column4: // spend some more time on the 'universal principles' vs 'empircal stability' foundations for natural law
column5: somewhere in here is an argument that the very state of affairs which UP folks think is likely (slow emergence of rational consensus, starting perhaps around science) is one whose very possibility undercuts the existence of universal principles. Such an emerging consensus (already showing in natural sciences) suggests that a stable tradition is not only possible but expected. And if possible, either it diverges from UPs, or not. If it does, it opens the question of how we could ever know that such UPs were true, and indeed even meaningful. And if it doesn't diverge, it is unclear why tradition-free UPs are necessary or even useful. Instead, UPs seem justified in light of their belonging to a maximally stable set, and not otherwise, exactly what advocates of T-bound principles assert. So those advocates of T-bound rationality and T-bound principles, including a corresponding account of truth as enduring stable information processing, seem to be correct if what their opponents deem likely is even possible.

column4: // all I have for you is an invitation. Even whether to receive it as an invitation is up to you, and your choice, as well as mine, must cohere if there is to be a fact to the matter about whether or not it is an invitation. But not your choice--I so choose--under any cricumstances (in anger, under hallucinogens, under conditions of evident sustained inability to persuade me that you have any idea of what I am attempting), but rather your choice when you are responding to information--stimulus, interpreted by you--about what I am attempting when I issue what I take to be this invitation. In the end, what my actions mean--even whether they are actions or have meaning--emerges as a consensus between us. We interact in a stable way--we bond over this--or we don't. If there is an enduring answer to the question as to whether some set of conditions might reliably, stably produce such bonding interaction--such agreement--is an experiment that must be conducted under fairly elaborate set of conditions, like any other: GIGO; precision in, precision out. We are free to reject such precision--to reject science--if we choose, and it has no status independent of whether we would reject it under some set of richly-informed circumstances. But it may have such status; we may find that doing science, engaging the world as measurable and predictable--is an attractive way to engage it, and that there are conditions which we prefer to interpret as productive of such preference and of the stability that emerges from activity under that scientific preference. Predicting so is my preference.

column6: imagine a floating 2-d grid of numbers (1/0, or 1-9, or...), infinitely extended. If infinite, then every combination of numbers and neighbors, of any arbitrary extent, is present in this grid. Now imagine a line, and folding of the sheet across the diagonal of any finite sized subset, rippling out infinitely, a new mathematical object, a relation, where each number is related in some way (is identical with, or not, if 1/0; is =, or is <> by so much, if 1-9, etc.). One can now consider that relation, and each pair of numbers related, and in multiple orders: 1R2, 2R1, 12R, 21R, R12, R21--6 different logical orderings between this same set of numbers and relations. The relations themselves populate a different matrix (eg, if all numbers on the top of the fold were double those underneath, the relations would all be 2x of x to y, 1/2x in y to x; these two directional matrixes would have 2s in all cells, and 1/2 in all cells, respectively. Now imagine considering these in some particular order, one xy or yx relation logically before another...perhaps this logical ordering is analogous to the flow of time. (In our 30W brains, we would consider these consecutively in time, or, when we parallel process, at the same time but in different spaces. (Parallel processing is just serial processing spread out in space, rather than in time.)
column9: Life (created life) is limited awareness, perspective, perception, consciousness. We break up kinds of life in ways that correspond to the kinds of limitations on each perspective. (We focus on kinds according to fitness-for-us.) What kinds of things are alive? Anything that percieves the world is alive; life is perception. Life is no longer created life when its perception is no longer limited. Death is the release of limitations of perspective. (In that sense, all of life is preparation for death.)

Very small children are persons but not moral agents.//Moral agents perceive consequences of their actions and impact on the knowledge and enjoyment (the lives) of others.
column11: For a limited perspective to participate in conscious life is to come to know and enjoy; the need to move toward these ends is a consequence of limitation of perspective; there is something outside of that perspective that must be efficiently bundled within the confines of the limitation (in our case, in accord with the laws of nature, implications of culture, etc.) That process of coming to know is an exploration, a calculation: mistakes can be made, and we learn through them. Insofar as we fail, we do not intend the failure. From our limited perspective, we do intend what is in fact failure, but is not recognized as such (not all-things-considered.)
column12: Divine (unlimited) consciousness is aware of everything and of its interconnectedness, and enjoys it all. All of this is immediate and effortless; effort is required when limitations on consciousness must be overcome (as limited consciousness must do, and accomplishes through language/model/narrative/schema creation. This pathway toward divine life is what created consciousness is calculating: these are relationships that are discovered and incorporated into the limited perspective to overcome in some measure its limitations. So living together in maximal awareness and enjoyment of one another (as friends) is the end of created life.

column5: key point for me is Dennett's view that consciousness is like a user illusion, very much akin (says me) to DH''s desktop image: "1:32:34 SC: So just to be super clear, to get the lingo right, you’re not claiming that consciousness is an illusion. It’s real in the same sense that the patterns that we talked about are real. These concepts of experiences play a useful role in how we explain what we go through. 1:32:52 DD: Well, I’m glad you asked that question. 1:32:56 SC: Or not so. Let’s get it right. 1:32:58 DD: Because I like the term illusion. 1:33:00 SC: Okay. 1:33:03 DD: And I think it’s a generational thing. I think that the younger generation has no trouble with illusion as a positive term, as in the user illusion. Consciousness is a user illusion, in fact, the manifest image is a user illusion. It’s nature’s way of simplifying the world for us. In the same way that software engineers have brilliantly created these metaphorical icons and sound effects, and, think of how badly you would misunderstand the computer if you tried to figure out how computers work by simply extrapolating from the user illusion. 1:33:53 SC: Yeah, taking literally the files on your desktop. 1:33:56 DD: That’s right. The user illusion is a brilliantly designed… The user illusion of a laptop or a smartphone is brilliantly designed to exploit your perceptual and locomotory and hand dexterity powers, and your audition to your hearing, to permit you to perform things you wanna do ignorant of the details of how it’s going on. Same thing is true in your brain, the one difference is that there’s no screen, because there’s no eyeball in there."

starting at 1;53;30, CS summarizes DD's worldview, and asks for implications for ethics: 1:51:04 SC: I like that, okay, very good, I will remember that. Not how the black mirror episode ended but still a good one. Good. I think to wrap up, let’s deviate a little bit from you’ve had a long career with many greatest hits, so I think we’ve hit some of them here but what are the… There’s a world view that you’re sketching out that is very coherent and fits together in various ways, laws of physics, Darwinian evolution, intentional stances, real patterns, what are the implications of that, as we’ve been, we’ve begun to touch on this but for morality, for ethics, for how we should live our lives, right? Is there a meta ethical conclusion that comes from this or even ethical conclusion?

1:51:46 DD: Well, yes, I think there is and part of it is yet another aspect to my work we haven’t mentioned is it means we don’t need religion. Religion was maybe a good scaffold on which to build civilization, maybe the myths of religion kept people in line and cooperating because they were worried about big brother watching them and maybe I’m quite content with a hypotheses, not provable, but they might be true and say that that civilization depended on religion. I don’t think it does anymore. I think we can grow up and simply abandon the myths. But when we do that, we wanna be sure that we don’t destroy or discard some of the valuable things that came along with that. The one that most concerns me is one that you can get at with the line of Robert Frost. He says a home is where when you have to go there they have to take you in. Now, in that sense, there’s a lot of people that are homeless and don’t trust the state to take care of them. One of the things that religions have done over the decades, over the millennia, is taken in and provided a sense of meaning and love for people who otherwise would not have that.

1:53:41 DD: And those of us who were fortunate enough to live exciting lives should recognize that this is a social service, to call it that is to under play its significance by orders of magnitude. This is a life healing, life protecting, life improving feature of the world that we don’t want to throw away. The question is how do you save it without also saving the sort of brute irrationality or arationality, the valorization of unreason and superstition? I think it’s possible to domesticate religions a little further. They’ve been domesticated a lot, but I think we can go a little farther and keep ceremony, keep community, keep music and art and celebration intact and leave out the myths. But that’s a tall order but I see progress all around and I do share the concern that a lot of people have that while the fastest growing group in the world is the nones, the N-O-N-E-S, those that have no religion at all, if they have no community, if they have no allegiance, if they have no, if there’s nothing that they think of that’s bigger than and more important than they are to guide their lives then we’re in trouble. It just shouldn’t be…(Religion)."
column16: on morality, at 1:56 DD: "So I think morality is itself a, is a human...a social construct and that…t’s not given by god, it isn’t deducible from a set of axioms, it’s a, in a certain sense, political and racial creation of ideally an informed community of people.
column17: DD: 1:01:47: Andrew Parker’s idea about the Cambrian explosion. And Parker hypothesizes that the shallow ocean became transparent in a way it hadn’t been before, and this suddenly permitted distal perception, permitted eyesight. And the book is called In the Blink of an Eye. And he argues that the arms race of predator and prey locomotion, camouflage, armor, this all was generated by a growing transparency. And it’s not the only theory out there, but it’s one that I think there’s gotta be an element of truth in it. And I’ve been arguing that what we’re facing right now is the second great transparency, and that’s the electronic transparency. And everybody’s now worried, and so they should be, about privacy. And we can now see farther and we can see into things we could never see into before, but we can also be watched and we can be seen.

Cs is (my language) something like a parallel feature of safety (could have said fitness) responsiveness; something like turning up the volume on music which is otherwise the same. column4: // information storage in grandmothers: Proc Nat Acad Sci https://www.pnas.org/content/pnas/116/52/26669.full.pdf
column5: Understanding why females of some mammalian species cease ovulation prior to the end of life is a long-standing interdisciplinary and evolutionary challenge. In humans and some species of toothed whales, females can live for decades after stopping reproduction. This unusual life history trait is thought to have evolved, in part, due to the inclusive fitness benefits that postreproductive females gain by helping kin. In humans, grandmothers gain inclusive fitness benefits by increasing their number of surviving grandoffspring, referred to as the grandmother effect. Among toothed whales, the grandmother effect has not been rigorously tested. Here, we test for the grandmother effect in killer whales, by quantifying grandoffspring survival with living or recently deceased reproductive and postreproductive grandmothers, and show that postreproductive grandmothers provide significant survival benefits to their grandoffspring above that provided by reproductive grandmothers. This provides evidence of the grandmother effect in a nonhuman menopausal species. By stopping reproduction, grandmothers avoid reproductive conflict with their daughters, and offer increased benefits to their grandoffspring. The benefits postreproductive grandmothers provide to their grandoffspring are shown to be most important in difficult times where the salmon abundance is low to moderate.

column6: Sarah thinks about information all the time. Her take is different than many physicists, because she puts it, it may be that biology is prior to physics, since the physics that we have turned out emerged from biological existence. Moreover, biology might even be prior to chemistry in the sense, because life might have emerged at the biological level, not as a chemical level, although chemistry isn’t easier and less complex, though still stochasticDomain. Among her interest and claims is that the fundamental unit of life might not be the cell, but might be the world.

The take I am exploring is that full human thought is dialectical, holistic. What you see when science jumps from one place to another place without a good explanation is in fact a thirst for a holistic explanation, which people are generally not accustomed to pursuing or slaking. So starting with a love-centered bias, one rooted in recognition that love is real, and that somehow outstrips what we can generate ourselves, but comes to us from the outside and from something greater, that instinct is a theological instinct, and it points to levels of consciousness that would outstrip our own. Starting from that bias, is a way of sweeping the possibility space, but not looking mindlessly, as if under the streetlight because it’s easier to find keys there, rather than the place they are most likelyTo be found. Of course, the love-centred bias can be like the street light, and like the place they are most likely to be found. We might adopt it because it’s comfortable, rather than because we think it’s true. Bad idea. But we might think it’s true, and we might sweep the space of possibilities for a whole ways of looking at the world starting by uniting those ideas we think are most plausible. What scientist wouldn’t do that… should we prefer a beginning that we think is implausible?
column24: so let’s come back to this possibility, then, in which consciousness is fundamental, is real, is capable of infinite manifestation and find a manifestation. Let’s add to that, drawing from our ordinary experience that we can adapt limited perspectives intentionally, so interestingly enough it seems like limiting our perspective is what requires the energy, although connecting our perspective more broadly that it currently is also seems to require energy. What does not seem to require energy is entertaining what one already knows. Or better still, entertaining all together what one currently has, whether or not it is knowledge. Starting from that ordinary perspective, it makes a lot of sense to explore the idea of conscious realism, and wonder, precisely as Dial Hoffman is wondering, whether the rest of our experience can be driven from the mathematics of this very ordinary experience of consciousness. In Hoffman’s work, it seems like there is a very strong possibility that this could happen. So let Hoffman, and let others following often, conduct a research program, sweep that space. meanwhile, we ought to look at that program and see how it fits together with other programs that we are committed to following. This is precisely where the resurrecting civility project comes from. Information understood as what you are aware of, opens up the possibility of understanding social reality is information processing, and of solving a whole range of problems that are currently plaguing us in our social life. included among these problems are the problems of too much information, the problem of filter and bias and not knowing how to think about that, and confusion about objectivity versus subjectivity versus bias versus unbiased. Included in that are the problem of echo chambers that we so easily form. The problem of fake news, as if there’s non-fake news. Or as if there’s any common agreement about what that is. column11:
"narrowcasting" v broadcasting: "First, there was the birth of cable television around 1980, and with it, the advent of cable news. CNN (which literally stands for Cable News Network) launched in 1980. Cable channels, burdened with fewer regulations and hazier expectations than mainstream networks, could be more experimental with the way they covered the news.

Then there was the end of the Fairness Doctrine. In 1949, the FCC (the U.S. Federal Communications Commission) enacted the Fairness Doctrine, which required anyone who held a broadcast license to present controversial issues of public importance in what they called an honest, equitable, and balanced manner. In 1987, in the face of arguments that the Fairness Doctrine was in direct conflict with the First Amendment’s freedom of the press clause, it was revoked.

Not coincidentally, the demise of the Fairness Doctrine was soon followed by a sharp rise in blatantly politically biased media. Conservative talk radio exploded onto the scene in the late 1980s, most notably with The Rush Limbaugh Show, which debuted in 1988 and made Limbaugh the country’s most syndicated radio host by 1991. In 1996, Fox News and MSNBC were born."

column4: January 31, 2020.The subtitle of Jeff Hawkins book, on intelligence, Makes reference to a new theory of brain intelligence. column5: On Hawkins theory, intelligence is model based prediction. But models, in the brain, are different than the generally more simple mathematical/computational models that we know in Science. Models in the brain or more like memories, which is to say, Stored patterns which are very complex, hierarchically ordered, auto associative (that is, the whole of the model or memory can be called by a simple reference to the part), and have remarkable Fidelity across change of media, the way intervals In music can be preserved across changes of key. (Hopkins has a particular name for this feature of Cortical memory, which I should look up And use.) processing in the brain is something like throwing forward a very full model of the world As a predicted pattern, one characterized by all those preceding features, toward a modest amount of sensory input, which in turn generates a revised predicted pattern, The important thing to note here is that this predicted pattern is not a calculation from the previous sensory input; it’s way too complicated it happens way too fast for that. It’s more like a shift, triggered by a small amount of century input, the way entire function might be transformed by changing a single coordinate and operational matrix.To take a simple example, an entire function can be reflected from an upper right quadrant to a lower left quadrant by multiplying through by -1, So that now the very same pattern is present, but in a different way, such as the way that Y equals 2X, when multiplied by -1, Y equals minus 2X. somehow, all those recalculations for every possible value of X do not have to be made; there is a mathematical model in which the whole pattern of the original is preserved with just a slight adjustment. On some such analogy, our brains predict efficiently through the transformation of patterns, The transformation effected efficiently through the assignment of some probability to future state of affairs, or rather, to future transformations of stable patterns in current affairs.

column9: remarkably, these predictive model calculations are not performed primarily by the brain as a whole, but rather, by thousands Of complete calculators working in parallel, not on separate parts of a problem being running parallel, but I am the whole prediction.
