column11: Let’s focus for a minute on just one of these calculators. The calculating unit is a roughly 1 mm² by typically less than 3 mm high stack n of neurons which are interconnected with one another in remarkably complex ways, but in ways which themselves admit of higher ordered patterns, patterns which are different in ways that amount to six recognizable layers throughout each of these columns. This six layered columnation is repeated throughout the entire cortex, which, to put it simply, is where intelligence resides.To borrow one of Hawkins many excellent analogies, consider a business card, somewhere between two and 3 mm thick, with the sharpest of points visible to the eye made by a pencil, something roughly the size of a period. imagine that business card extending out in each direction until the hole is something like the size of a dinner napkin, and those periods multiply into the tens of thousands. Imagine each of these periods as an independent calculator, with amazingly complex connections throughout the entire six leveled stack of intern 10,000 neurons, each of which connected thousand of times to other neurons in other stacks throughout the entire surface area of this dinner napkin, which is our cerebral cortex. The number of connections made in a single human brain in this way numbers into the trillions.
column13: A remarkable feature about our brains, already suggested by the simplicity of the analogy just given, is that, for the most part, all of the stacks are the same. Moreover, all of their connections to one another are roughly the same. And so, also roughly the same, are all of the connections which link these to our sense organs. There are intern millions of such connections between calculators and century input in the brain: 1 million for a visual system alone; another million for all of our physical senses, amir 30,000 for auditory system, and many others, from a set of sensors which we in common language number is five, but in fact or more like a dozen. Think, for example, of the way we both here and keep our balance with our years. These are two systems, which we failed to distinguish in our ordinary listing of fights. Without running through all the details, the sort of distinguish ability applies to other senses too, and so we have a roughly a dozen such systems of input, each using tens of thousands or hundreds of thousands of connections from the senses to each of our calculating cortical columns.
column15: just at this point, having introduced almost no distinctions in the physical arrangement between the sensory connections and the cortical columns, I have perhaps introduced too much symmetry. After all, our optical nerves bring senses from our eyes through the visual visual system which resides in the back of the brain, and proceeds upward through a hierarchy in different sections of the visual system. And so on for our auditory system, And taste, and sensory motor connections, etc.: each of these functions is located, famously, in different portions of the brain. Moreover, there are in fact two of almost all of these sets of functions, one in each of two hemispheres of the brain, With a relatively smaller number of connections from one hemisphere to the other, running together in the corpus callosum. a region small enough to be snipped by a surgeon, As sometimes is done to Prevent electrical storms from moving from one hemisphere to another In patients who experience quite severe seizures. and so the symmetry is broken in the brain, in quite a variety of ways.
column17: and yet this breaking of symmetry masks a deeper, underlying symmetry. For a turns out that for the most part, each of the calculated columns on a dinner napkin are the same. column20: This model of the brain as a whole neocortical network of independent calculators, Hawkins calls the thousand brain theory. from the account I’ve given it so far, he might well wonder why it’s not called the 10,000 or more brain theory, since there are many more than 1000 independent calculators. The answer to that question lies and yet another feature of brain operation, which is called sparseness. It turns out that we have enormous redundancy in the brain, and that very little of it is used for anything at any time. this relative dormancy peppered through with relatively distant activity turns out to be important for stability, for error correction, and for other features that fall out of the mathematics of sparseness. We need not go any furtherWith the concept of sparseness than that. So it turns out that at any one time, it’s roughly On the order of I thousand independent calculators, not tens or hundreds or thousands of thousands, that are Constructing and comparing independent models of the whole world.
column22: let’s consider this dinner napkin sized network of calculators, only sparsely operative at any one moment, and let’s wrap this whole network of calculators with all of the support functions and needs, like oxygen to catalyze the distribution of energy throughout the system, and a complex maintenance of a chemical equilibrium to support the many biological machines that do things like keep Things like viruses from Jumping in and disrupting the ordinary operation of the system. call this whole system of supported parallel holistic calculation, a brain. Will take a little deeper still, but we might pause and wonder already. We might notice, that it is in a very strange way rather like a community of calculators, of computers, of model creators, of perceivers. One might wander a bit further, and ask whether this whole network is a network of intelligent entities. And if they are intelligent, we might ask whether they are conscious. So let’s make it a little less crude, and then ask the same series of questions again, in an attempt to understand what the brain is, what intelligence is, and indeed what and who we are.
column24: It turns out that in the account given so far, we have not yet provided enough input for our network of independent calculators. We have indeed wired them with a great deal of sensory input, recognizing specialization of connection with the senses, but also The commonality of input across the sensors, the flexibility regarding specialization with respect to kind of input (Alfred called neuroplasticity), and also the extreme degree of output sharing both within each of the layers and between the layers within each cortical column, in between cortical columns, so that in the end, whether directly or indirectly, all or nearly all of our columnated calculators receive input Of one kind or another from all of our senses. And yet our senses or not the only source of input that a human being processes. if we were to follow along and build a picture of you and intelligence on the same model, we would need to systematically identify the source of input to the system, and then, at least on a very simple model of the thousand brain theory sketched so far, simply distribute, either directly or after processing, the results from each source of input.

column4: ... Suppose we thought, for a moment, that every higher-level thought that ever enters our head is constructed from sensory experience. To use the model as described just above, imagine All of what we might consider our higher ideas are Outputs of complex higher level processes, and that these ideas are shared across the brain quite widely and complex ways, within layers of each cortical column, between layers within each, and between cortical columns, distributed quite widely throughout the brain, so that all the calculators in her brain all have got, for example, a sense of personal identity, a sense of me, or you. if that were so, it wouldn’t seem so far-fetched to imagine our brains as communities not only of calculators, but intelligent calculators, and perhaps even conscious calculators. column6: Back now to intelligence. I’ve already suggested that the brain, and indeed intelligence, are both community phenomenon We can understand them as a network of multiple model based prediction engines that take into account everything, not only the input from the senses, but also everything we call ideas, To assemble all models of the world and to predict constantly what comes next, not only at the level of what we will see here taste touch smell etc. but also of what your next be said, we are next to go, How next to feel, What it will next be like to experience what is about to transpire. Purposes here, hope is here, fear, love, meaning. Were there were a network of Computers which predicted at this level, we could no longer tell them, perhaps,From conscious agents, or from persons. column4: The notion that all our ideas are processed in the brain using the very same circuitry as used to process what we perceive through our senses is in someways a sensible claim, in another way is not. On one hand, where else and how else might our ideas be processed, other than in the brain, and using the circuitry that the brain has? On the other hand, ideas are not things that we see, nor is there an evident since organ for them, nor connections between such an organ and the neo cortex Or any other part of the brain. So somehow, ideas seem different. And indeed, when we look at the details, we see That in some ways they are.
column5: turns out that a lot of creatures with brains do not have a neocortex, yet they sense the world quite well. The simplest I’m nervous systems seem to have evolved on the order of 1 billion years ago, or 10 of the ninth, Whereas the neo cortex is peculiar to mammals Who have only been around for a few tens of millions of years. That’s 10 to the seventh. The difference is 10 to the 2, or about a factor of 100. So for a very long evolutionary time, brains were processing input for the sensors using elements of their brains which are also in some ways, with some differences but not radically many, parts of our own brains as well. The neo cortex evolves much later, and our human brains are distinguished primarily by the sheer size of our neo cortex, rather than by any particular functioning of its elements. Our sensory input is not only connected to this older part of our brain, though it is, the so-called old brain (though it to has evolved and interacts with the neocortex in subtle and many not yet understood ways.) We will return to the question of how and why our sensory input is connected in the human neo cortex, in addition to those parts of the brain with which alone our lizard cousins must make their way through the world. We mammals have a much larger and more complicated system. But before we go to explore those complications and Peer more carefully into the neo cortex, let’s begin a dive more carefully into the older brain. It will turn out that the development of mammals, and particularly of human beings, occurred very fast on an evolutionary scale, and us very likely reused or re-purposed resources that were already present to assist with functions which were already taking place, adding incremental advantages to already existing functions. At least that’s what we might suspect, and when we look, that seems to be what we find.
column8: it turns out that essential piece of the processing circuitry in the old brain, ours included, Is it kind of location processing that takes place in A series of complementary navigation cells, (technically place cells, Grid cells, position cells, and border cells.). These clever systems work on a mathematics that enables tracking movement relative to certain other locations, either to fixed locations, or to other relative locations. Adrift at sea, I may have a little idea where I am absolutely, but be able to tell when a wave is turning my small vessel upside down. That’s an example of location tracking Relative to a relative location. Olympic divers on the other hand, not only know which way is up, but roughly how much time he or she has before contacting the surface of the water. That is location Relative to a fixed location. The clever mathematics embodied in These location cells enable these calculations, and not just for human sailors and divers, but for reptiles as well. These functions, and the cells That embody these functions, Reside in the old brain, and more specifically in the hippocampus and other regions of both the older and newer brain. (double check and footnote.)
column10: If our suspicions are right that relatively rapid evolutionary processes borrow from and trade on existing Structures to incrementally improve existing functions, We might expect to find grid cells in the new brain as well. And indeed these have been discovered in the neo cortex. Grid cells here also are fed by sensory input, through the same series of connectors (the same connectome) that runs sensory information first to the Grid cells in the hippocampus, and then to our friends the columnated cortical calculators scattered throughout the neo cortex. our question will be, what are they doing there?
column11: it turns out that the neo cortex has a very complicated memory system that both serves and is served by the older brain. At the sensory level, we cannot only sense that something has happened, but that we reacted to it in a certain way, with a certain result. Armed with such patterns, we might be getting to predict that matters will fall out similarly, all things being equal, in the future. And when our senses tell us that all things are not equal, we have a basis for predicting change in the future. Our sense of the past, including the patterns we discern in the past, our our means for seeing into the future, for making predictions. These memories become for us models for interpreting new sensory input and making new kinds of predictions. And all of that pattern recognition, taking place in the memory that is the neo cortex, Relies on and deploys the grid cells that are present, with some modification but chiefly very much more complicated interconnections, in the layers of our collimated cortical calculators. These memories and predictions outstrip the capabilities demonstrated by our cousins with nearly lizard brains. But rats and dogs and generally all mammals have neocortex capable of performing such computations and generating such predictions.Throw a ball three times for your dog, and then hold your hands behind your back, and watch Him predict. Watch him strain to get it right. Watch him enjoy nearly every second of it.
column13: We would be right to notice that our dogs predictions are not exactly in the realm of ideas. He has no idea of play. he can play, but he cannot tease. He can be teased, but he won’t really get it. so we have not yet come far enough on our journey toward understanding what ideas are and how they might be processed, but neither have we finished exploring even the fundamentals of the complexity of our neo cortex. For while all the basic cortical functions are present in our mammalian cousins, what a striking is how much less of it is present in their brains than ours. Whales are an exception to this rule of thumb, but no exception when we plot The ratio of cortical mass to body mass. for our size, we have a great deal more to calculate with than any other mammals, including our primate cousins.

("Relect on our knowing expression of personal retrodiction, rather than surprise: "Of course he/she did".) This is an important point about freedom. But it missed what Pat was driving at, perhaps
column6: Getting to his point, perhaps: IN moments of surprise, we experience a kind of delight. Isn't that enjoyment also a mark of intelligence? Isn't it cultured, in fact...becoming able to enjoy the best things might be thought the purpose (mistakenly, I think, but not without some real insight) the point of education? Here's Allan Bloom's reflection along a similar line, from his chapter on Music in the Closing of the American Mind, p. 79-80:
column7: My concern here is not with the moral effects of this music —whether it leads to sex, violence or drugs. The issue here is its effect on education, and I believe it ruins the imagination of young people and makes it very difficult for them to have a passionate relationship to the art and thought that are the substance of liberal education. The first sensuous experiences are decisive in determining the taste for the whole of life, and they are the link between the animal and spiritual in us. The period of nascent sensuality has always been used for sublimation, in the sense of making sublime, for attaching youthful inclinations and longings to music, pictures and stories that provide the transition to the fulfillment of the human duties and the enjoyment of the human pleasures. Lessing, speaking of Greek sculpture, said "beautiful men made beautiful statues, and the city had beautiful statues in part to thank for beautiful citizens." This formula encapsulates the fundamental principle of the esthetic education of man. Young men and women were attracted by the beauty of heroes whose very bodies expressed their nobility. The deeper understanding of the meaning of nobility comes later, but is prepared for by the sensuous experience and is actually contained in it. What the senses long for as well as what reason later sees as good are thereby not at tension with one another. Education is not sermonizing to children against their instincts and pleasures, but providing a natural continuity between what they feel and what they can and should be.
column10: But this is a lost art. Now we have come to exactly the opposite point. Rock music encourages passions and provides models that have no relation to any life the young people who go to universities can possibly lead, or to the kinds of admiration encouraged by liberal studies. Without the cooperation of the sentiments, anything other than technical education is a dead letter. Rock music provides premature ecstasy and, in this respect, is like the drugs with which it is allied. It artificially induces the exaltation naturally attached to the completion of the greatest endeavors — victory in a just war, consummated love, artistic creation, religious devotion and discovery of the truth. Without effort, without talent, without virtue, without exercise of the faculties, anyone and everyone is accorded the equal right to the enjoyment of their fruits. In my experience, students who have had a serious fling with drugs — and gotten over it — find it difficult to have enthusiasms or great expectations. It is as though the color has been drained out of their lives and they see everything in black and white. The pleasure they experienced in the beginning was so intense that they no longer look for it at the end, or as the end. They may function perfectly well, but dryly, routinely. Their energy has been sapped, and they do not expect their life's activity to produce anything but a living, whereas liberal education is supposed to encourage the belief that the good life is the pleasant life and that the best life is the most pleasant life.
column13: But now watch (thanks, KT) Of Montreal's "It's different for girls": https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7_WDugKPnlk. The very picture of Bloom's licensiousness, on the surface. Except for the joy, the freedom, the intelligence it expresses. At its extreme, Bloom's culture becomes Nazi culture: the approved, what the wise know, in their shameful ignorance. Somehow, Bloom's recognition of the role that evocation of the passions through the senses plays in education--making what is good, seem good--has got to be acknowledged while also acknowledging and avoiding not only the threatended loss of happiness of straightjacketed persons (as 10,000 pricks do for every crazy bitch), but also the threat of loss of information that these 100-trillion synapsed wonders of sensory-social exploration are hindered from discovering. Culture thus protects and threatens human dignity. The challenge of our day is to recover appreciation for the role of culture, of fertile sheltering of human beings as children, while empowering and setting them free as adults, at the same time, in ways that in some ways depend on age but in others, do not.

column4: // amendment of understanding about interpretations of QM: Sean Carroll's Biggest Ideas in the U series, on entanglement, gets to what he says are no longer called interpretations of QM, but rather foundations of QM, where all candidates are testable, falsifiable scientific theories
column5: Everett's Many Worlds, SC's favorite, consists of just two claims: there are wave functions in hilbert space, and they describe the world all the time. (Not until collapse of wave functions.) A second: Bohmian Mechanics, which adss to the wave function a series of particles and fields that exist independently but are guided by the wave function in their behavior, wholistically, that is, non-locally. (Bell was an advocate of this view when he showed that nonlocal hidden variables were excluded excperimentally (his theory, later done in experiment) by the behavior of QM systems. Holeness (and the Implicate order--Bohm's book title) is a nonlocal hidden variable, in effect. It adds to the wave function some specifying parameters which guide collapse...
column7: Other theories think the collapse is an objective phenomena, either a random event (electrons decay randomly at imperceptibly small rates and induce collapse) or induced colllapse of some other kind. Yet another view is that QM is an epistemic theory; it doesn't describe the world, but rather our knowledge about the world. Quantum Baysianism is the old name, now Qbism, is such an epistemic theory. Find more in wikipedia article, SC says...

column6: The tradition-bound account of rational inquiry MacIntyre articulated and deployed throughout these lectures suggests reforms, explored by MacIntyre in chapter X, both for the lecture as a genre and for the university as an institution. To advance rational inquiry, MacIntyre argues that lectures ought to take account of the tradition-constituted roles both of lecturer and of student. Lecturers as members of explicitly articulated traditions should engage students, recognized as standing in various relationships to the lecturer's own and/or rival traditions, on material situated in the historically-contextualized progress of the lecturer's home tradition. In support of such lectures and of tradition-bound inquiry on the research front, universities should become forums for growing and engaging rival traditions. For students, such forums would invite intentional formation within a tradition, and support learning how to profitably confront rival traditions through imaginative participation in them. For researchers, inquiry at the frontier must advance toward holistic, inter-disciplinary accounts, at once both theoretical and practical, undertaken together by members of communities of practitioners bound together in traditions. Such tradition-bound inquiry contributes to the advancement of researchers' host traditions on their own terms. Moreover, undertaking such inquiry in a reformed university setting would support encounter among host traditions and their rivals, and thus visibility as traditions both to themselves and to others through imaginative engagement with rival perspectives. The public hosting of such engagements at suitably-reformed universities, including opportunities for agreement from complementary perspectives and for sharpening differences, would support both adjudication among the mutual claims to rational superiority among rival traditions, and initiation of students into the skill sets required to profitably join and assess such encounters.

column5: This video introduces a podcast series (a Zoom conversation, broadcast live to YouTube) focused on the creation of a new science of computational politics. That science emerges from the creation of a high-information-flow network of rich communities. In a rich community, information drawn from (optimally) a full range of experience about the world by members united in a common worldview is parallel processed in communities that become richer through growth in ordered causal contact with the world as they progress. As Alasdair MacIntyre has taught us, the incommensurable differences that emerge between such communities can be overcome through information exchange that preserves such causal contact, with the historical record of such encounters between rival traditions constituting a new source of data for a new master science. This new science fills the role of Aristotelian politics, but enriched by all that we have since learned about the sciences generally and about computational science most recently. Because such a science aims at a fully integrated theoretical and practical account of the world, it requires not only new forms of communication among disciplines, but more fundamentally new forms of social arrangements where the emerging implications of thus-far-successful world views are lived out in communities whose understanding and values these worldviews express. This discussion will take a medium-resolution pass at this whole new direction for human self-understanding and organization, including how to begin leveraging available technologies for setting up a high-information-flow network of rich communities and begin to generate data from it.

column4: // the whole of you which is conscious is not the whole of which you are conscious. We as conscious beings are more than that of which we are conscious. Our coming to know ourselves, our discovery of NCCs, are both dimensions of this limited self-awareness. Long traditons of becoming through growth in self-knowledge testify to this reality. So also, in another way, do traditions that treat self as illusory. They too find that conscious discovery is possible. What they think they discover--that the self is not real--may be better interpreted as discovery of an underlying unity among conscious beings that takes us beyond self. But this need not be interpreted as a denial of self, but rather a fulfilment. My personal love for you individually will remain precious to you as you mature in self-knowledge, or if you prefer, as consciousness matures in conscious knowledge. Each limited personal perspective is precious, far from unreal; rather, each reveals accurately--not falsely--the deep interconnectedness that runs through the whole fabric of consciousness, which is God. Each of us is God experiening what it is like to be us. This is no small part of the divine life. Love attends to these details. A mother's love for her child is of this mother for this child, wonderfully and irreducibly and completely. Models that miss this particularity may capture something else wonderful, but fail to capture this reality.

column14: "One can think of a Markovian kernel as simply an indexed list of probability measures. In the case of perception, for instance, a Markovian kernel might specify that if the state of the world is w1, then here is a list of the probabilities for the various conscious experiences that might result, but if the state of the world is w2, then here is a different list of the probabilities for the various conscious experiences that might result, and so on for all the possible states of the world. A Markovian kernel on a finite set of states can be written as matrix in which the entries in each row sum to 1"...."A Markovian kernel can also be thought of as an information channel. Cover and Thomas, for instance, define “a discrete channel to be a system consisting of an input alphabet X and output alphabet Y and a probability transition matrix p(x|y) that expresses the probability of observing the output symbol y given that we send the symbol x” (Cover and Thomas, 2006). Thus, adiscrete channel is simply a Markovian kernel."
column16:
"So, each time a conscious agent interacts with the world and, in consequence, has a conscious experience, we can think of this interaction as a message being passed from the world to the conscious agent over a channel. Similarly, each time the conscious agent has a conscious experience and, in consequence, decides on an action to take, we can think of this decision as a message being passed over a channel within the conscious agent itself. And when the conscious agent then takes the action and, in consequence, alters the state of the world, we can think of this as a message being passed from the conscious agent to the world over a channel. In the discrete case, we can keep track of the number of times each channel is used. That is, we can count the number of messages that are passed over each channel. Assuming that all three channels (perception, decision, action) all work in lock step, we can use one counter, N, to keep track of the number of messages"
that are passed.

column4: on PK Dick: https://www.theverge.com/2012/10/1/3424828/philip-k-dick-festival-science-fiction-change-your-life  For the AI-literate, there may be no more effective initial testimony to the power of love than this.
column5: “Jonathan Lethem notes how often, within their flawed and fallen worlds, Dick allowed his characters moments of humane grace. "There are a couple of his books that end with this uncanny expression of sudden, absurd, human connection of love — against the odds of which the entire book may seem to have been stacked." says Lethem. One example is Now Wait For Last Year. [Spoiler alert from here on out.] At the end of the novel, Dr. Eric Sweetscent finds himself in a taxi with his wife, Lethem says, "I mean, this is a wife as bad as American literature has ever presented, whether in Philip Roth or anywhere else...She's been destroyed by a drug she's taken, which is also a drug she's slipped into his drink at one point, in what is in some ways tantamount to a murder attempt. She's shattered her own and several other people's realities as well as their marriage by the end of this novel."
column7: Now she’s falling apart, giving him the opportunity to discard her, to get away, which he’s tried to do throughout the novel. "And the cab is a mechanical cab; it's a robot," Lethem explains "so Dr. Eric Sweetscent, in the way that characters in Dick have to do, is walking this inanimate object through the terms of the human condition: 'You know, you're a cab, you're a robot, you don't really get this, but she's horrible and I'm sad and this has all been so terrible and yet here she is, she's destroyed.' And the cab is listening to him, basically functioning as Freud would claim a therapist ought to: as a kind of impassive mirror. And the cab says, 'What I hear you saying is you have no choice.' And he says, 'What do you mean? No choice but to leave her?' And the cab says, 'No, I hear you saying you have no choice but to love this person.'"
column9: In the retelling he takes a moment here. There’s a hitch in his voice as he looks up and away, blinking a few times before continuing. "And, uh...that's as good as it gets," he says, "It's still as powerful a vision of the uncanny property of connection and love and empathy against the vortex of nihilism and despair and existential bleakness that the universe is. I don't even want to say it's hard-won, because I don't know how it's won. It's like claimed without being won. That's the essence of Dick. He claims love against a backdrop of its impossibility. He claims connection and empathy against a demonstrated nihilism. And that, for whatever reason, is the persuader for me."

column5: ingenious experiment, purporting to show that 1) our knowledge of particle trajectory is what detemines wave or particular behavior; and b) an entangled partner behaves differently in advance of that trajectory having been undertaken.
column6: Even a single photon that makes its way through a pair of open slits exhibits a wave-like interference pattern on the other side. Drop in a detector around one (or both) of those slits to see which way it passed, and you lose the interference pattern and get just particle-like behavior. So, split that single photon into two entangled, lower-energy photons, detect the one, and see if the other participates in creation of an interference pattern...it does not. So far, if you measure it, even indirectly, you lose its wavelike independence.
column8: OK, so take one further step...measure the entangled partners of each of the candidate interfering particles, but then scramble that information to keep it from the experimenter through a clever arrangement of overlapping pathway possibilities. That reintroduces the interference pattern...as if what really matters is what the observer knows, not where the particles travelled.  This is the quantum erasor...here, you erase information, and the result is interference of the entangled partners.
column9: Now, if you withdraw that scrambling system so that the scrambling takes place only well after the interference pattern is determined, the interference pattern remains...as if some future indeterminacy of information caused the interfering of particles in the past. Now we seem to be erasing not just information but the past, since that information erasing had an effect in the past. (Go further: put a quick switch in which either erases or doesn't the information about which path was taken by which particles, and make decisions about that switch long after the impact of any erasure. Now you seem to be erasing the past, at will...as if it's not just knowledge, but free choice on which the past depends...TL.)

column5: "what Shulman defined as Pedagogical Content Knowledge (PCK) [18,19]. Shulman defines PCK as the subject matter knowledge for teaching, or, in other words, PCK is a form of practical knowledge used by experts to guide their pedagogical practices in highly contextualized settings. Shulman writes “Within the category of pedagogical content knowledge, I include […] the most useful forms of representation of those ideas, the most powerful analogies, illustrations, examples, explanations, and demonstrations—in a word, the ways of representing and TEACHING ASSISTANTS’ PERFORMANCE AT … PHYS. REV. PHYS. EDUC. RES. 12, 010131 (2016) 010131-3 formulating the subject that make it comprehensible to others.” In addition, according to Shulman, PCK also includes “an understanding of what makes the learning of specific topics difficult: the conceptions and preconceptions that students bring with them to the learning of those most frequently taught topics and lessons.”"
column8: This is precisely what is needed at the interface of rich traditions: a craft of effective exchange of perspectives, a two-way PCK where the route to mutual understanding from each beginning is mapped. column5: dendrites fan out in seach of patterns; they spike when a cluster of synapses in a small (say 40 micron) area are all excited in some same window...thus dendrites are pattern recognizers...(where each of roughly 20 synapses (which turns out to be a mathematically optimal number) is uniquely connected to a different axon from some different neuron, almost always 1:1, so that you can model these as bits (on/off) in spite of the fairly wide stochastic distribution of chemical flow across synapses; that flow basically only gets you (never say never) a 0 or a 1.
column7: most dendrites are excitory, signal-seaching in function; about 20% (very important) seem to be negative, inhibitory in function, with maybe 20 or so different functions performed; these seem to be the logic, the gatekeepers of signal (only fire under this condition, when these other conditions are met...)  inhibitory cells (like basket cells) tend to be connected to many pyrimidal cells (with the pyramid conjoining dendridic trunks to axons...), and their connections tend not be learned, but just present and maximally push a stop signal to neighbors...
column9: neurons are connected in clusters, many to many, where each axon connects to a single dendrite, but with lots of close neighbors from cluster 1 to close neighbors to cluster 2, so that patterns embodied in neuron distribution are passed as information across biological neural networks...90% of what the neuron does is predictive; most of these predictions are silent; they don't leave the neuron. (Prediction: this isn't part of what's about to happen....(TL))
column10: There is backward spiking, forward down the axon, but backward up the dendrites (back action potential), which likely follows back along the route of the most recent forward dendridic spikes...(at least in terrns of efficacy on the synapses...)...hebbian learning gets back to condition synapses in dendridic sections via this back action potential propogation...
column11: ...error / unpredicted input is what stirs neuronal activity maximally; much less activity around predicted input...
column12: multistage predictive neuron: distant dendridic signals, correlating with promimate (near pyramid) dendridic spikes, passing through local inhibitory cells, producing axonal (somatic) spiking...all sitting inside a network of neighboring, shared (basked) inhibitory cellss...this is a much more complex picture than the standard input/output model of neurons...// frequency coding (there's another name) is a big deal in some cells (such as grid cells) but less so generally in cortical neurons...

column4: // reflections on Sean Carrol's Biggest Ideas in the Universe (BIU) #20: entropy and information
column5: Shannon: the amount of information caried by a symbol across a channel--the content--is a function of the "surprisal" factor", where p(x) is the probability of that information symbol x being the case, against contextual background; the amount of information I(x) = log (1/(px) = - log p(x) = negative exponent of the mathematical expression of the unlikelihood. (If the information is a 1-in-a-million chance of being expected, then I(x) = - log (10^-6) = 6 (that quantification is my interpretation of Sean Carrol's account of Claude Shannon's meaning.)
column6: Think about what this implies for information processing within vs. across rich communities. As a rule, filtered through the same worldview, claims from peers inside a tradition will be largely expected; claims from persons outside of one's tradition, filtered differently, will likely be more surprising. So to grow in information content, a tradition's store of information will want to include some proportion of information from outside the community. That's the question, the idea, the avenue for exploration. Can we tighten this up, and quantify it? Somehow we need a distinction between "surpising" and "improbable". For we can come to accept very surpising things, only if they cohere in some way with a number of other things we think, and thus become probable enough to accept, though surpsising in some other sense. So there must exist some sizable pocket of background against which a piece of information is inprobable, and another against which it is probable, for us to accept any piece of information as surprising. These pockets of information must cohere--seem likely to stand or fall together--to delimit pocket boundaries.
column9: So, then, from external sources may be more likely to come communication--bits of information--with large content, as unlikely against general background view but as especially plausible against some important subset of information, some coherent pocket of information, which is valuable as information and at risk without itself coherening with the new content brought in from the outside. There's got to be some math for this. And this would describe, for example, a potential analysis from outside a tradition of why it emerged, and a potential solution for it of an epistemological crisis within a tradition, such as AM describes as pivotal in the history of interactions among traditions, potentially of historical significance in terms of the results of the processing by the mature members of engaging traditions. These processings may, on occation of conversion of mature inhabitands from one to another tradition on account of the illumination of the roots of the epistemological crises of the one in light of the other, So, Q: might the net results of such encounters be an overall increase in information, even in principle measurable or quantitatively and nomologically characterizable? For perhaps, in such cases, a potentially rich pocket of information, highly productive and illuminating if true, stands at risk of diminishment when multiplied through by the improbability of the pocket surviving scritiny against the background of the whole tradition (thus creating an epistemological crisis--a potential loss of net information--inside the tradition), only to be rescued by inclusion in that tradition (via subversion, transformation, or even just abandonment and escape to a more promising and information-rich rival)--of an explanation for how that crisis arose and how information can better be preserved through repair (with external, surprising resources) of the original tradition, or replacement of it by another with even better problem-solving, i.e., information-amassing, capacity.
column14: So when communities interact, they do so in such a way as to maximze information retention and growth...or, rather, this is what successful interaction looks like. Isn't it possible that they go to war with one another and destroy both accumulated stores of information, in largest measure? Or systematically warp the store of information trickling in from a percieved rival, and so fail to learn from it, instead deflecting only those apparently false claims trickling through the self-imposed filter, claims the rival does not in fact make? (Better language here is perspectives, that the rival tradition does not in fact adopt, so that the one tradition imagines deeply dysfunctional human beings adopting rediculous perspectives, when those are not at all what mature members of the rival tradition entertain or adopt.
column16: If there is a quantitative law governing interactions among communities, it might be more like this: the quantity of information exchange across the whole is a function of the quantity of specific types of information exchange among specific types of its members (something like imaginative cohabitation of rivals among trusted mature members.) Trust, maturity, and imaginative cohabitation are all necessary (sufficient?) for information to flow: (1) maturity, for setting the relevant probabilities (such as surprisal against one subset, plausibility against another necessary for rich-content information flow); (2) imaginative cohabitation, for making contact with the actual information stored cognitively and noncognitively in the rival tradition; and (3) trust, for information to flow throughout the receiving tradition through cognitive (explicit teaching) and noncognitive (exemplification, cultural placement, etc) mechanisms.
column18: Way out on a limb, here, bearing in mind the distinctions among content (amount) of info transfered, rate of that transfer to mature members of t1, rate of transfer throughout t1 to less mature members, and degree of saturation of that information throughout all members):  is the total effective information transfer from t2 to t1 (I-trans-t2->t1) equal to the product of the amount of information available in t2 (I-total-t2) * # of full information transfers through cohabitation of mature members from t1 in t2 (I-trans-mt1<-t2) * speed of that transfer * average rate of transfer from mature members of t1 to less mature members of t1 * # of less mature members. This is something like the growth rate to a new effective surface area in t1 of a quantity of information from t2. This is something like the virality of that information to spread itself from a single instance in t1 to effective spread throughout t2. Not the only quantity to measure, just the first that occurred to me to try to hammer out.

column5: Hey, everybody. Again, very sorry (Rachel : ) to have misfired a bit on my text canceling tonight's meeting. Setting up for Monday, I want to add just one more (a 3rd) dimension to this limited discussion of rights, which are central to the retelling of the American story. Last time, we discussed (1)) a definition of rights (as needs, whose satisfaction is, in the judgement of the mature members of the most stable community(ies), conducive to the good of every person); and (2) envisioned a flourishing federalist system of limited government promoting experiments in human living to nurture the growth and interchange among a diverse range of experiments in human communities. So far, sounds pretty American. But what exactly are the contents of those rights? That content comes from the deliverances of the mature members of the best (most stable) communities, so far. I want to capture essential content of that sort in the idea of the dignity of human persons, which is defined loosely in the attached "Resanctifying Human Life", and developed more fully in terms of the then-best current thinking about self-esteem in "Towards a Full Theory of Self-Esteem", parts 1 & 2.
column9: These are old papers, but they were written as foundational material for the very project we are now seeing come to life (the full Resurrecting Civility project.) The point in sharing them is to share some idea of the kind of terrain, (3)--sometimes called rational psychology, which is roughly about human psychological health/wellbeing--that must be tapped in some way to provide substance to the form of rights, (1) and the venue for discovering the content of rights (2). In this sense,
column11: I think America is a Christian country in its roots, insofar as respect for the dignity of human beings is the fruit of reflection on Christian living (which I think in some important sense it is.) No worries about digesting all or any of this. We'll recap on Monday, and dive only as deep into the papers as you're inclined to go. We'll then have a wrap on the fundamental pillars of the story of the American past: an empirical account of the nature of rights claims, of the sort--with the content--that we associate with our founding documents, and as the emerging fruit of the limited government American federalism. Then on to a similar light treatment of Christianity. Then back for a higher-resolution pass at the foundations, following roughly the schema of the 3-lense matrix. Have a great weekend : )

column5: A sense of order is the easiest and most natural way to begin; it is a needed first “container.” I cannot think of a culture in human history, before the present postmodern era, that did not value law, tradition, custom, family loyalties, authority, boundaries, and morality of some clear sort. While they aren’t perfect, these containers give us the necessary security, predictability, impulse control, and ego structure that we need, before the chaos of real life shows up. As far as I can see it, healthily conservative people tend to grow up more naturally and more happily than those who receive only freeform, build-it-yourself worldviews.

column5: On Law: "As Paul belabors in his Letter to the Romans (see especially chapters 2–7), the law is given for the sake of information, education, and transformation, but is not itself enlightenment. Even though allegiance to boundaries, limits, and laws is almost universally confused with religion and even salvation itself, “the law will not save anyone” (Galatians 3:11). Law has to do with the pattern of how transformation happens—and that’s all. The struggle with boundaries and law creates the wrestling ring, but is not, itself, the encounter or the victory."

column8: "If there are truly stories from the great patterns that are always true, they will catapult us into a universal humanity and pluralistic society. We will both stand on solid ground and, from that solid ground, create common ground. If it does not support our movement outward, then it is not solid ground at all."
column9: A couple of important points here. First, in complete agreement: note the instrumentalist account of what constitutes solid ground and even possibly common ground: stories that help us filter reality give us useful, ultimately transformative and pespective-transcending purchase on the world.
column10: Second, the stories that may illumine common ground RR describes as universal. That phrase has multiple possible senses. He may mean that these stories appear in every tradition, and are something like least common denominators (to borrow the mathematical cliche.) But other stories, that are not LCD in that sense, may have universal appeal, only in conditions of full imaginative immersion in a given tradition, which may be (in the limiting case) the only repository of that wisdom, but universally recognized by members from every tradition who enter into that 2nd kind of maturity. That's a very different sense of universal, or rather, maybe the same sense but applied very differently. We should be careful to distinguish these two. Only the latter, not the LCD variety, could store much information, it would seem, even though the LCD variety may describe some very important but hard-to-miss (unsurprising, low information content) features of reality.

column4: // a couple of moments on the proposed definition of love as "active favor toward the active favor of another" ... the line of inquiry is about a computational, information-processing understanding of that account. When we love, are we computing something? It seems so: we understand a situation (sit in a particular informed state) as one containing another informed state, with a corresponding set of inclinations, understood as a computed response to current input in that perspective, that standpoint (thanks, Jo-el), that problem so composed, so compiled, so poised for a result, which is an action (this is practical deliberation, whose deliverance is an action (thanks, Aristotle). This is response, this inclination, is an active favor, a symmetry-breaking result, a favored response, a tentative result, all in the life of the beheld agent. And we behold that state of affairs, that sprung spring, that acting person...exercising active favor of some set of perceived, intended, pursued outcomes (pursued, because our perspectives are particularized in ways whose boundry conditions are perceived by us as the flow of time in space, and reflectively, abstractly, as space-time.) So observing the active observer, we join our own computational capacity toward theirs, and move closer to a model of coherent computation, a more powerful explanatory and directive model that includes more than that governing the observed agent. We provide computational assistance. "I'm going to help that person, insofar as I can...their perspective embodies in some measure something successful and promising and contributory to the emerging universal model, and I'm aware of and in favor of that outcome...I'll enhance it, as I can...this is active favor toward the active favor of another. It is computational assistance...
column8: ...continuing...can we think of love computationally...in terms of information processing...the instinct here is that we can't not think of it this way, once we get the model...this is a powerful, low-potential-energy state, a perspective one falls into and doesn't climb out of, because it keeps providing computational power (supports understanding and guides...)

column9: 2b. (or not...that's the q...): there is a stronger relationship between lens 1 and (now call it) CRm (conscious realism monism): not only is nothing else required; nothing else makes any sense...that's how the argument will go. The very short version runs along these lines: Thomas Kuhn offered an account of scientific practice that moves toward ever-greater sets of solutions to solved problems, but in no particular direction, toward no truth, only away from recognized but unsolved problem sets. MacIntyre has resources, used resources, to say to TK (in a place I recall but can't quite find): sure, progress may take place happenstantially, but it doesn't stay happenstantial. Later groups of researchers can retrospectively identify sources of epistemological crises that occasioned then-seemly-arbitraryness-tinged scientific revolutions, and show why those revolutions occurred, had to occur, and needed the solutions provided through change of paradigm. OK...so far, so good...looks like progress toward truth. But TK: "thanks for your insight. But I can explain how your insight had to arise---not in this form, but in some form, with other problems remaining yet unsolved, which be which, a complex accident--from future inquiry. And yet all you are moving toward is stability, an ensemble of resources for solving problems as so far recognized (in turn stochasticly distributed across recognition space), being pulled toward no particular future, but forging ahead toward any more stable future...finding its way toward scientific richness and stability (TK might say but may not quite have said), There is no other possible outcome: stability, or less, in one community, or more than one, but only a measure of stability. column5: consciousness is information of a certain sort: namely, a subset of information exchange processes called C-processes, involving information closure. In general, information closure of a system is a state where no information from the external environment flows into that system. This can be due to system independence, but it need not be: a system can contain information about its environment, so that is future states are determined by its internal information about that environment, so that the shared information content between environment and system is greater than zero. This is the meaning of NTIC, non-trivial information closure. Need to learn to say what C processes are, more precisely, in terms of NTIC.
column6: More specifically, a C-process is a course-graining of X onto Y, which is an NTIC process. Course-graining seems to amount to modeling, or perhaps to include modeling.
column7: also, we should apply Michael Graziano's challenge (Atlantic article, above) to ICT: why does information closure explain the emergence of a conscious experience? How does the ICT necessary and sufficient conditions (that Cs be a C-process) explain why Cs emerges when that C-process emerges? Isn't it plausible that the same set of conditions could be generated in computer modeling? Do we want to thereby have discovered AI Cs by finding the right account of Cs?

Intelligent activity is consious activity. Perception (not simple reaction, but abstracted, modeled, lensed reaction) is awareness (the detector knows that it has detected, has a counter that it reacts to and offloads to longer-term memory according to some algorithm programmed into it, even if by another, or perhaps by itself, according to some other program operating that it did not itself program nor thus can recognize, except in the abstract) is conscious at some basic level. column15: Perspective (limited or othewise) is the principle of individuation for all of being, which is, all of consciousness. Our models classify similarities and differences among these limited pespectives, and thus create a taxonomy, an ontology. And the hightest ontological category in that process is being = consciousness. There are many ways to classify, but the most productive/efficient/insightful/appealing classifications achieve a measure of stability, and the limit of such stability (through comparison with rival classificatory schemes, rival models) are what we mean by true classifications. There are what we create as true, in this artistic process of model/craft development. (Models are the guiding principles to craft, the flip sides of the perception/reaction dichotomy, the p/r, or model/craft, m/c coin.)

column4: // Notice the account of truth embodied in the preceding line of reflections on perception/action. My long suspicion has been that this is Aquinas's account of truth, but applied to a perspective on creation that Aquinas did not as fully (as stably) grasp.
column5: In DeV and (if memory serves) through ST (and probably also in DeM: check, re/ maturity of his thought), Aq has a two-tiered account of truth: correspondence between mental image (here, model) and object, but in two ways distinguished by the direction of causality: 1) as the correspondence of the thing made to the image in the mind of the artist (this is T in God); and 2) as the correspondence of the mind of the modeler (the mental model, the image) to the thing modeled, perceived (this is T in creatures.)
column6: This dual sense of truth applies in a pespectivally individuated consious realist monism. The creative sense is the primary sense. But that creativity is exercised by created agents (that is, precisely by conscousness as operating under limited perspective) under conditions moving from the more limited to the less and eventually the unlimited. That process of model transformation seems to be responding to an external reality, a mind-independent, thus consciousness-independent, reality. But in fact that process of model transformation is responding to preferred creative perspective as it emerges through the process of increasingly less limited computational modeling. We model until we like the results, in a stable way that responds maximally to information, gathered through some process of discovery in moving from more limited to less limited perspectives. That discovery of "my truth", my perspective, is essentially creative, and produces internal harmony among the limited perspectives in the whole: all created, limited life, is God's life, but not yet fully. So the reality that we encounter, and to which we seek to align our models, our understanding, is in fact a self-created reality, but not created by us under the limitatioins of our current perspective, but instead under increasingly less limited perspective, toward the unlimited perspective of the whole. We are taking computationally reducible short-cuts, through our modeling, to the fully interconnected and computationally irreducible whole that is unlimited consiousness and the generator of every limited perspective, of every created thing.

column5: https://www.power-and-beyond.com/understanding-the-difference-between-n-and-p-type-semiconductors-a-905805/ describes the basic unit of information processing: the electronics that embody a 1 or a 0 (current flows, or current doesn't, in a forward or reverse (respective) bias configuration.
column6: n n-type doping, arsenic or phosphorus is added in small quantities to the silicon. Both of these elements have five electrons in their outer orbitals and so they are not out of place when they get into the silicon crystalline structure. Since the fifth electron has nothing to bond to, it is free to move around, allowing an electric current to flow through the silicon.
column7: In p-type doping, boron or gallium is used as the dopant. These elements each have three electrons in their outer orbitals. When they are mixed into the silicon lattice, they form ‘holes’ where a silicon electron has nothing to bond to. The absence of an electron then creates the effect of a positive charge. Holes in the silicon can conduct an electrical current and they can accept an electron from a neighbor, moving the hole over by one space.
column8: If you connect the negative (-ve) end of the battery to the p-side and the positive (+ve) end to the n-side (‘reverse bias’), free charge carriers are pulled aside, and the depletion zone widens. This turns the junction into an insulator and further inhibits electrical current flow.
column9: However, if you connect the -ve end of the battery to the n-side and the +ve end to the p-side (‘forward bias’), charge carriers are pushed into the middle, knocking out the depletion zone and turning the p-n junction into a conductor. This is because holes from the p-side are repelled by the +ve end of the battery and electrons in the n-side are repelled by the -ve end of the battery. Atoms at the junction can now hand off charge carriers to one another, allowing current to flow freely.
column10: This is a very basic example of how the most elementary type of semiconductor device, the diode, works. Put a few billion of these together back-to-back and you’ve got a computer chip!

column4: // here's a sketch of a deep dive (multilevel exploration from biggest picture down to some relevant detail) in the area of combining lenses=models. Diving down to the way model combination is used in ML: https://blog.statsbot.co/ensemble-learning-d1dcd548e936
column5: intelligence is all about model creation, combination and extension. (This is an intelligence lens, an intelligence model.) Intelligent activity is fundamentally about noticing, including notice of particulars, connections, and patterns at multiple levels. (Illustrate this, especially quantitatively.) We are driven to notice: noticing is a fundamental end, and activities undertaken to notice are end-directed in that sense. Because noticing is an inherently perspectival, conscious activity (though, importantly for AI, see aside #1 below) and because some perspectives are limited (abstracted at some level, functioning at intermediate levels of resolution (is resolution the full or best analogy/metric?), limited (created, if self-limited?) intelligence is constantly looking for models that more efficiently reveal what is outside of their current, limited perspective. In that sense, intelligent activity consists in satisfaction of its end (noticing, which as satisfying is enjoyment), and striving to notice (computing, calculating.) Models are efficient ways of noticing, shortcuts to observation. (In Steven Wolfram's language, they are shortcuts to the computationally irreducible, that is, computation reducers, ways to get to a predicted outcome faster than waiting for it. ("Once the thing happens, then the fool sees it."))
column8: (now, after running off after the next two themes on AI/CI and the nature of scientific justification and the role of a master science), returning to this deep dive sketch around model combination: so we are constantly creating and expanding models, toward a more complete and coherent grasp of our possibilities (possible ways for us to experience the world.)
column9: Thus pursuit, application and connection of models characterizes and in fact IS intelligent behavior. Combination of models amounts to alignment of lenses, creating new, more powerful, more useful (predictive, controlling) lenses from less. This activity, quantified in its results, is science. The emergence of human behavior into scientic behavior is the natural (end-directed, end-productive) evolution of thought.
column10: So combining lenses, models, is a big deal. We study it only haphazardly now: in neuroscience at the level of synaptic mapping; in computer science at the level of algorithmic design (with software and hardware components); Hegel, Marx and others studied it philosophically and historically. MacIntyre has picked up that philophical/historical study and recaptured an algorithm for combining lenses (worldviews, traditions) that would characterize a master science.
column11: The RC project advances M's project toward design and implementation of a social, historical, philosophical, scientific motherboard for rational inquiry, an enacted master science. That's the sketch of a deep dive into model combination, or ensembling, as described in the article linked earlier in this row, from which sections are abstracted next. Note the last line ("In order for ensemble methods to be more accurate than any of its individual members, the base learners have to be as accurate as possible and as diverse as possible.") What is true for combined models in ML is analogous to combining competing whole perspectives in dialectical engagement: in order for any worldview (lens, model, perspective, tradition) to advance, its base learners (considered as pre-combined, though there is combination inside of every calculation to bring two sets of values together, a state vector and some operator) have to be as accurate as possible individually, and as diverse as possible collectively. There are associated virtues with each of those regulative ideals (accuracy and diversity): for another time.
column14: Ensemble methods are meta-algorithms that combine several machine learning techniques into one predictive model in order to decrease variance (bagging), bias (boosting), or improve predictions (stacking). Ensemble methods can be divided into two groups: (1) sequential ensemble methods where the base learners are generated sequentially (e.g. AdaBoost); The basic motivation of sequential methods is to exploit the dependence between the base learners. The overall performance can be boosted by weighing previously mislabeled examples with higher weight. (2) parallel ensemble methods where the base learners are generated in parallel (e.g. Random Forest). The basic motivation of parallel methods is to exploit independence between the base learners since the error can be reduced dramatically by averaging. Most ensemble methods use a single base learning algorithm to produce homogeneous base learners, i.e. learners of the same type, leading to homogeneous ensembles. There are also some methods that use heterogeneous learners, i.e. learners of different types, leading to heterogeneous ensembles. In order for ensemble methods to be more accurate than any of its individual members, the base learners have to be as accurate as possible and as diverse as possible.

column5: Imagine, sit inside, TK's account of science. There is T, no final end of inquiry, just movement toward some richer base of solved problems. Even in that system, there is craft, there is mastery, there is consistency with what has already been found, what is promising as a new direction (granted, no single way forward, but surely many dead ends.) Already, it looks like there are boundaries on rational inquiry. Other people shape the language, with you as the smallest part; that inherited language (concepts, presumptions, vocabulary, accepted rules of practice, etc) shapes your inquiry, constrains it as if it were from the outside, while empowering/guiding it, rendering it intelligible. Within those constraints, inside it, you are free to think and move and create. Outside, you are doing either merely not science (but intelligible some other way: art?) or nothing intelligible (if you are constrained/empowered by no rules/concepts with anyone.) But you are not entirely free to succeed, even then, moving in just any direction: the world contrains you beyond the constraints of language or craft. It just ain't so; things might just not be as you imagined them to be. Very creative hypotheses might fail. These are puzzles, after all, and solutions are hard to come by.
column8: Now, stay there, but also jump inside (add a 2nd lens) of pCRm. Now these constraints are not from external physical reality, or from people who fundamentally are or emerge from that reality. So what is there to constrain, to explain the relucance of the world to submit easily to explanation, prediction or control?
column9: Well, the contents of one's perspective might contrain. And if there are multiple perspectives, multiple conscious agents, the limitations either of another's perspective, or of one's communication with another, might contrain. The shared language, common elements of limited shared perspective, might have only so many resources. OK, but what's the analogue of contraints from the physical world: great idea, super science, but just not so?
column10: One possible answer: contraints from others which are limited and common but not arrived at through the front door of consciousness, but noncognitively, noncognitively for you, that is, but not for someone else. Suppose that what we see or are aware of might come to us from sources we see or of which we are also aware, but perhaps otherwise. So ringing in my ears comes from where, exactly? I've got a theory, but I did not myself observe it coming into my conscious field. How about what I see directly in front of me? I may have seen it come to be, but I may not have: I am just aware. There is some stability to that of which I'm aware, but some flux, some uncertainty. I create and predict it, JH and other neurotheorists tell us. But from physicalist input, they say. But what if that's not so: what if it comes to us from other conscious agents, other perspectives which are plainly in some respects richer than ours, but still calculating, still reaching, trying, generating...?
column12: Suppose our own perspectives are made up of, and those to which we ourselves contribute as lesser parts are also made up of, multiple perspectives that add wholistically (multiple parts make some new and larger whole) even though each part is a perspective? And what if that communication is two-way: from smaller to larger (often unnoticed), and from larger to smaller (again, noticed or not)? As if the rules of quantum mechanics prohibit our knowing certain things, so the reality we perceive just lacks that information, because the pespective calculating by means of those QM rules just isn't feeding us what it doesn't have? So perhaps there is no fact of the matter as to what happened first, since all the perspectives feeding us are relative, and no fact to the matter about the past or cause of an event, because no perspective feeding us that perception is trying out a perspective that contains a past or cause?
column14: Feeding us? What is it for one perpective to feed another? What is it to communicate? Often this is done intentionally and consciously by and to both parties, but often from either to or from the other unseen but not unimpacted. Now I see; you arranged it but this I did not see. Or I intentionally earned from you, but this you did not see. Or we interacted, but neither of us saw...there was some information exchange, some changes in each of our perspctives, because of the other but not known to be because of the other or maybe even not possibly known to be because of the other. This could occur and be known to occur at the instigation of some third party. One rabbit sees another but never knows that they were brought into this engagement by a breeder...that's over their head, outside their perpectives. You can't get here from there: "we were bred" is never part of a rabbit's perspective, though it is true about their lives impacted their perspectives.
column16: So now suppose that what we perceive as the phyical world, not persons in it impacting our perspectives in ways we can perceive, but other dimensions we perceive as nonpersonal and nonintentional, nevertheless are part of our lives at the agency of persons with intentions and perspectives that outstrip our own. column18: Moveover, we might actually be part of the life of that higher perspective, as if the set of all human perpectives were at once perceived as a human life. column5: First, I really like DWP. // So what is real? On the DWP view, what is real is something near to the deliverances of our senses, and the distance between the real and the perceived narrows as we extend our senses with various detectors (DWP: "our technology will save us".) What I want to explore here are the advantages of pCRm with respect to our intuitions re/ what is real.
column6: Context: 2-way causality re truth: mind to object, object to mind. St. T: Correspondence both ways, one as artisan, the other as observer. But world has two nasty observer problems: relativity - many perspectives from different observers of seemingly same world; and observer-dependence - apparent causation by observation of being at least at quantum level. pCRm addresses both problems. Re relativity: strategy is to bite the bullet, admit different realities except insofar as information would eliminate them. So what is perceived is real, unless further inquiry would indicate otherwise, exactly what we now think. Here, to be real is to belong to, be a subset of, the conscious field of a mature member of a stable tradition. One reality is an empirical possibility (one possible feature of the computation of the path to maximal enjoyment, to unity) and in fact the goal of rational activity. Re observer-dependence: duh. everything is observer dependent. What about apparent observer independence and increasing correspondence of observation to world through further inquiry? Such inquiry is calculation of new states. Insofar as additional information contact would lead to computation of new states, calculation/perception-as-present is susceptible to change in certain respects (since erroneous computation is computation emerging from states and producing new possible states of awareness); isfa no new information will impact the stable computation, those states are stable. We perceive such states as corresponding to correct computation, to stable states of consciousness, to the way the world really is. But these are all observer-dependent states of consciousness. Some will change on contact with new information; others will not. This is the world as it is revealed at the quantum level, as observer-dependent. At more complete levels of perception, this stability-or-otherwise is experienced as output from worldviews, which depend upon much larger sectors of state vectors than those much more limited states focused on at the quantum level. Clashes of worldview and choices/conversion among them are the same as? akin to? emergent from? collapses of the wave packet, observer-dependent state production at the quantum level.
column10: Careful: new states are constantly being produced in dynamism of information flow, changing perspectives in the information sea of consciousness. (Information is conscious states; perspectives imply limits on information content; these are mathematizable? subsets of the set of all conscious states, all possibilities (by way of limited perpective), pure actuality by way of full awareness. (The actual world is a perspective.) Each change in perspective (perceived under our perspectives as the passage of time) is a computation, an output, a new state vector resulting from an operating vector acting on a previous state vector. These relations are logical orderings in themselves, present all at once in pure actuality, Creation is consideration of limited perspectives, which are both us as simply limited and God/All as containing the limited. Optimal relations among perspectives into a united mutual benefit/maximally enjoyable whole state are present in themselves, but also are present as logical relations, output of computations, and in that respect are experienced as limited perspectives, the perceived flow of computation is the measure of time. p. 8 of 11: "The real question is this: Is Gödel's theorem a mandate for revolution, anarchy, and license?! Can
one give up after trying for two months to prove a theorem, and add it as a new axiom? This sounds
ridiculous, but it is sort of what number theorists have done with Bernhard Riemann's ζ conjecture
(Pólya, 1959). Of course, two months is not enough. New axioms should be chosen with care,
because of their usefulness and large amounts of evidence suggesting that they are correct, in the
same careful manner, say, in practice in the physics community.
Gödel himself has espoused this view with remarkable vigor and clarity, in his discussion of whether
Cantor's continuum hypothesis should be added to set theory as a new axiom (Gödel, 1964):"
column6: "... even disregarding the intrinsic necessity of some new axiom, and even in case it has no intrinsic necessity at all, a probable decision about its truth is possible also in another way, namely, inductively by studying its ``success.'' Success here means fruitfulness in consequences, in particular in ``verifiable'' consequences, i.e., consequences demonstrable without the new axiom, whose proofs with the help of the new axiom, however, are considerably simpler and easier to discover, and make it possible to contract into one proof many different proofs. The axioms for the system of real numbers, rejected by intuitionists, have in this sense been verified to some extent, owing to the fact that analytical number theory frequently allows one to prove numbertheoretical theorems which, in a more cumbersome way, can subsequently be verified by elementary methods. A much higher degree of verification than that, however, is conceivable. There might exist axioms so abundant in their verifiable consequences, shedding so much light upon a whole field, and yielding such powerful methods for solving problems (and even solving them constructively, as far as that is possible) that, no matter whether or not they are intrinsically necessary, they would have to be accepted at least in the same sense as any well-established physical theory."
column8:
"Later in the same discussion Gödel refers to these ideas again:
'It was pointed out earlier... that, besides mathematical intuition, there exists another (though only probable) criterion of the truth of mathematical axioms, namely their fruitfulness in mathematics and, one may add, possibly also in physics... The simplest case of an application of the criterion under discussion arises when some... axiom has number-theoretical consequences verifiable by computation up to any given integer.' "
column9: "Gödel also expresses himself in no uncertain terms in a discussion of Russell's mathematical logic (Gödel, 1964): 'The analogy between mathematics and a natural science is enlarged upon by Russell also in another respect... axioms need not be evident in themselves, but rather their justification lies (exactly as in physics) in the fact that they make it possible for these ``sense perceptions'' to be deduced... I think that... this view has been largely justified by subsequent developments, and it is to be expected that it will be still more so in the future. It has turned out that the solution of certain arithmetical problems requires the use of assumptions essentially transcending arithmetic... Furthermore it seems likely that for deciding certain questions of abstract set theory and even for certain related questions of the theory of real numbers new axioms based on some hitherto unknown idea will be necessary. Perhaps also the apparently insurmountable difficulties which some other mathematical problems have been presenting for many years are due to the fact that the necessary axioms have not yet been found. Of course, under these circumstances mathematics may lose a good deal of its ``absolute certainty;'' but, under the influence of the modern criticism of the foundations, this has already happened to a large extent...'  "
column11: " I end as I began, with a quotation from Weyl (1949): ``A truly realistic mathematics should be conceived, in line with physics, as a branch of the theoretical construction of the one real world, and should adopt the same sober and cautious attitude toward hypothetic extensions of its foundations as is exhibited by physics.''  "
