column13: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zlm1aajH6gY from Quanta magazine - here, a very helpful background on Reimann's Zeta Hypothesis, which is Chaitin's example of a hypothesis adopted based on its usefulness in both mathematics and physics. Here's the background: the distribution of primes runs a lot like 1/log(n); this is Gauss's conjecture. Add to that, Euler's work on convergence/divergence of series (to a limit, or not.)  Reimann takes that further: first, proving that the limit of the series of 1/(each prime, squared) is pi^2/6--wtf does pi show up here?--and in general for exponent s, pi^s/some constant. Now consider the general series of the sum of 1/)each prime)^s: what's its limit? R creates a framework for answering this question, by expanding it into the complex plane (?), and conjectures with some experimental verification that the limit is 1/2 in that expanded universe of numbers (Real and Imaginary.)

column4: // The DNA revolution, over the last 3 years...fro Robert Plomin's Royal Institute Lecture, Is it nature instead of nurture that makes you? CAREFUL HERE: THIS ("Bueprint: ....") IS AN IMPORTANT BOOK BUT MUCH CHALLENGED, BY KAUFMAN AND ANOTHER IN SCI AM BOOK REVIEWS. // RP is producing an intro to how to think about DNA and behavioral differences, abstracting from epigenetics, gene editing, and evolution, as he says, to focus on this one area of heritability of DNA influences on personality (and tangentially and for illustration, physical differences including health) in this book. This row, part 1.
column5: Key sentence: "The only systematic stable longlasting source of who we are is DNA." // last line of preface: "Our future is DNA." //  DNA is the overriding factor. RP: "Title should be: how DNA makes us who we are as individuals" Environment is important, but doesn't seem to work systematically. You'de be the same person, no matter how you were raised and educated. See "Three Identical STrangers", a set of identical triplets, coming out soon.
column6: Blueprint is about what makes us different psychologically. // Taken together, psychological traits are 50% genetically influenced: DNA matters as much as all the other factors--most hard to control--together. (Already: "hard to control, random", etc, in what environment? That's what cultures do. Hard to control consistent academic encouragement...almost impossible...in one culture; the air that they breath, in another.)
column7: All psychological traits are heritable: how much of the differences between individuals are explained by DNA. 99% of our DNA does not differ between us. Heritability refers to the 1% that makes us different; having the same 99% is "genetic" but not a matter of heritability. Heritability doesn't include effects of mutation across generations, either. Heritability is about the successful passing on the 1% of SNPs (single nucleotide polymorphisms--ways we can be genetically different in our DNA).
column8: Heritability of body weight is greater in wealthy countries, because it exposes genetic differences in tendencies to react to fatty foods, which drive body weight in wealthier cultures. So heritability can be environmentally influenced. What is v. what could be is important: heritability is about what is. So body weight is heritable in these circumstances. Breast cancer: genetics is only 10%. (ID twins, one with breast cancer: 15% more likely that the other has it...a 50% increase of relative risk of 10% that everyone has...)
column9: "Environment" is used more widely in genetics studies--any factor other than inherited DNA differences--than typically used in behavioral studies. Genetic changes due to mutation would be environmental in genetic studies, but typcially not environmental in behavioral studies. For geneticists, "environment" means "non-heritable".  Watch for a "completely different way" of thining about the relationship between nature and nurture as we go forward<< Ch 1.//
column10: Variance, covariance, correlation: variance and covariance are statistics describing individuality. Variance describes the extent to which people differ; covariance indexes similarity. Correlation indicates the proporation of the variance that covaries. Correlation of zero indicates no similarity between two traits; 1 means perfect resemblance between the two traits. Moderate correlation is .3; .5 is substantial. (Weight and height correlate .6.) Can correlate a trait for one member of a twin pair with the other trait in the other twin. (W;W: .84 for ID twins, .55 for nonID cotwins.) ID twins reared apart is the strongest test for heritability. Several hundred results have been reported; MN Study of Twins Reared Apart, 56 pairs. Swedish study had 100+ pairs. Weight correlation, MN and Sweden: .73 (.75 over all ID Twin studies.) Thus the 70% weight heritability estimate.  UK study of 20K sets of twins (TEDS.)
column12: Heritability increases during development. Weight is 40% in EC to 80% in adulthood. Weight of adopted children correlates about zero to adoptive parents. When two unrelated children are adopted in the same home, weight correlation is about zero. Similary to weight of adoptive birth mothers (.3) is the same for parents generally. important: correlation between children and birth parents (c:bp) of inheritable traits (.3 for weight) is based on 1/2 DNA inheritance; so heritability estimates are doubled to .6 for W, c:bp. //
column13: Weight runs in families for reasons of heritability, not nurture. There are 40% of factors unexplained by heritabililty. But those 40% factors are not controlled, by, say, the same raising (based on studies of unrelated adoptions, where correlation is about zero.) TL: this is a BIG DEAL in understanding why RP emphasizes just the DNA side of explanation for individual differences. There are other factors, but you can't appeal to them in explanation in any easy way (such as "raised in the same home" that "nurture" would be used to reference.
column14: Recent review of studies of 18K traits over 2700 publications including nearly 15M twin pairs: avg heritablity is 50%. All psych traits exhibit substantial heritable influence. (This is the 1st law of behaviorable genetics: all traits are heritable.) // Effect size is a big deal; effect size = variance explained. 5% of variance explained is typical. So sample size can get confidence of a difference being present, but that difference may not be very large difference. (Gender matters in academic peformance measures: explains 1% difference in effect size.)
column15: This is why it is so astounding that 50% of differences between persons are explained by genetic differences between them...this is a huge effect size. This is off the scale 1:small; (eg: classroom size is significant statistically, but effect size is 1%.) 10% variance can be seen with naked eye; parental achievement explains 10% of children academic performance. Large effect: 25%. there are very few such (general intelligence is 25% coorrelated with educational achievement.) 50% heritability is off the scale.
column16: Ch 3: Lots of things that look environmental are in fact heritable. Divorce: .15 and .55 for fraternal vs identical twins raised in adoptive homes. TV watching: .15 and .3 for nonrelated adoptive children in same home, vs nonadoptive children in same home: 1/2 of the tendency to watch TV that is thought to be an environmental factor is in fact a heriditary factor. Bottom line for RC: can't build culture and education well, without genetic information. Can't address tribalism & echo chambering without investigating and (results pending) possibly addressing genetic factors. As we need personalized genetic medicine, and education, so we are likely to need personalized genetic news consumption.  As genealogy has become genetically interwoven (and soon genetically overtaken with AI assist), so does looking forward and planning admit of such development. Q: pros and cons of availability of genetic information for such purpose? Safety/security/privacy? (Aside on education: what good is intelligent tutoring, without personalization through DNA? Attacking perhaps half the problem without it?)
column18: The nature of nurture: about half of the correlation between environment and behavior is explained by genetics. This is beyond the direct correlation between genetics and behavior (about .5 on average.) Genetics may correlate directly tendency to study (my example.) But how much TV you watch also correlates, so that genetics + TV time correlate more highly than just genetics. But suppose also that genetics correlates significantly with TV watching time (which it does to a significant extent.) Then genetics accounts in two ways for the "genetics + TV watching" variable, and thus more highly with time spent in study than just the genetics factor directly (aside from TV watching.) LOOK FOR CONFIRMATION OF THIS UNDERSTANDING. As a general rule, assume that half of environmental effects are explained genetically. This "nature of nurture" looks at an active model of experience, not something that happens to us. In that sense, we are a walking self-fulfilling prophecy: we impact what impacts us to impact us. We "use the environment to get what our DNA blueprint whispers that it wants".  (Personality, psychopathology and cognitive abilities: three dimensions of our impact on our environment.)
column20: Ch. 4: DNA matters more as time goes by. No psych trait grows less dependent. But cognitive ability grows remarkably dependent. G, General cognitive ability, reflects the liklihood that dimensions of intelligence tend to cluster together: got one, got others. Gentic differences in fact account for about half of intelligence. But this increases over time. (RP tells story of invite to Soviet Union to study impact of genetics in preschool learning: hypoth was, education would eleminate these genetic differences over time. Instead, (louisville twins study) showed that inheritability (500 twins, 14x assessed) showed ID twins correlation incrased from .75 to .85. Nonidentical twins became less simlar over time, from .65 to .55.) Suggests increasing heritablility from .2 in infancy to .6 in adolescence. But 500 pairs were not enough. But CAP provded the needed power. .1:.2:.3 infancy:childhood:adolescent correlation with parents. Also with biological parents who didn't raise their children. By age 16, correlation to bio+nurturing vs bio only parents achieved parity. Astonishing. How about correlation between adoptive only parents and nonbio children (again, re/ intelligence)? About zero.
column22: Increasing heritablity continues through adulthood to about 80 heritability at age 65. (You can't help bcoming your parents: true re/ intelligence -- TL. ) But there is no corresponding increase in heritable school achievement, which is about 60% across age ranges, even though it correlates with intelligence, which does become more heritalbe over time. What gives? One possibility, univ education pushes out differences of environmental disparity, whereas intelligence is not taught. The effects of genes changes over time (eg, baldness, or schitzophrenia.) Genetic effects on intelligence from age to age remains about the same. So heritability of intelligence over time get magnified as time goes by...snowballing, "genetic amplification" (eg, high intelligence pushes study over time. "We grow into our genes."
column24: Ch 5: abnormal is normal. Psychopathologies are in fact on a spectrum, not a single thing with a cause (deeply engrained in psychiatry.) Medical model is in fact all wrong. There are no genes for any psych disorders. We all have many of the genes that produce any given behavior (disorders or otherwise.) The question is how many. There are no disorders; just extremes of qualitative dimensions of behavior. Dyslexia given as examples, ADHD, dyscalculea. Multivariate genetic analyses show that the same genese are reponsible for heritaility throughout the normal distribution. (MGA: take one twin with diagnosis, correlate with the other twin's behavior, etc.) Disorders are just extremes of same inherited factors as support normal dimensions. Abnormal is normal. Changes everything in clinical psychology. OGOD hyp: one gene, one disorder. Generally false. Huntington's is a counterexample. Gene is necessary and sufficient for the disease. Genetic effect is qualitative, not quantitative: there are thousands of these, but they are all rare. No common disorder works that way: OGOD is false for all common disorders.
column26: Abnormal behavior is genetically normal. There are no qualitatve, disorders, only quantitiative dimensions of behaviors. No sense in trying to find whether or not you have any disorder. Use "dimensions" rather than "disorders". Can only change degree of unwanted dimensions. Spectra disorders. "On the spectra" reflects this. But there is a more radical claim here: there is no distinction between the abnormal v normal. Eg height. No "almost a giant" diagnostic category makes sense. Nowhere else, either, not even schizophrenia, autism, etc. Dyslexia, etc.)
column27: Ch. 6: generalist genes. Genetic causal diagnosis, rather than symptoms-based diagnoses, are possible. But genetic effects tend to be general. Inherited genetics tend to be displayed in multiple disorders, so that eg generalized anxiety and major depressive disorder are genetically the same. Genes put you in that space, and environmental factors determine which way you go (anxious or depressive.) 3 broad genetic clusters of genetic roots of phsychopathology (internalizing problems, externalizing problems, and psychotic behaviors.) Within these clusters, correlations are .5 in order. But quite often these effects fall into clusters of pathologies. Even the three clusters admit of further clustering into a general psychopathology cluster. So symptoms don't help much at getting to genetic causes. // Cognitive abilities also run in general genetic clustering, like psychopathologies. If one gene is associated with one cog ability, most are general to all cognitive abilities. So general cognitive ability is a powerful construct. Education-related skills correlate even more highly, around .7. Good at one aspect of education, likely good at the others too. Eg: genetic correlation between reading real words and nonwords (sounding out the unfamiliar) is .9: if you can do one well, you can do the other. Same dna differences are responsible for both fluency and phonetics, though these are separate cognitive processes. Spacial ability, too. 12 different spacial tests: correlation .8. // Generalist genes seem to have diffuse effects in the brain, rather than traditional modularity re/ structure and function. There are 1000s of genes involved in genetic influence; and any given gene impacts many behaviors:  polygenicity and polytropy, 2 great principles of genetics. Generalist genese result in generalist brains. Generalist genes are (2018) way understudied in neuroscience. The DNA revoltuion will change this. Genes to brains to behavior all will be impacted by the generalist genes approach.
column30: Ch. 7: why childen in the same family are so different. 2 of 5 big findings are about environment: 1) nature of nurture, genetic basis of environmental influences; 2)___________  (siblings correlate .4 for intelligence. .09 for schizo. nurture was used to explain similarities, but left unaddressed for differences. avg IQ difference for sibs: 13. At random in population: 17. Not hugely different. So nurture in the traditional (nongentic) sense only explains so much by way of differences in intelligence. Bill and Roger Clinton, eg.) // Things run in families primarily due to nature (.5), meaning the other half is other factors. Growing up in the same family does not make you resemble them beyond your genetic similarity. We would be just as similar to parents and sibs if we had been adopted and raised by someone else. Adoption research shows this. // Moreover, this research implies that family environmental factors do not explain the differences at all, any more than weight. Twin studies show this, agreeing with adoption studies. Yes, there is .5 nongenetic factors. But these show no correlation with growing up in the same family. (TL: it would seem to follow that loving your children well makes no difference in how they grow up. But that's an incorrect conclusion: loving your children might be genetically influenced. Or that we lack the tools to pick out, operationalize, love. Or both.) Years of studies seem to indicate that shared environmental identifiers have about zero impact in populations at large. Nonshared env factors seem (after three decades of study) to be matters of chance, and not generally of lasting (but rather of temporary) consequence, though many exceptions (war, great fortune--Darwin making the cut on the Beagle in spite of his nose shape just barely passing the captain's phrenological inspection--and the like. Even here, the nature of nurture qualifies all of these nonshared env factors. Even true for 'agreableness" = kindness, altruism, conscienciousness, etc.: No lasting (subject to i:c:a:a aging qualifier) impact of shared environment in this area. Again, all subject to what is, rather than what could be qualifier. Mostly, abuse doesn't make a big difference, cause it mostly doesn't happen. Same (TL) with love of your children: it mostly does.
column33: Ch. 8: the DNA blueprint. Intro: test of phonemic awareness is .7 heritable...not based on how well students are taught reading.  Here, repetition of "dna makes all the difference" claims, detailed re/ education. Interesting point: the ONLY exception to shared env factors making a substantial difference is religious and political beliefs, with a 20% correlation, subject still to nature of nurture analysis. (TL: evolutionary Q:why is that? We've evolved to shape culture (R&P): why? Does it explain differences in cultures, communities, if not individuals? Even so, there is only a moderate (sparse?) correlation... // Parents, and schools, and life experiences matter, but they don't make a difference. You'll come out roughly the same over the long run...heritability of life experiences is about 25%, but the rest of environmental impacts are unsystematic and unstable.

column5: Ch. 10 Nice summary of DNA basics. New to me: how much more is known about Intron regions as loci of gene regulations, illustrated with FTO gene. Polytropism and polygenicity complicate mechanisms and interventions, esp re/ genetic background of behavioral traits. 80M SNPs in the human genome, 10M in any one population (Great Britain), 4 M in a person.  Most in Introns, not extrons where genetic code is stored and from which it is replicated. 6 B (over two genomes) nucleotides in our genome (mapped by whole genome sequencing). 99% identical across individuals, leaving 3M rungs that individuate us. All we inherit is the genome sequence. (True? no epigenetic inheritance?)
column6: Now snp microarrays are a shortcut (snp chips): postage-stamp size mapper of snps...need not do them all...many are inherited together as a package. a few 100 thousand snp samples can tell us most of the differences between us. Only common alleles, now cheap. Now 3 days and $7 for a recent technique (https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-020-62518-6 March 2020.) Genome-wide association studies use SNP chips to scan a whole genome for a number of SNPs (100-2000, now) assocated with the most prominent genese associated with a particular psychological trait (or biological/pathological, no doubt.) This genome-wide association study possibility kicked off the DNA Revolution. (TL: now you can use snp chips to ask genomes about specific macro properties, and they will answer economically and reasonably quickly.)
column8: Ch. 11 Gene hunting. long and fascinating account of 25 years of hunting for the wrong (few genes, big impact) quarry. In fact, no gene impacts behavior at more than 1% correlation level, and most are less than .1%, meaning thousands of genes (sometimes hundreds of thousands) need to be added in polygenic scores to reveal any significant correlation with behavioral traits. Thousands of individuals are not enough to reveal these; 10s and 100s of thousands of individuals must be studied and compared in light of 10s of thousands of traits in order to approach a detailed explanation for the genetic impacts visiible through adoption and twin studies.
column10: Ch. 12. The dna fortune teller. main repository; genome-wide summary statistics for 173 traits, based on 1.5 million individuals, 1.4B Snp trait associations. just the beginning of the era. 200 traits now, hundreds of others coming, based on bigger studies will produce more powerful polygenic scores. polygenic scores are significant, though very few of the snps included are themselves statistically significant. Enter the age of individualized behavioral genetics. RP illustrates in this chapter with his own traits.
column11: Ch. 13; predicting who we are. Tide is turned with polygenic scores, looking at some of the best (schizo, bipolar, and depressive disorders.) Doubling sample size for bipolar increased power from 3 to 10%, the most powerful predictive tool in psych for bipolarity. 40K case studies for each are coming on line. To know, or not to know questions examined. Arg for individual predication, understood probabilistically. Illustrated with Alzheimer gene. polygenic scores go beyond avg family risk to indiv risk. high polyg scores for schizo tend to fall in creative professions: focus on the bright side. Alz (apo-e allele 4 is 80% predictive.) head injury is the 1 env factor increasing risk for Alz...don't injure your head. yrs of education; 1000 sig snp associations, predicts more than 10 percent of ed attainment.

column5: https://www.thegospelcoalition.org/blogs/trevin-wax/expressive-individualism-what-is-it/ That’s a nice primer on expressive individualism. MacIntyre discusses it at length in his ethics and the conflicts of modernity (2016). The direction I am taking in the book is not so hard on expressive individualism...I think in the main it is correct, or rather a legitimate beginning, but needs some further development in a way that harmonizes it with MacIntyre and other contemporary critics. // I’ll check out Shapiro on Sneed.
column7: https://www.elevenjournals.com/tijdschrift/rechtsfilosofieentheorie/2018/1/NJLP_2213-0713_2018_047_001_006 Here’s a nice brief review of M’s Ethics and the culture of modernity that focuses on the issue of expressionism (=expressive individualism) and contrasts it with M’s teleological approach. My approach is that taken highly, each of them leads to the other, and neither can stand alone as articulated/defended thus far.
column9: https://ndpr.nd.edu/news/ethics-in-the-conflicts-of-modernity-an-essay-on-desire-practical-reasoning-and-narrative/    And if you want one further step into the debate, here’s another review of M’s ECM, by a very good philosopher (Richard Kraut) who does not (by his own admission) understand MacIntyre. But he sets the issue up around which he doesn’t understand him quite well. He finds a fork in the road to interpret M, and then guesses wrongly at the fork. M thinks that the development of one’s powers is good for you, and that this is so is independent of your current desires. (And that is all correct.) What M does not do (does not see, I would say) is that there is an adequately-informed expressivist foundation for what does and does not constitute one’s developed powers, one’s telos, one’s own flourishing qua human being. He’s got no account of this, or rather, has one but doesn’t see it. What I’m doing in the Resurrecting Civility project is providing an account of truth and goodness that is fully expressivist, and fully teleological. Neither concept, I think I can show, is coherent without the other. And there is a new science about to emerge, a science of both, a computational natural law approach, where it is the information, individually and socially processed in rich communities, that tunes the expressive desires of its mature members into stable preferences that are the best current theory of how human beings in fact flourish; the stability is the key idea here. That stability is an empirical question, discerned under appropriate testing conditions, which M has himself already spelled out but not quite recognized as the foundation for a new empirical political, master science.
column14: So here, just sketched, is the background against which to understand Sneed’s book. (Actually, it’s the context for understanding every book on every subject, if the master science argument holds. That “master science” argument goes like this: it is all of human inquiry, theoretical and practical, that forms and informs a person. Communities that aim toward complete accounts of the world and how to live in it are the only communities that can produce stable opinion and desire--expressive desire. There are many rival ways to do this, currently almost none of them tried. (PoP is becoming an exception, and there are others.) But until such communities flourish, and until M’s algorithm for profitable interaction between such communities gets run, gets put into practice; that is, until the experiment is set up and run, then we are blind to the stable outcomes that might emerge from such interaction. From this inadequate perspective, no person’s desires are sufficiently informed, stable, or normative. None give data that support the empirical inquiry. So our task now is to lay out the requirements of a high-information-flow network of rich communities, develop them, and begin to run the experiment through their meaningful interactions. What emerges from these fully interdisciplinary accounts of human life is the data of an empirical science of human behavior, a computational natural law. This is a new master science of what Aristotle called Politics and Aquinas (arguing that a Christian Aristotelianism was then the current best prospect) called Theology.

column5: So, get all these numbers right. NC = ~150k cortical columns, each the size of a small grain of rice, each containing 100k neurons of dozens of different types (neurons, and what else?). Columns are columnated largely by connections that they have; they are otherwise hard to differentiate, although of the more than 2 dozen broader functional areas of the NC, there are a few distributed different kinds of neurons in some of them. Still, on the whole the Mountcastle (and now Hawkins/Numenta) hypothesis is that every column operates on roughly the same algoritm, and that distinctions are a function primarily of the wiring of the connectome. Each column a total of 0.5B interconnections (intra, or inter-across columns?), and a few kilometers of axon/dendrite wiring (and synapses, 1:1 with interconnections, so .5 B?), all in a 2mm thick dinner napkin-sized, roughly uniform in appearance but for folds, separated as to function by wiring patterns but not so much by any other structural differentiation, comprising 100 such minicolumns per square millimeter of NC area, i.e., per cortical column.

column5: The entire brain predicts; JH has an account of what prediction looks like neurologically, and it ties back nicely (in theory) with  MWMBNBM constant justification theory...come back to this here
column6: The brain's (NC) universal predictive role is based on a dynamic reference frame algorithm that seems rooted in the entorhinal cortex and the hippocampus, said to be a finger-sized midbrain mirrored structure near the base of each hemisphere.  Now we know that this algorythm taps place cells and grid cells. (Define each.) These support intentional motion, and prediction, which are 2 sides of the same coin, and which together form models of our world. Now add place cells for abstract concepts and grid cells (mathematics, logic) interconnecting them, and abstract models arise.
column7: JH introduces it as a fundamental principle of neuroscience that thought is brain activity (get his exact phrasing right, and put it here: ____________). On a pCRm worldview, this seems to go both ways: brain activity is thought, also. Everything is. Brain activity is the way we model the activity of thought under limited perspective; it is how under our limited perspective we make our way forward in interacting with other limited perspectives, all the while preserving and maximizing the efficacy of the relations with other pespectives that we encounter under our own interacting limited pespective. The logical relations describing these perspectival interactions are modeled by us as brain activity, and at many other interactive levels of analogy. The causal relationships studied in neuroscience and every other science are part of the constraints that need to be satisfied by pCRm or any other worldview
column9: meeting numbers of contraints is what renders likely any scientific theory: JH (roughly interpreted) on how he could be certain that the reference frame algorithm was correct, even before empirical testing, how it could go on to specifically predict. Rethink solved problems as contraints, in the problem/resource/solution approach.
column10: Attempt to summarize the 1000 brains theory (1st 1/3 of JH's book): brains build models of the world through movement, driving interaction; those models are used to generate predictions that are tested against subsequent input. (Importantly, the priming of neurons--residing in the 80? 90%/ of neurons whose functionalty had not otherwise been understood as involved in processing of input--lowers the threshold for confirmatory signaling. This seems, on the face of it, to predict what Mistakes Were Made BNBM authors describe at the brain's constant preoccupation with justifying what it already expects to happen. Find out how close a connection this predictive mechanism described in 1000B is to this self-justifying preoccupation described in MWMBNBM.) Brains build multiple models using their 150k independend model constructors--note that in 1000B JH does NOT DISCUSS THE IMPORTANT ROLE OF SPARSENESS or quantify how many (or did I miss it) of these C columns are typically involved. Where is spareseness: in the number of columns activiated, or in the number of neurons per column involved, or both? Over what kinds of activities might these ratios vary?) So these identifications of 1) a common NC algorithm (Mountcastle?); 2) across multiple models simultaneously processing in multiple independent NCC units; 3) utilizing reference frames inherited and modified from the old brain (ERC and HC); and 4) resolving multiple models through a voting process which at least in part can be underststood as a simple application of process-of-elimination logic; all this describes intelligent activity largely rooted in the NC, the intelligent 70% of the human brain.
column13: Part II of JH's text argues that existing AI are not intellegent because they cannot flexibly extend models to new situations, but that creation of truly intelligent machines is possible along the lines of replication of the many-active-models-with-motive-prediction approach taken in the NC. These AI will be possible, but not sinister: the real alignment problem is a human-to-human alignment problem, not AI to human (my phrasing of JH's point.) We should watch and regulate, but not over worry about, the emergence of intelligent AI. These will impact our future in ways which we cannot predict (as we could not the computer revolution), but will transform human life along the radical but recognizable trajectory that computing already has. This real AI will be conscious iff they are truly AI: if they store and have immediate access to memory of past successful modeling experiences,

column4: Design thinking and movement through epistemological crisis. Driving paradigm change purposively, rather than accidentally. How might we intentionally align what we learn in one field of study with the new direction we are seeking another?
On senses of natural law in MacIntyre, reflecting on M's concluding essay in Intractable Disputes About the Natural Law (Cunningham, ed.)
column5: M: it is in fact a revealed truth that the 1st Ps of the natural law can be known prior to and independently of any revealed truth, including this one. (IDNL p. 341.) Primary precepts (aligned with decalogue, p 31.) can be derived from 1st Principles with "only minimal reflection" (so M says he and Porter agree.) Also, 1st Ps of the natural law are (or only can be?) known noninferentially, though they are not per se nota (p. 318.) Further, judgements in particular cases can be derived from primary precepts through arguments that are sound in tradition-independent ways, as tradition-independent as are the primary precepts (p. 320.) Put all that together: there are T-independent rational standards of moral conduct that (all the way down to judgements in particular cases, such as instances of murder, and, elsewhere, p. 339-340, that abortion violates our primary duty to nurture prospective rational co-deliberators) can be known also Revelation-independently.
column7: In my terms: there are problem-resource-solution sets that can be grasped by multiple traditions. Problem: how to guide actions in treating one another; resources: 1st Ps of natural law, general rules/primary precepts and immediate consequences (because this is a rational agent with whom I must deliberate to arrive at more richly informed decisions, I should not kill but work with all such agents); solution: follow the dictates of adequately informed practical reason to become the kind of person formed to make such decisions (such as not to kill this person in these circumstances) well. (M in IDNL, p. 317: "Adequately informed and directed practical reason is all that we need." (to determine whether our habits are what we need them to be, through reflection.) This is a triplet, a lens, graspable independently of traditions (though more easily graspable in supportive and informative--rich--traditions, since grasping these conclusions requires freedom from error from many and hard-to-remove sources, such as misconceiving of one's final end, and in consequence missing the importance of means necessary to achieve it; or of attaching too much importance to particular goods, and in consequence misperceiving the final end, and yet other sources of error, such as confusing means with ends (such as particular goods productive of happiness, with that final end itself, philosophical mistakes that can be hard to root out, IDNL, p. 336-337.)
column11: it might be that stably grasping some lenses, and it seems to be in fact with that "natural moral law" lens, that a stable grasp of other lenses (such as that something is a revealed truth depends on grasping 1st principles of reason, including practical reason: M in IDNL, p. 341) depends on grasping prior lenses. This is a kind of coherentism (inside the lenses) inside a foundationalist framework (primacy of stably grasping--which may require character formation necessary to avoid certain errors, implies M on Flannery in IDNL p. 340ff--some lenses before others. So this hierarchy-of-model-construction (lens construction) framework seems to be both coherentist and foundationalist, in different respects. There seem to be both coherentist and foundationalist elements at work both in model construction and in model reinforcement.
column13: So what is the natural law? Any propogated, intelligible order. There is such order re/ out treatment of the world around us, that is, a practical intelligible order, including all that we think of as the moral life. M gives a set of lenses, a model, of the primary and easily derived features of that order, intelligible by any human being who's mind is properly functioning and thus a rational agent, concerning the moral law. Mistaken grasp of that order is easy, so richer traditions bring aids to grasping that foundational core, but that lens in itself can be grasped in tradition-independent ways (it can achieve a kind of stability not nested in and dependent on the stability by which the whole of a tradition's model, a whole world view, is secured.) Yet there are philosophical and moral impediments to reasoning adequately in the moral life that perhaps are nearly always avoided with additional helpful features of traditions richer in information-propagating mechanisms (including moral/habit/character formation). So an adherent of any T incorporating and affirming the fundamental moral lens (recognition of the question how must I live as problem, grasp of 1st Ps, shortly derived precepts, as resources, and some paradigmmatic instances of application in context as solutions and, as paradigmatic, further resources resources) can participate in practical reasoning with some degree of success. That moral lens, by itself, has some pull, and that pull may be explored as a rhetorical strategy for attracting persons from some Ts (those without the moral lens) to others (those with.)
column17: But acceptance of the pull of that natural law lens does depend on other features of traditions, such as the resources to cultivate adequate habits and avoid certain philosophical mistakes. The pull of the natural law lens must be added to other forces to produce an all-things-considered appealing whole. (One could imagine a person thinking that accepting the natural law lens is constricting and incompatible with human freedom and thus propserity, and so having both a seemingly rational and moral resistance to accepting that lens and any traditions which incorporate it...perhaps a person must jettison some rationally-appealing resources, including the moral lens, to achieve some better overall balance of resources.)
column19: So it seems that a fuller defense of the rational justification of the moral life would have to outstrip, in some respects, the defense that could be mounted on first principles of the natural law and closely-derivable consequences, that is, beyond the rational defense for for accepting the moral law. This might be maintained without denying the pull, the rational justification, of accepting the smaller lens in itself and by itself. It is just that such rational justification can be challenged, and when it is, some deeper appeal to the natural law, beyond the rational justification of the moral lens, might be required. And that appeal will be to principles that are not tradition-independent, but rather wholistic and tradition-bound.
column21: So in that context, any argument for particular moral stances, such as requiring respect for the sanctity of human life, will both have to include considerations from the moral law, and yet go beyond those considerations. Let us call an argument which is such that being able to reproduce it to the satisfaction of everyone else able to produce it, produces acceptance of its conclusion that no further consideration of additional, currently available considerations could unseat, a full justification for a conclusion. Such full justifications would be time-indexed, and person-indexed, amounting to the best arguments so far available for a given conclusion for a given person. It is possible that some argument at a given time may function as a full argument for every What follows is an outline for a full justification of the sanctity of human life.
column23: (Note that to time-index an effect (including being able to reproduce an argument that persuades you) in fact locates it in a tradition; one's past picks us out uniquely in a set of circumstances. In fact, some kinds of arguments can only be made by mature members of a given tradition, so the reproducability requirement--if it is a challenging, full-bodied argument--picks out mature members of traditions, in some circumstances. (Those that aren't such, won't be ones in which full justifications are made.) Call a full justification for everyone--one that persuades everyone who can make it to accept its conclusion) a universal full justification for a claim.
column25: What are arguments, anyway? A set of relationally-ordered propositions that induce acceptance of the last of them? In a CRm world, perhaps acceptance is all there is to logic. Some relations just persuade? Problem with that interpretation: nothing drives us to the mistakes we make, which seems to be required by the nature of love. No one would make these mistakes on purpose, who loved. But awareness of a systematic unfolding of logical possibilities seems compatible with love...as long as one learns from them as quickly as possible. But then a problem: why suppose logic persuades? Perhaps because logical relations are part of the nature of consciousness, part of God's nature, and thus part of ours, or of God's, as picked out by some limited perspective (who is each of us.)

column6: In the 5th century AD this rejection of abortion at every stage was affirmed by the great bishop-theologian St. Augustine. He knew of theories about the human soul not being present until some weeks into pregnancy. Because he used the Greek Septuagint translation of the Old Testament, he also thought the ancient Israelites had imposed a more severe penalty for accidentally causing a miscarriage if the fetus was "fully formed" (Exodus 21: 22-23), language not found in any known Hebrew version of this passage. But he also held that human knowledge of biology was very limited, and he wisely warned against misusing such theories to risk committing homicide. He added that God has the power to make up all human deficiencies or lack of development in the Resurrection, so we cannot assume that the earliest aborted children will be excluded from enjoying eternal life with God. (in Respect for Unborn Life page, cell C this row)

Reflections on Fredrickson's love 2.0, first three chapters. She offers a new paradigm on love, in which love is treated as an emotion with, like all emotions, a fairly fleeting biological basis. But that basis is communal, something like reflexive positivity (check on her exact repeated phrase here), and she seems to want to define love not just around that shared experience as a focal meaning, but as the core and exclusive meaning, with all kinds of other positivity dimensions revolving around it.
column5: And indeed this makes sense, from her perspective. She is trying to build from that core emotion, with its physical basis, remarkably a shared physical basis, and build up from that, toward an account of loving maturity with a very strong physical basis in Weezle tone, oxytocin optimal functionality, and indeed a set of habits built up around the cultivation of the score emotion (as if it were were the metaclorian live). The account she gives of maturity in this respect looks a lot like loving Christian maturity, and perhaps also a lot like maturity in some Buddhist mindfulness practices centered around the cultivation of loving kindness. But she seems to have failed to consider, and measure, what I admittedly have not measured, but I have considered. Living in love, in a constant relationship with another person who is reaching out and touching us even as we are reaching out and touching the other person, communicating with us, in and through that Netwerk of cultivated loving kindness that looks to her like maturity. But that Netwerk can be perceived as a person, related to as a person, and when it is, the question is, can you measure that effect. Can you, to use her language, be loving even when other human beings are not around to engage in these fleeting moments of mutual positivity that she calls love.
column6: But I should be careful here as shown in a Christian reader. She's arguing for a valuable new paradigm, and we could quite quickly and prematurely incorporated into something that looks more familiar, without first understanding the full benefits of the full shift to the paradigm she advances. That said, I don't yet see any reason not to except everything she said, but to see it as falling short of yet another dimension of loving mutuality with a person who is constantly interacting with us, and perceived to be so, and thus generating (admittedly not in the normal case, but in the mature, practiced case, Or perhaps in the very simple and faithful case, an actual experience of personal exchange when the only person in the room seems to us like a solitary human being, even ourselves.
column7: What is pretty plain is that no account of psychological maturity, such as I imagined advancing in toward a full theory of self-esteem, could be responsibly advanced without taking into account the interactive biology of the sort that Fredrickson is summarizing, and in her lab and .then advancing.

It is precisely our joy at elements of experience and their unity in the boundless human prospect that grounds this sense of value, and the corresponding respect for the kind of thing both we and other human beings are. There is potential for beauty in every life journey. We imagine it so in other forms of life, but know it ever-more richly with those whose natures supports a common human experience of sharing through our evolved capacity for communication with one another; this ever-growing recognition grounds a special affirmation of human dignity. Yet it is in analogous ways the same respect for every form of life, and in fact in every form of interacting being--all of being--which is shot through with teleological activation. This is so even in the teleological activity we ourselves design, exercising our own teleological functionality which came to us from outside of us, though also through human activity; this is AI dignity. // The concept under investigation, dignity, isn’t catalyzed by the experiences and the meta-cognitive reflection on those experiences that ground it. That affective dimension changes as your appreciation of what it is to be alive changes. As a richer set of possibilities become known to you, you simply do want to be alive more, because pursuing those possibilities is life, is being alive. It’s as if awareness of those possibilities, of what life can be, is in fact being wound up, becoming spring-loaded, toward pursuing those possibilities. Seeing that they are good is being wound up toward them. You only unleash that energy in a particular subset of directions: you visit this national park, and that one, but not this other one; you deliberate (calculate) and choose. But that ever-growing awareness (ever growing, in a healthy relationship to the world, that is), that awareness of what it is to be a living human being, that awareness IS respect for your own life, for yourself; the particular capacities that you further develop by running down some of these roads sit on top, are nested in the context of, that healthy educated awareness, that sense of the dignity of being a human being. The analogy here is with expressive vision, which when it becomes fully informed reaches out to stable modes of inclination, stable so that no additional information makes any difference, enabling us to refer to full information, fully informed inclinations. It just doesn't follow that the stability can be reached in only one way, or that it is simply revelatory of the nature of external reality. And then the question arises, what is that external reality; why not just suppose that it is conscious reality. column4: Cultural exchange that is motivated by aesthetic an altruistic calls is admirable and can be effective in producing a wide range of human goods. That can be so, even when there is plainly lacking a framework within which to order those goods and develop a craft of pursuing them Systematically. Of course, such a framework can be provided and sometimes has been. These sustainable development goals put forward by the blank committee of the United Nations in year here represents one such effort.

While these lack a hierarchy, they do propose a comprehensive and integrated account of a wide range of human goods, and cannon have formed the basis for collaboration across cultures, very much a model of cultural exchange. We could imagine adding to those goals a model of human maturity that implies a kind of hierarchy. Indeed, I have written down such a model proposed for graduate education, it is not hard to articulate a range of skills, capabilities, values, virtues, etc. which are such that any mature member of Academia would noticeably limp, professionally, were any lacking. All such approaches provide a basis for the development of craft, even hinting at the prioritization of some goods over others in terms of developmental acquisition, at least. One could imagine developing communities around the development of those crafts, even communities dedicated to cultural exchange enhanced by such crafts as developed perhaps differently in different cultures. There would be emerging, through such community, craft, and culture, a number of elements that might be seen as productive of wisdom and sufficient for at least a kind of progress. The state here is something like the pre-scientific state which Thomas Kuhn describes,

Were such traditions, each articulating a measurable account of maturity and a craft for producing it with ever greater maturity, to enter into contest with one another, one might imagine something like a Kuhnian science of cultural exchange emerging even at this level. These might amount to cultural revolutions, even scientific cultural revolutions, where for extra cultural, non-scientific reasons one culture emerges as dominant and another left behind.

One could easily imagine such encounters inside the cultural interactions which BOSCO-Uganda fosters. A career full of careful consideration of best practices in achievement of millennium or sustainable development goals, heads full of logic models and proposals made to wealthy western organizations, members of the BOSCO team might vote with their feet, following paths more secular than Acholi or Christian. Weather entered into accidentally or systematically for purposes of measurement, such exchanges could look very much like pivotal episodes in a Kuhnian Scientific revolution. But these would not be scientific exchanges, on McIntyre‘s view of science. That they would not be is so for several reasons.

The first of these is that without a master science defining and ordering subordinate sciences, these subordinate sciences are not truly sciences, not in the fullest sense. It is possible that some may be accidentally near to sciences, so it’s better to say that we cannot know that they are sciences in the absence of a master science.

Second, under any such a master science, there are no extra scientific considerations to which appeal might be made, intentionally or subconsciously, for resolving Epistemological crisis in scientific revolutions. In so far as cultures might be considered as rival master sciences, even if just in incomplete states of development— always the case for any science— then the rationality which governs the interaction between such holistic systems of thought and practice is utterly different and does not allow for appeal to considerations outside of the traditions in conflict: there is no outside.

Third, moving away from less successful to more successful traditions (communities/craft/cultures) Is change but not progress, not without a conception of truth as the Telos of rational inquiry throughout the contesting of rival traditions articulated in and guided by their master sciences. Scientific paradimes under the Cooney and conception cannot aim at falsifiability, because nothing can ever be false, or true. But further reflection on this third consideration leads to a fourth.

What it is to be true on the craft tradition model of rationality is to be characterized by a certain level of stability: whether the reliably successful execution of the aims of persons with unchangeable virtuous character in the order of practical rationality; or the conformity of judgments to considerations lying in some sense outside of the judging mind. (In what sense outside that mind, is in Christian accounts of human nature a question of the meaning of the Incarnation; “but we have the mind of Christ.“) but that stability is something determined in contests that endure through time, and cannot be settled once and for all. Their outcome depends upon the ongoing interaction between traditions; any stability which emerges as a dynamic stability, a stability intention that is ongoing

column5: One prominent SIP model is the one created by Crick and Dodge.37 The occurrence of aggression is explained as the outcome of a continuous social exchange between adolescents and their environments. Crick and Dodge identified six cognitive steps in the decision to aggress. In each stage, adolescents integrate environmental and internal stimuli with cognition from “databases”—memory, acquired rules, and social schemas and knowledge.38 In the SIP model, the person is an active agent who processes information with their existing knowledge, beliefs, and values to make decisions about behavioral choices. Adolescents first encode cues, both in the environment (such as people laughing) and internally (e.g., physiological arousal such as rapid heartbeat). Then adolescents interpret those cues, making intent attributions and evaluating  past performance and what the interactio person or people. Adolescents clarify their goals, generate possible responses, evaluate and select a response, and enact that response.39n means for them and the other
column8: six cognitive steps in SIP: 1) encode cues (environmental and internal); 2) interpret cues (making intent attributions, probability assessments based on past performance, etc); 3) clarify goals; 4) generate possible responses; 5) evaluate and select response; 6) enact response. Sounds very much like aristotelian/thomistic deliberation, say AJ & KM
column9: AJ & KM argue that Thomistic psych should be prepared to meet a minimal adequacy challenge of alignment with correlation studies of human actions (vs acts of humans, natural and not moral acts, which Thomistic Psych is not addressing); here alignment consists of consistency with correlations studies in areas where thomistic psych claims causation. (If causation, then correlation). And SIP generates such studies, as AJ & KM illustrate in the case of SIP studies on agression in this article, but which would seem to apply more broadly.

column4: From an exchange with Gene S on authority: 1. How do we--how does anyone--know, or ever reasonably claim to discern, what the leading of the Holy Spirit is?
column5: If I were to give this a full reply, I'd start by reading Francis on discernment (which I've just found perhaps best explicated in these 14 catecheses.) I've seen a nice very short description of the marks of being in a discerning state in some PoP-generated remarks in preparation for our present consultation, which if I can relocate I'll share; what I recall is that it was a particularly appealing account. (Both, of course, draw a lot on Ignatius.) But to start quickly from scratch, I'd begin with judging a tree by its fruit, and a listing of the fruit of the spirit, and of Paul's long ode to the characteristics of love. And then I'd say that when one is in a flow state focused on love, one is in a discerning state. Part of being in that state--again, drawing on Ignatius--is a refreshed and abiding commitment to submit to love-as-a-person, or in the Christian tradition to love-as-three-persons. Persons who are regularly able to achieve such a state, coupled with some other gifts and experience that might be relevant to the particular area under discernment, would be more reliable guides than most.  Does that seem reasonable to you?
column6: 2. When discernment of those leadings takes place in and through ministry gifts distributed unequally throughout the body, how does that system keep from devolving into (or even ever being more than) a smaller group of people convincing themselves that God is on their side and telling presumably some larger group of others what to do, backed with putative divine authority?
column7: Here the discussion is about checks and balances. ("Test everything.") Processes of discernment must be continually observed and evaluated by a wide range of people with a wide range of gifts and perspectives over a long period of time. This is what it means to judge a tree by its fruit, in a collective context. Insofar as such a system of testing is in place and is robust and continuously improving, one can have greater confidence in the systems of discernment that it is reviewing. Insofar as there is resistance to such review or atrophy in exercising it, confidence in reliability deteriorates (or should, and often does.) This is the state of affairs I see us having devolved to in PoP. I'm not yet convinced that the attempt to discern major common directional questions together under provision of a rich cornucopia of relevant gifts is a non-starter, but it sure can go wrong.
column8: 3. How can individuals effectively stand up and retain their autonomy in the face of such authoritarian pressure?
column9: That "lonely line" in the S & P--and it's not quite alone; there is a whole authority and obedience teaching series in support of it--always meant a great deal to me, and I think that's because I was formed (in this respect) reasonably well: as generally intended by the earlier-mature members of PoP. Hence, 20 years ago I was writing in objection to what I saw as authoritarian tendencies and missteps that were contrary to our intention as a community...which is what I always thought good community members should do.
