column7: Merlin summary: Physicists Challenge the Assumption of Spacetime as Fundamental in Theories of Consciousness 1. Consciousness and Spacetime: - The assumption that spacetime is fundamental in theories of consciousness is challenged by physicists. - Physicists argue that spacetime is not fundamental and is doomed. - Spacetime lacks operational meaning for distances smaller than the Planck length and durations shorter than the Planck time. - Particles colliders are limited in their ability to probe smaller particles due to the effects of gravity. - As the wavelength of radiation decreases, the concentration of mass-energy increases, creating greater curvature in spacetime. 2. Agents and Conscious Experiences: - Conscious agents are proposed as fundamental entities beyond spacetime. - Agents can combine into more complex agents and fuse into simpler agents with new conscious experiences. - The combinations of n agents form an 𝑛(𝑛−1)-dimensional polytope, and the fusions form an (𝑛−1)-dimensional simplex. 3. Spacetime as an Interface: - Spacetime is viewed as an interface for conscious agents. - The dynamics of conscious agents can be represented by decorated permutations. - The dynamics of conscious agents are projected onto spacetime and quantum theory to generate a data structure. 4. Revising Interpretations of Neurons: - Neurons and brains are considered icons in the spacetime interface. - Neurons have no causal powers, contrary to standard interpretations in neuroscience. - Interpretations of neural correlates of consciousness need to be revised. 5. Limitations and Future Directions: - The theory of conscious agents has limitations that need to be acknowledged. - New directions for the development of the theory of conscious agents are suggested. 6. Quantum Theory of Measurement: - Distances and durations below the Planck scale lack operational meaning. - Quantum uncertainty for energy, time, position, and momentum affects measuring devices. - To improve accuracy, degrees of freedom must be added, making the device more massive. - Physicists conclude that spacetime may not be fundamental. 7. Spacetime and Quantum Mechanics: - Physicists argue that spacetime and quantum mechanics may not be fundamental. - A deeper structure is sought to explain the emergence of spacetime and quantum mechanics. - Particles and macroscopic objects are not fundamental if spacetime is not. - Theories of consciousness based on spacetime or particles face challenges. 8. Evolutionary Games and Perception: - Evolution by natural selection does not necessarily lead to perceiving the truth. - Perceiving the truth does not always enhance an organism's fitness. - Our minds evolved to solve life-and-death problems, not to commune with correctness. - Sensory systems guide adaptive action, not truth perception. 9. Counterintuitive Insights from Evolutionary Game Theory: - Studies based on evolutionary game theory show that sensory systems do not reveal truths about objective reality. - Fitness payoffs shape the evolution of sensory systems. - Our senses are not designed to perceive the structures of objective reality. - Middle-sized objects are not exempt from being perceived inaccurately. 10. Interface Theory of Perception: - Our senses provide a user interface to an unknown reality. - Spatiotemporal objects are data structures that guide adaptive action. - Objects are clusters of fitness payoff functions. - Clustering fitness payoffs into objects is a cognitive impenetrable process. 11. Objects are not mind-independent entities: - Objects are data structures that we construct and employ as needed - Objects do not exist when they are not observed 12. Evolution shaped our senses to guide adaptive behavior: - We must take our senses seriously, but not literally - Our senses interact with some reality that exists whether we look or not 13. Neurons and brains do not exist when they are not observed: - We create them when we look and delete them when we look away - Neurons do not create our behaviors or conscious experiences 14. The relationship between neural activity and conscious experience is complex: - We need more funding for neuroscience to understand this relationship - Consciousness emerges from the organization and operation of the brain 15. Theories of primary and secondary qualities: - All qualities are secondary qualities - Qualities of objects only exist when perceived 16. Spacetime and objects are not fundamental reality: - We need to propose a reality beyond spacetime - Consciousness is fundamental and can be modeled as a network of conscious agents 17. Posits about consciousness and the notion of conscious agents: - There are conscious experiences and probabilistic relations among them - A conscious agent has a set of possible experiences and actions - A conscious agent is embedded in a network of other conscious agents 18. Formalization of posits using measurable spaces and Markovian kernels: - Measurable spaces for conscious experiences - Markovian kernels for probabilistic relations among conscious experiences 19. Definition of Conscious Agent: - A conscious agent, C, has a set of potential conscious experiences, X, and a set of potential actions, G. - The agent is influenced by a network of other conscious agents and can influence their experiences as well. - The agent exists within a well-defined set of states, W, known as 'the world.' 20. Markovian Kernels: - When the agent experiences a specific conscious experience x, it chooses an action g using a decision kernel, D. - This action then influences the experiences of the agents in the network W using an action kernel, A. - The conscious experiences of the agent C are influenced by the network W using a perception kernel, P. 21. Conceptual Representation: - The set X represents the potential of the aware subject to have specific experiences. - The 𝜎-algebra 𝒳 represents the conceptual representation of the subject about its potential for experiences. - The set G represents the potential for actions by the aware subject. 22. Constructing Networks of Conscious Agents: - Networks of conscious agents are computationally universal and can model themselves. - Various complex concepts, including learning, memory, problem solving, and a self, can be constructed within these networks. - The combination problem of consciousness and the conception of physical objects within the theory are addressed in the next sections. 23. Combination Problem: - The combination problem of consciousness is one of the most difficult problems in theories where consciousness is fundamental. - Solving this problem will provide insights into the notion of physical objects in spacetime. - The concept of VR headset as an allegory for our immersive experience in spacetime is introduced. 24. Interface Theory of Perception: - The interface theory of perception and the theory of conscious agents are distinct theories. - However, accepting both theories suggests that the distinction between conscious and unconscious objects is not principled. - Our perceptions are interfaces to a network of conscious agents, so we are always interacting with consciousness. 25. Living and Non-Living Objects: - The distinction between living and non-living objects is also not principled but an artifact of our perceptual interface. - Attempts to define life have been inconclusive, as the limitations of our interface limit our insights into conscious agents. 26. Doubting our evolved capacity for judging others: - If we doubt the testimony of our senses, we should doubt our capacity for judging others' emotions. - Our hardwired capacity to judge emotions is evolved for survival, not truth. 27. The reality beyond our user interfaces: - Our senses are interfaces to a network of conscious agents. - This reality goes beyond the theory of evolution by natural selection. 28. The problem of combining conscious subjects: - Can conscious subjects combine to form new conscious subjects? - Can conscious experiences combine to form new conscious experiences? 29. William James' combination problem of consciousness: - James raised the question of how individual feelings can form a new consciousness. - He argued that individual feelings remain separate and do not create a higher compound mind. 30. Two mathematical approaches to the combination problem: - The theory proposes combination and fusion as mathematical approaches. - These approaches study interactions among agents and how they affect experiences. 31. The qualia kernel and conscious experience: - The qualia kernel represents the relation conscious experience has to itself. - It accounts for the 'what-it-is-likeness' of conscious experience. 32. Agents with distinct qualia: - An agent with n distinct qualia is called an n-agent. - Qualia can differ from one n-agent to another. 33. Interaction and combination of qualia kernels: - The qualia kernels of two agents can interact and combine. - The combination is represented by a 2x2 matrix of conditional probabilities. 34. Definition of a single conscious agent: - The 6-tuple 𝐶1×𝐶2=(𝑋1×𝑋2,𝐺1×𝐺2,𝑊1×𝑊2,𝑃1⊗𝑃2,𝐷1⊗𝐷2,𝐴1⊗𝐴2) satisfies the definition of being a single conscious agent. - The Markovian kernel 𝑃1⊗𝑃2:𝑊1×𝑊2→𝑋1×𝑋2 is defined by 𝑃1⊗𝑃2(𝑤1,𝑤2;𝑑𝑥1,𝑑𝑥2)=𝑃1(𝑤1;𝑑𝑥1)𝑃2(𝑤2;𝑑𝑥2). 𝐷1⊗𝐷2:𝑋1×𝑋2→𝐺1×𝐺2 and 𝐴1⊗𝐴2:𝐺1×𝐺2→𝑊1×𝑊2 are defined similarly. - This juxtaposition of two simple agents leads to a more complex agent. 35. Combining non-interacting agents: - When agents 𝐶1 and 𝐶2 do not have any cross terms (interaction), they are considered non-interacting. - Even though they do not interact, a pair of such non-interacting agents can be thought of as a single (combined) agent. - The new agent has a simple Q kernel, namely 𝑄1⊗𝑄2, which has a block-diagonal form. 36. Combining and fusing agents: - Two agents can combine into a more complex agent with cross terms that describe their interaction. - The set of kernel cross terms can be represented by points in the Markov polytope, ℳ2. - Two consecutive interactions can be described by the kernel 𝑄2. - By finding the derivative of 𝑄2 with respect to a discrete-step parameter, we can determine the kernel flows in ℳ2. 37. The fusion of agents and creation of new qualia: - Along the line 𝑦=1−𝑥 in the Markov polytope, kernels drop from rank 2 to rank 1, signaling the fusion of agents and creation of new qualia. - Each kernel has a unique combination of colors that it leaves invariant, represented by a probability measure 𝜇. - Kernels with the same invariant measure lie on a straight line passing through the origin. 38. Invariant Measures and Stationary Kernels: - The figure shows the mix of red and green in the invariant measure for the kernel associated to each point - The diagonal line represents the stationary kernels, which satisfy a certain equation 39. Fusion of Agents: - When kernels reach the line of stationary kernels, they drop from rank 2 to rank 1 and fuse into single agents - The fused agents form a one-parameter family with a new quale 40. Possible Fusions of Conscious Agents: - The possible fusions of n conscious agents form a unit (n-1)-simplex - Each fusion corresponds to a possible stationary kernel 41. Ultimate One Consciousness: - The theory of conscious agents does not start with a theory of the Ultimate One Consciousness - Instead, it starts with a countable infinity of agents and explores combinations and fusions 42. Spacetime and Decorated Permutations: - The theory of conscious agents aims to project the dynamics of agents onto spacetime - This projection involves structures called "decorated permutations" 43. Structures Beyond Spacetime: - Physics has found structures beyond spacetime, such as the amplituhedron and decorated permutations - These structures arise from a deeper theory of conscious agents 44. The Amplituhedron: - The amplituhedron is a geometric object that lies beyond spacetime - It is obtained by computing the volume of amplituhedra, which are faces of an infinite-dimensional master amplituhedron 45. Positive Grassmannian and Decorated Permutations: - The positive Grassmannian, which describes the invariant physical content, can be represented by "decorated permutations" - Decorated permutations are mappings generated from ordinary permutations 46. Definition of a Decorated Permutation: - A decorated permutation is a mapping that satisfies certain conditions. - It is an injective mapping and a decoration of an ordinary permutation. 47. Representation of Decorated Permutations: - Decorated permutations can be represented by plabic graphs or on-shell diagrams. - These diagrams help in reading off the permutation. 48. Decomposing Decorated Permutations: - Decorated permutations can be decomposed into a sequence of adjacent transpositions. - This decomposition leads to a minimal on-shell diagram. 49. Recovering Decorated Permutations from Diagrams: - All consistent decorated permutations can be recovered from the diagram using specific rules. - The rules involve following paths in the diagram. 50. Connection to Structures Beyond Spacetime: - The theory of conscious agents should connect with structures beyond spacetime. - The associahedron and cosmological polytope are important structures in this context. 51. Insights from Physics: - The decorated permutation is the deepest structure beyond spacetime in physics. - Differential forms on on-shell diagrams give rise to scattering amplitudes. 52. Target for the Theory of Conscious Agents: - The Markov-chain dynamics of the theory of conscious agents should map to decorated permutations and spins. - This allows for a correspondence between agent dynamics and on-shell diagrams. 53. Correspondence between Agent Dynamics and Physical Particles: - The map from Markov chains to decorated permutations is still an open problem. - It is a key area of research connecting agent dynamics and on-shell diagrams. 54. Recurrence and Transience: - A state in a Markov chain is recurrent if it has a positive probability of returning to itself infinitely often. - If a state is not recurrent, it is transient, and the probability of its returning to itself infinitely often is zero. 55. Markov Decorated Permutations: - Given a Markov kernel on 𝑛 states, we can define a decorated permutation 𝜎 of the states. - For transient states, 𝜎(𝑎) is 𝑎 itself, while for recurrent states, 𝜎(𝑎) is the first element 𝑏 greater than 𝑎 that includes the communicating class [𝑎]. 56. Decorated Permutations of Arbitrary Graphs: - We can extend the concept of decorated permutations to arbitrary graphs. - For nodes with no links, 𝜎(𝑎) is 𝑎, for nodes linking only with itself, 𝜎(𝑎) is 𝑎+𝑛, and for other nodes, 𝜎(𝑎) is the first element 𝑏 greater than 𝑎 that includes all nodes in the strongly connected component of the graph containing 𝑎. 57. The Markov Polytope: - The Markov polytope 𝑀𝑛 is a cell complex that represents all possible Markov kernels. - Each cell 𝑆𝜎 corresponds to a Markov kernel that gives rise to the decorated permutation 𝜎. 58. Decorated Permutations of Subspaces: - Given a matrix representing an element of the positive Grassmannian, we can generate a decorated permutation 𝜎𝐶. - This decorated permutation corresponds to a unique subspace of ℝ𝑛 and can also encode the communicating class of a Markov chain. 59. Agent-Particle Correspondence Conjecture: - There is a conjectured correspondence between the dynamics of conscious agents and the dynamics of particle scattering in spacetime. - This correspondence suggests that projections of communicating classes onto faces of the amplituhedron can be interpreted as particle interactions. 60. Projection from Markov Polytope to Particle Interactions: - The cells of the Markov polytope can be seen as different flow patterns. - The projection from the Markov polytope to its cells, represented by decorated permutations, may have meaning as particle interactions in physics. 61. Agent-Particle Correspondence in Smallest Communicating Class: - The smallest non-trivial communicating class in a Markov chain is a single conscious agent. - The Markov polytope 𝑀𝑗, representing all possible dynamics of the conscious j-agent, may have projections onto the dynamics of j-particle scattering in spacetime. 62. Agent-particle correspondence: - Consider the Markov polytope ℳ3, which describes the dynamics of conscious 3-agents - Appendix B presents the 17 distinct decorated permutations for the 27 vertices of ℳ3 - The adjacency graph for ℳ3 is shown in Figure 12 - ℳ3 has projections onto the two possible on-shell diagrams for three-particle interactions shown in Figure 13 63. Physics moving on: - Newtonian physics is not fundamental and was superseded by Einstein's theory of special relativity - Quantum field theory and Einstein's theory of gravity are also not fundamental - Physics has proposed alternative theories such as string theory, loop quantum gravity, causal sets, amplituhedra, and cosmological polytopes
column19: 64. The problem with current theories of consciousness: - Current theories assume that spacetime is fundamental - Functionalists seek instantiation within spacetime - No theory explains specific conscious experiences - Leibniz argued that consciousness cannot be explained by mechanical causes - Steven Pinker acknowledges the failure of current theories to explain conscious experience 65. The theory of conscious agents: - Consciousness should not be reduced to mechanisms involving objects - The theory of conscious agents proposes a dynamics of experiences - Consciousness is not an immaterial soul but a projection of the dynamics of conscious agents - The theory aims to make formal advances with testable consequences 66. The emergence of time: - A Markovian dynamics of conscious agents with constant entropy can induce an entropic arrow of time - The theory of conscious agents may find that entropic time is not fundamental but an artifact of projection 67. The Arrow of Time and Conscious Agents: - The concept of an arrow of time is often associated with physical evolution, but it can also be seen as a projection of the dynamics of consciousness. - Markovian networks of conscious agents can explain both scattering events in spacetime and experienced temporality. 68. Evolution as a Projection of Conscious Agent Dynamics: - In the theory of conscious agents, it is suggested that the entire framework of evolution may be an artifact of projection from the dynamics of conscious agents. - This requires a rigorous specification of the dynamics of agents and their mapping into spacetime. 69. Geometry and Dynamics of Markov Polytopes: - In order to understand agent fusion and its connections with particle physics, the geometry and dynamics of Markov polytopes need to be studied. - The analysis focuses on Markov polytopes of order 2 and 3, which already demonstrate their richness and complexity. 70. Geometric Structure of Markov Polytopes: - A stochastic matrix is a Markov kernel if its entries are real numbers between 0 and 1 and its rows sum to 1. - The set of all stochastic matrices forms a compact, convex set called the Markov polytope. - The vertices of the Markov polytope are stochastic matrices with unit rows. - Markov polytopes of order 2 and 3 are multi-dimensional manifolds in ℝ2 and ℝ3×3 respectively. 71. Mapping the Parameter Space of Markov Polytopes: - The parameter space of Markov polytopes is a subset of ℝ𝑛(𝑛−1), denoted as Π𝑛. - There exists a bijective mapping from the parameter space Π𝑛 to the Markov polytope ℳ𝑛. - This mapping enables the study of the geometric structure and dynamics of Markov polytopes. 72. Convex Hull and Faces of a Polytope: - A convex hull of a set X of points in a vector space is generated by that set. - The boundary of a convex polytope consists of faces, which are maximal flat regions generated by a subset of vertices. - The dimension of a polytope is 1 more than the dimension of its facets. 73. Adjacent Vertices and Caps: - Two vertices are adjacent if they differ only in the position of a unit entry in the same row, with the other rows being identical. - The cap of a vertex V in ℳn has n(n-1) vertices. 74. Description of a Polytope: - A polytope can be described as the convex hull of its vertices (𝒱-description) or in terms of the set of half-spaces bounding the polytope (ℋ-description). - Each face of a polytope is also a polytope. 75. Faces of ℳ3: - The faces of ℳ3 can be generated by converting certain inequalities into equalities within the groups of inequalities. - There are various types of faces in each dimension of ℳ3. 76. Examples of Faces in ℳ3: - Example A1 provides examples of different types of faces in ℳ3, such as vertices, edges, triangles, squares, and higher-dimensional faces. - Proposition A2 gives details on the shapes and numbers of each type of face in ℳ3. 77. Summary Point 1: - The faces of ℳ3 can be counted and classified based on their dimensions. - There are 27 vertices, 81 edges, 108 triangles, 81 squares, 54 triangular prisms, 27 cubes, 9 9-vertex faces, 36 12-vertex faces, and 9 facets in ℳ3. 78. Summary Point 2: - Each face in ℳ3 is a polytope, and its faces are lower-dimensional faces of ℳ3. - For example, the 6-vertex 3-face is generated by two triangular faces and three square faces. 79. Summary Point 3: - The Euler–Poincaré relation holds for ℳ3: 𝑓0−𝑓1+𝑓2−𝑓3+𝑓4−𝑓5=1+(-1)^5. - This relation links the number of vertices, edges, faces, ridges, facets, and the dimension of ℳ3. 80. Summary Point 4: - The Birkhoff polytope ℬ𝑛 is a subset of ℳ𝑛 generated by doubly stochastic matrices. - The vertices of ℬ𝑛 are in a one-to-one correspondence with permutations in the symmetric group 𝑆𝑛. - The Birkhoff polytope ℬ3 has six vertices, including two derangements and three mirror matrices. 81. Summary Point 5: - The vertex matrices of ℬ𝑛 are periodic, meaning they have a period 𝑘 satisfying 𝑄^𝑘 = 𝐼. - The periodic set 𝒫𝑛 consists of all periodic stationary stochastic 𝑛×𝑛 matrices. - The k-periodic set 𝒫𝑘𝑛 consists of the period k matrices in 𝒫𝑛. 82. Summary Point 6: - In ℳ2, the Birkhoff polytope ℬ2 is a line segment between the NOT operator and the identity matrix. - In ℳ3, the Birkhoff polytope ℬ3 has additional vertices called mirror matrices, along with the two derangements and the identity matrix. 83. Summary Point 7: - Periodic matrices in ℳ2 and ℳ3 only occur at vertices of the Birkhoff polytope. - No convex combination of periodic stochastic matrices can be periodic. 84. Summary Point 8: - The conjecture A1 states that the vertex set of the Birkhoff polytope 𝒫𝑛 corresponds to the set of all periodic stochastic matrices. - For each k from 1 to n, 𝒫𝑘𝑛 consists of all Birkhoff matrices related to cyclic derangements in 𝑆𝑛. 85. Birkhoff matrices and permutations: - Birkhoff matrices correspond to permutations with unique cycle decomposition. - The fusion polytope consists of Birkhoff matrices with cycles of the same length. 86. Stationary complex and fusion complex: - The stationary complex consists of idempotent stochastic matrices. - The fusion complex consists of rank 1 matrices in the stationary manifold. - The intersection of the fusion complex and Birkhoff polytope is a singleton. 87. Fusion polytope and partial fusion complex: - The fusion polytope is an (n-1)-dimensional simplex. - The vertices of the partial fusion complex form a hexagon. - The partial fusion complex intersects with the Birkhoff polytope at three points. 88. Conjecture about fusion complexes: - The conjecture states the form and dimension of fusion complexes. - The vertices of fusion complexes follow a specific pattern. - Further study is required to analyze the structure of intermediate fusion complexes. 89. Dynamics in Markov polytopes: - Markov chains on finite state spaces are studied in Markov polytopes. - Every stochastic matrix has a stationary distribution. - The fusion polytope has unique stationary distributions. - Partial fusion matrices have a 1-simplex of stationary distributions. 90. Appendix A.2: Dynamics in Markov Polytopes: - Study of flows of Markov chains in Markov polytopes. - Stochastic matrices have stationary distributions. - Fusion polytope has unique stationary distributions. - Partial fusion matrices have a 1-simplex of stationary distributions. 91. Stationary Distributions of Matrices in Partial Fusion Complexes: - The fusion complexes ℱ𝑘𝑛(𝑘=1,…,𝑛−1) are simplices of dimension 𝑛−𝑘. - For 𝑛=2, the fusion polytope is the fusion complex and its stationary matrices have equal rows. - The Birkhoff polytope corresponds to the segment 𝑥=𝑦 including end-points. - The periodic set 𝒫22 corresponds to the green upper right corner. - The powers of any matrix other than the identity and the periodic point converge to the fusion complex. 92. Dynamics in ℳ3: - Caps and their dynamics in ℳ3 are illustrated in Figure A5. - Powers of the tail matrix become the head matrix for single solid arrows. - Powers of the cap matrices of 16 and 20 alternate between itself and the partial fusion matrix at the other end. - Dynamics between the caps and the fusion polytope are as in Figure A5. 93. Proofs: - Proof of Proposition A1: Convex hull of two vertices is an edge of the Markov polytope. - Proof of Proposition A2: Counting the number of faces in ℳ3. 94. 1. Introduction: - The blog discusses the generation of a 2-dimensional square using vertices and edges. - The process involves selecting two rows in a vertex and shifting the unit entry to its position in another vertex. 95. 2. Summary of Figures: - The blog summarizes different types of faces and their properties in the generated figure. - It describes 3-faces, cubes, 4-faces, and 5-faces with their respective number of vertices. 96. 3. Proof of Proposition A3: - The proposition states that rank 1 stochastic matrices are idempotent. - The proof shows that the matrix Q squared equals Q. 97. 4. Proof of Lemma A1: - The lemma states the existence of a fixed point of the continuous map Q. - The proof uses Brower's fixed point theorem. 98. 5. Proof of Theorem A1: - The theorem discusses the behavior of the sequence (Q(p0)^n) as it approaches the cross-diagonal. - The proof shows that the sequence is strictly monotonic towards the cross-diagonal. 99. 6. Appendix B - Decorated Permutations: - The appendix provides decorated permutations for the 27 vertices of the Markov Polytope ℳ3. - References for further reading on the topic are also included. 100. The missing science of consciousness: - This blog discusses the search for the missing science of consciousness. - The author references various studies and theories related to the nature of consciousness. 101. Integrated Information Theory: - The Integrated Information Theory 3.0 is explored in relation to the mechanisms of consciousness. - The theory discusses the integration of information in the brain and its relationship to subjective experience. 102. Spatial Experience and Consciousness: - The blog delves into the question of why space feels the way it does in relation to consciousness. - It explores a principled account of spatial experience and its connection to consciousness. 103. Mechanism integrated information: - The concept of mechanism integrated information is discussed in relation to consciousness. - The blog highlights a study that investigates the relationship between mechanism integrated information and consciousness. 104. The Global Neuronal Workspace Hypothesis: - The Global Neuronal Workspace Hypothesis is introduced as a framework for understanding conscious processing. - The hypothesis proposes that consciousness arises from the global availability of information in the brain. 105. Different Theories of Consciousness: - The blog explores various theories of consciousness proposed by different researchers, such as Baars, Dehaene, and Dennett. - These theories offer different perspectives on the nature and mechanisms of consciousness. 106. Panpsychism and Physicalism: - The concept of panpsychism, which suggests that consciousness is a fundamental property of the universe, is mentioned. - The blog also discusses the relationship between physicalism and panpsychism. 107. The Emergence of Spacetime and Consciousness: - The emergence of spacetime and its connection to consciousness is explored. - The blog references the work of Arkani-Hamed and Schneider on this topic. 108. Public Objects and Private Qualia: The Scope and Limits of Psychophysics: - Hoffman, D.D. discusses the scope and limits of psychophysics in relation to public objects and private qualia. - The chapter is part of the Handbook of Experimental Phenomenology. 109. Does evolution favor true perceptions?: - Hoffman, D.D.; Singh, M.; Mark, J.T. explore whether true perceptions are favored by evolution. - The study is discussed in the context of Human Vision and Electronic Imaging XVIII. 110. World, environment, Umwelt, and innerworld: A biological perspective on visual awareness: - Koenderink, J.J. provides a biological perspective on visual awareness, discussing the concepts of world, environment, Umwelt, and innerworld. - The chapter is part of Human Vision and Electronic Imaging XVIII. 111. The all seeing eye?: - Koenderink, J.J. explores the concept of the all-seeing eye and its implications for perception. - The study was published in Perception. 112. The physicalist trap in perception theory: - Mausfeld, R. discusses the physicalist trap in perception theory. - The discussion is part of the book Perception and the Physical World: Psychological and Philosophical Issues in Perception. 113. Umwelt und Innenwelt der Tiere: - von Uexküll, J. explores the concepts of Umwelt and Innenwelt in relation to animals. - The book provides insights into the worlds of animals and men. 114. A stroll through the worlds of animals and men: A picture book of invisible worlds: - von Uexküll, J. takes readers on a stroll through the invisible worlds of animals and men. - The book provides a visual depiction of these worlds. 115. Eigenforms, interfaces and holographic encoding: - Fields, C.; Hoffman, D.D.; Prakash, C.; Prentner, R. discuss eigenforms, interfaces, and holographic encoding. - The study is published in Constr. Found. 116. Conscious Agents and Their Interactions: - Conscious agents interact based on their experiences. - Figure 1 illustrates a diagram of a conscious agent interacting with other agents. 117. Qualia and Agent Interactions: - Figure 3 shows the qualia kernels of two 1-agents, one experiencing red and the other experiencing green. - These agents do not interact, and their qualia do not combine. 118. Markovian Kernels and Their Flow: - Figure 4 illustrates the flow of Markovian kernels on the Markov polytope ℳ2. - The polytope is a unit square where arrowheads indicate the direction and speed of flow. 119. Invariant Measures on Markov Polytope: - Figure 5 shows invariant measures on the Markov polytope ℳ2. - Kernels with the same invariant measure lie on a straight line passing through the origin. 120. Stationary Kernels in Markov Polytope: - Figure 6 depicts invariant measures and stationary kernels in the Markov polytope ℳ2. - Kernels are associated with points (𝑥,𝑦) and color indicates the mix of red and green in the invariant measure. 121. Fusions of Conscious Agents: - Figures 7 and 8 represent the unit 1-simplex and unit 2-simplex of fusions for conscious agents, respectively. 122. On-Shell Diagrams and Decorated Permutations: - Figure 9 shows an on-shell diagram for the decorated permutation 𝜎=[3,4,5,6]. - Figure 10 demonstrates the construction of a reduced on-shell diagram corresponding to a decorated permutation. 123. Cell Complex and Adjacency Graph: - Figure 11 displays the cell complex for ℳ2 and the associated on-shell diagrams. - Figure 12 shows the adjacency graph for the Markov polytope ℳ3, including the total fusion simplex and partial fusion complex. 124. Fusions of Consciousness: - The article titled "Fusions of Consciousness" explores the concept of conscious fusion. - Written by Donald D. Hoffman, Chetan Prakash, and Robert Prentner, the article delves into the idea that consciousness can merge and combine. 125. Article Metrics: - The article has received citations from Crossref, Web of Science, and Scopus. - Google Scholar also provides additional information on the article.

what is the information graph of the universe and what does it have to do with consciousness?
column5: we are conscious in delimited ways. Those ways are shaped by information exchange with other delimited ways--other conscious entities, and those exchanges may or may not be included in the ways we are conscious (i.e., we may not be aware of these information exchanges).
column6: Some of the interactions between ways we are conscious and other conscious entities which do in fact interact with us and whose interaction with us is in some way part of our delimited consciousness are perceived by us as part of models wherein our preferred states are rendered more probable through the processing of information through that model; others not. These "others not" strike as as brute facts about the world not subject to our immediate control; others (within our model's input and output) strike us as changeable.
column7: For example, I am aware of text on the screen, letters on a keyboard, hands which change both keys and screentext , and more satisfactory percerived outcomes through attending to this screentext/keys/hands system. So I attend--I process information--and the world gets better, more probably the way I prefer it to be. This whole set of interactions is a model of the world in which screentext captures thoughts--states of awareness--that stand in preferred probability relations to other and future sets of experience of mine; i.e., I think the world is likely to get better as a result the understanding--itself a set of probability relations among certain perceived states of affairs--that becomes part of the model by which I process future information (awarenesses) of the world, either consciously or possibly conscious-of-unconsciously (e.g. improving my body's relationship with itself over time but undermeath awareness, with outcomes popping out of a model in which we have some faith without much if any visibility: we can be dimly aware of but largely trust, unaware, that elements in occasional, somewhat-aware interaction stand in regular preferred-state-optimization relations with other elements of our awareness. We take statin to optimize our model of cell wall artery health in its relationship to heart health, all with only occasional and varied levels of awareness of these elements of our model of our own physical health; where "health" is itself an optimized relationship to preferred states of consciousness.)
column10: So, running down this road thus lightly armed, what are internal vs external states, "my body"; "my mind", "my awareness", me, myself and I, etc. Moreover, what is going on in LLMs (to take a complex example), and how can we possibly give a pCRm account of the training of LLMs, the being of LLMs, our interaction with LLMs, etc. And here LLMs are a first instance of a general inquiry into the pCRm status of AI.
column11: How about this: "I" am a model. That model is useful for optimizing preferred states of affairs (perhaps including SOA of which I am aware, and others of which I am not directly aware (think here of "my connectome" as a model explaining that of which I am richly and that of which I am only dimly or indirectly aware, as Hercleano-x) as capable of being rendered more probably and advantageous through processing of a model which is used by me so regularly that I identify with it as my own, my self. // Is a model, a pattern of perceived control? Is perceived control a relationship between perceived processing and perceived heightened probability of preferred states of affairs? Is the "I" of my experience a product--a model--created by patterns of successful optimization between perceived states of affairs, some of which seem to improve the likelihood of certain abstractions from other states of affairs perceived as desirable?
column13: How about this: an LLM is a model. It turns (processes in ways characterized by attention models, etc)  a very large set of expressions of relations between conscious experiences (captured in training data) brought in perceived (modeled through relations of abstractions) contact with other such expressions (captured in contextualization data), to the production of preferred screentext first through weighting interactions (through the supervisory community) and finally through prompt engineering interactions with end users. On such a model of LLMs, the ontology is a pCRm ontology of relations among conscious experiences. All that exists are conscious experiences with two sort of fundamental properties: desirability, and probability relations among them. Perhaps constructor theory ( itself a model at a very high level of abstraction) will map these probability relations and allow all conscious experiences that are not impossible (p=0)
column15: Back to the universe information graph: is all information conscious awareness, including preference and probability values? Does preference within the confines of probability determine the forward direction of information processing, as well as our experience of time (limitation of processing speed reflecting probability relations, or perhaps reflected in probability relations: which is primary, and in which sense, if either?) All entities are (on this model) models we generate at higher levels of abstraction to speed the production (arrival at) preferred conscious states? There is giving and receiving; there is application of a shared model to some new and shared set of instances, observables, things-in-arrangement. column11: On such an approach to love, loving is unitive; it casts a vote of faith in another, not abstractly, but practically--not just in the articulation of some model, but in its applicatiion: use, not mere mention, of a model. Brain rewiring happens when you love. Enjoyment grows. (So does suffering; so does life.) To process information more is to live more; to love others is to live more, to include their struggles and enjoyment as your own; to befriend, to "other-self". These are my children, my life's-blood.)To love is to elevate, to include, to co-participate, to unify. To love more fully is to include more completely, with greater awareness and intention; it is to work their efforts more deeply into one's model, to intertwine prospects more thoroughly.

The failure to make room for the heart, as distinct from our human powers and passions viewed in isolation from one another, has resulted in a stunting of the idea of a personal centre, in which love, in the end, is the one reality that can unify all the others." column5: The heart is the integrator of human faculties and experiences. This includes much we can't pin down, but of which we are somehow dimly aware; we sense. column6: And this (#11) bears much thought: "11. If we devalue the heart, we also devalue what it means to speak from the heart, to act with the heart, to cultivate and heal the heart. If we fail to appreciate the specificity of the heart, we miss the messages that the mind alone cannot communicate; we miss out on the richness of our encounters with others; we miss out on poetry. We also lose track of history and our own past, since our real personal history is built with the heart. At the end of our lives, that alone will matter."

column4: I, Tom, a conscious agent, can calculate things. It seems like I can calculate those things consciously. I can solve a maze, consciously, by eliminating possibilities and holding them in memory. Now if you were to cut my corpus callosum (or remove it, not sure.) I would have too consciousnesses, instead of one. Or perhaps I would not be, and those two would be. Either way, both of them seem to be conscious. It's not clear how they were conscious before… If they were, we had no insight into that. but it certainly isn't a huge sleep to think of both hemispheres as conscious agents. They calculate, and they calculate differently. They have a full set of wants, perceptions, actions. At least they both do, the moment you set them free by separating them from one another.(This is freedom in at least some sense; each seems to have a measure of control over its own actions now.) (now suppose that the roughly 500 neocortical columns that are calculating the world and presenting multiple pictures of it for resolution into the one story that will be adopted for each hemisphere, for the same relationship to the hemispheres as the hemispheres due to the whole brain. If that's the case, it's hard not to call the hemispheres, conscious, though you might deny the self consciousness. They would not be, in fact, selves, when they are working together, but elements of a single unit. But that single unit is conscious, the stroke of the scalpel: there is no substantial reconstruction, certainly no evolution, that goes on between the colostomy and dual conscious agency. What, then, keeps us from pushing the same insight down a level, to the neocortical columns, which we already know are individually calculating multiple copies of the same world, and then putting those copies together in someway that resolves differences and results in a unified field of perception? What keeps them from being conscious of this: do we suppose language facility is hiding somewhere else other than in the completely functional cortical columns, and their relationship with the rest of the brain? in this process could continue, although, from the fact that it can start, it doesn't all follow that it can never stop. Perhaps anything we would call consciousness is a more complex phenomenon than we might suppose, more complex than cognition. But we didn't used to think that cognition went all the way down, either. And now we know that, as it moves down in the hierarchy of complexity toward the more simple, consciousness sometimes follows a step or two. So it's natural enough to ask, whether some evermore simple level of consciousness might follow along with ever more simple levels of cognition, all the way down.
column5: Here's another line of reasoning toward the same consciousness – everywhere, conclusion. What exactly does consciousness do, and why would it involve over and above cognition? Interestingly, enough, either the two hemispheres are always conscious, or they just become conscious when separated. But that operation, that popping into being of a pair of consciousnesses, would seem to have no evolutionary history: it's just not how we evolved, coming together, from two separate hemispheres, half – headed human beings, interacting, perhaps meeting and reproducing. We know of no such history. How then, does consciousness evolve at the hemispheric level, or if it's never been there prior to the first corpus callostomy , what's the evolutionary pressure for it to come into being at that level? But if there's pressure for her to come to be at that level, why does that same pressure apply and one level lower, at the 1000 active, 100,000 standing ready neocortical columns, which each calculate a whole picture of the world, but separately?
column6: Third, one of the marks of consciousness, according to chronicler (name here), is the construction of an integrated field. We know that such a field can be integrated differently than it integrates now: in color, blind persons, for example, less is integrated then in a color-sighted person. From comparative sensory system studies, we believe that what animals perceive is significantly different, in some cases, simply other parent, such as insects who can perceive UV light); in other cases, with different and more simple colors, or simplified in other ways. Why not suppose that consciousness of each of the separated elements, if not all of them, then some of them as separated, is it itself a conscious state? We reserve the idea of quality for such states. We can induce more simple states in ourselves, as we might, if we floated in a tank, looking up at a lit white ceiling only, or a blue one. Such states seem possible conscious states. If so, then why not suppose that as we get more simple structurally, the kind of cognition, simpler at that level, that these lower states have is itself conscious? Why suppose there is a hard problem of consciousness to solve at all?
column7: Why suppose there is a hard problem of consciousness to solve at all? Why not suppose that the hard problem is the emergence of the physical world? Here, I mean the physical world that science creates models of? As to the question of why we would need models at all of something that doesn't exist, Donald Hoffman has answered that question for us quite plainly: for a kind of evolutionary fitness. Evolution is a process need not be restricted to the unpacking of ultimately physical interactions between physical beings. Evolutionary game theory shows us this much. So why not pose there is a kind of evolution that takes place at a conscious level, with a whole range of simplified and more complexlevels of consciousness?
column8: Well, why not suppose that it is matter that individuals things have one form from things of another… What would do that in the case of consciousness well, perhaps starting at the most simple level, nothing does. There is only an appearance of a certain shade of green. There are two of those that are exactly alike. That's not to say that there might not be different conscious agents each with that same consciousness, but that would be precisely because they are joined. With multiple conscious agents, precisely the way, Lemann and others are showing us takes place throughout a complex range of biological phenomena. And this is not as strange as it thinks: imagine otherwise, in the single case. Imagine another person who perceived exactly what you perceive. What do you suppose individuals the two of you? Why aren't you the very same thing? Perhaps what it is to be a particular is to be a particular way of seeing the world. For conscious agents, at least for more complex, conscious agents, this amount to the model we have for interacting with the world, a kind of story about the world. We tell ourselves to simplify it so that we can better interact with it. Maybe narrative is the principle of individual situation; designated that is particular, narratives, are what separate Into particular beings. This lack of information… Where I know my story, but I don't know yours, at least not the same way you do… may be precisely what spins particular patterns of being out of a field of consciousness. Here we get very close to Wolfram: the laws of physics, including quantum and relativity, emerge out of his observer theory on simply two presuppositions: that there is some limitation on the ability to compute; and that there is some sense of persistence through time. Perhaps that limited cognition is just the ability, or rather the inability, to process everything from every point of view at once. If we could, we would be the same. But we can't, so we're not. Limitation is the principle of individual, or what we might call, designated narrative. But limited stories have elements with logical relations among them, and perhaps the logic of considering one element logically before another – – the tree before the wood, the wood before the wooden staircase – – perhaps passing through that process in the sense of attending to each feature in some logical order is what produces the appearance of time,, the sense of time which qualifies us as temporal beings and is the second of only two necessary requirements for the emergence of relativity and quantum out of simple computation.
column9: This analysis of consciousness has an advantage of harmonizing well with the analysis of selfhood that Lemann and others argue is identifiable, differentiable, and manipulable in the physical world, although I should now say, the world we are conscious of as a physical world. That last reframing Seems tautologous, perhaps, but it's not. Referring to the physical world is different than referring to the physical world of which we are conscious. The first reference suggest something independent of our minds, Salmon; the second reference doesn't at all. Among the things we learned from our model of the physical world, and so far as it has illumine for us, the model of our brain and its relationship to other parts of the world of which we are conscious, is that our brains reach out and create the elements. We are conscious of this is Kristin's active inference, his minimization of free energy, has first and in love and have written together. and this, of course, the law of universal present preservation of state, is very much like the preservation of action, sometimes called the principle of least action, but perhaps better called the principle of constant action, or preservation of action. column6:
"Yes, And..." ... a comprehensive collection of Richard Rohr’s thought and teaching, based on seven themes:

Methodology: Scripture as validated by experience, and experience as validated by tradition, are good scales for one’s spiritual worldview.
Foundation: If God is Trinity and Jesus is the face of God, then it is a benevolent universe. God is not someone to be afraid of, but is the Ground of Being and on our side.
Frame: There is only one Reality. Any distinction between natural and supernatural, sacred and profane is a bogus one.
Ecumenism: Everything belongs and no one needs to be scapegoated or excluded. Evil and illusion only need to be named and exposed truthfully, and they die in exposure to the light.
Transformation: The separate self is the problem, whereas most religion and most people make the "shadow self" the problem. This leads to denial, pretending, and projecting instead of real transformation into the Divine.
Process: The path of descent is the path of transformation. Darkness, failure, relapse, death, and woundedness are our primary teachers, rather than ideas or doctrines.
Goal: Reality is paradoxical and complementary. Non-dual thinking is the highest level of consciousness. Divine union, not private perfection, is the goal of all religion. https://store.cac.org/products/yes-and-daily-meditations?_gl=1*1lr7n8a*_gcl_au*MzI2NTE5NDU5LjE3NDgwMDU0MzU.

But the thing about information is that it can be instantiated in multiple external systems...(though as A noted, those systems themselves have some underlying form, pattern, "informatino" in today's parlance, as if it were information all the way down, to "primary matter", featureless matter, pure potentiality. Pure possibility? Or, for Aquinas, "disignated matter", that is, possibility assigned with some basic particularlity?)
column6: But doesn't the same information get carried by, instantiated in, various external environments? And isn't it precisely ABSTRACTION, the act of noticing (an energetic investment), which is an act of model creation, which is a condensation, a compression, a means of interacting with our perceptions (to be information is to inform?) by investing it with energy, with causal power, seeing it as multiply instantiable, that is, as a model? (Think of colored text made out of tiles taken from around various fireplaces, starting with the downward-opening "C" from my fireplace. That's investment, on my part; that's model creation. I can unpack that variously, freely, creatively, preserving and instantiating information differently. Can we assign values to this process of investment and extraction of payoff, this creation of models and recreation of instances? Is that what Karl Friston has done? Is this Active Inference? Can we quantify the investment we make, as "free energy", useful investment?
column8: Does Sarah Walker's and Lee Cronin's Assembly Theory provide one pathway to quantifying this investment, through logically-required steps? column4: Carlo Roveli: On agents in physics, which are instances of physical states that are described in abstraction all of the details of microstates; these agents are in effect models that are--because they are described at a level of abstraction removed from microstate precursors--compatible with multiple future states. When one of those future states is chosen, order is introduced to the system of perceived states; that order must come from somewhere. Roveli's claim is that it comes from the loss of order in the process of agent creation / recreation. (Think Wolfram: this is the "persisting through time conception" in his observer theory.)
column5: "Since choosing is irreversible it cannot happen without entropy increase. Therefore during the interval ta − tb there must be an entropy increase ∆S > 0. On the other hand, suppose we observe the macroscopic evolution. Before the time ta we have no information about which branch will the system follow. After the time tb we can see which branch has been realised, hence we have novel information. Where does the information come from? The only possible answer is that I is paid for by the increase in entropy ∆S." https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.05300 p. 4
column6: The loss of order in the formation of agency is the reason why agentic activity is irreversible with respect to time, whereas transformation across physical microstates is time reversible.
column7: Roveli also cashes in his analysis of thermodynamic implications of agency into an account of memory and information. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2003.06687

column5: "By showcasing work in diverse, currently disconnected fields, we hope to sketch the outline of a new multidisciplinary approach for comprehending cognition, arguably the most fascinating and hard-to-fathom evolved function on this planet. Doing so has the potential to shed light on problems in a wide variety of research domains, including microbiology, immunology, zoology, biophysics, botany, developmental biology, neurobiology/science, regenerative medicine, computational biology, artificial life and synthetic bioengineering."
column6: "Understanding behaviour in all its forms will require a dramatic shift in perspective. The result, however, should be a potentially productive cross-fertilization of the life sciences and the cognitive sciences that could help to solve major problems in both domains, which currently barely reference one another. Indeed, some of the most powerful advances in science have been unifications—discovering the invariant core that underlies phenomena that were previously thought to be distinct. Taking seriously modern evolutionary and cell biology arguably now requires recognition that the information-processing dynamics of ‘simpler’ forms of life are part of a continuum with human cognition. The commonalities are mechanistic, not metaphorical."
column8: "Connections among all 302 neurons of the tiny nematode Caenorhabditis elegans have been mapped, a stunning technical achievement from a half-century of detailed experimental effort, but we still don’t know how the wiring diagram generates behaviour in a living worm [9]. Developing the tools so that we can understand how brains generate decision-making behaviour in real time is the mission of the US Brain Initiative and its international partners [10], but we need more than tools. We need a diversity of new models, as well as theories that apply not only to familiar ‘standard’ model species but to the entire option space of cognitive agents, which includes aneural organisms, somatic organs and novel bioengineered synthetic forms. Most of all, we need new theory"

column17: "New (similar) findings appear almost weekly in a wide variety of disparate journals that until now have had a limited impact beyond their individual fields of study, for example, microbiology, zoology, botany, biophysics, developmental biology, neurobiology, regenerative medicine, computational biology, immunology, artificial life, synthetic bioengineering, animal behaviour and cognitive science. Absent a connecting framework, this research will continue to be produced in specialist bubbles where discussion remains mostly parochial and evolutionary development often obscure. Developing a connecting framework is the aim of basal cognition research."
column19: What do we mean by ‘cognition’? The short answer is we don’t know because we can’t agree, and unambiguous, biologically grounded proposals are effectively non-existent. Cognition—and where it is found in the natural world—has been an inexhaustably meaty bone of contention since (in ‘western’ culture) Aristotle (384–322 BCE), for whom animals marked a singular transition [35], and (in ‘eastern’ culture) the Rig Veda (1700–1000 BCE) and associated texts, many of which admitted cognition in plants [36]. Not so long ago, it was suggested that an adequate definition would be possible when we have sufficient data [37], but this is not how it has worked out. The data available even 20 years ago ‘almost inhibited meaning’ [38]. Today the data are oceanic and meaning seems impossible without machine learning to detect relevant patterns. Defining cognition can even seem unnecessary, leaving its articulation a matter of scientific progress [39,40]. However, we do not have that luxury in this case. We cannot claim that basal cognition is a worthy research pursuit if we fail to provide at least a provisional working definition of the critical construct.
column21: Cognition comprises the sensory and other information-processing mechanisms an organism has for becoming familiar with, valuing, and interacting productively with features of its environment [exploring, exploiting, evading] in order to meet existential needs, the most basic of which are survival/persistence, growth/ thriving, and reproduction.  [21, p. 416] Should we replace this as "...(in order to) explore, discover identify and obtain goals achievable individually and collectively"?
column22: "A fairly straightforward entailment of this approach is that cognition is a function necessary for any autonomous biological system’s survival, wellbeing and reproduction, which is an uncomfortable proposition for many. We say that whether one wishes to concede cognition to prokaryotes (for example) remains a matter of personal choice. This is not the issue before us. The issue before us is whether proceeding as though this were the case, in a biologically realistic fashion,is productive. The answer to that question, we believe, is yes."
column24: For example, bacteria produce molecules (autoinducers) that they secrete into the environment. When a threshold density of these molecules is reached, which requires large numbers of other bacteria doing the same thing, the population as a whole coordinate metabolically costly changes in genetic expression that would be unproductive in just a few individuals, a phenomenon called quorum sensing [44]
column25: Basal cognition, like uncaveated cognition, importantly involves learning, including via epigenetic and genetic embedding in the course of evolutionary change, what neuroscientist Antonio Damasio elegantly calls ‘holding know-how in dispositions’ [46]. How is "holding know-how in dispositions" any different than storing advantageous information in habits AKA virtue? Do cells have virtue? Do they have beneficial habits, habits conducive to their ends, habits whose conducivity may lie outside their own range of experience, but depend rather on the know-how of the group?

column4: Question, while listening to the master and his emissary. The general question is, is there a relationship between the two hemispheres, and their differences, on the one hand, and the two requirements for observer status, and their differences, on the other. The first from me in McGilchrist; the second, from Stephen Wolfram.
column5: There are two requirements, or rather, features, of observers, which together (according to Stephen Wolfram) generate relativistic and quantum perspectives on the world: the first, that there is limited computational speed; the second, that there is a sense of enduring in time. The first of these, limited computational speed, seems to bear somehow on the right hemispheres attention to particulars. There's a limit to how many particulars you can attend to. If you wanna overcome that limit, you have to build a model, which is what the left brain does. Such models require energy to create, but then provide efficiency, when deployed. And in order to have a number of things that are not present at the same time to fall under the same model (concepts are treated as models, here), there must be some underlying sameness that unites the experience of separate instances; that is, there must be endurance through time. This raises a question or two: is there a relationship between the complementarity between our hemispheres, and the complementarity between quantum and relativistic physics? And if there's a relationship, what's the direction of causation: does the physics produce the hemispheres; or do the hemispheres produce the physics; or does some additional cause, such as the interaction of conscious agents on Hoffman view, produce both? Or is causation just an artifact of left-brain, temporally asymetric thinking? Is right brain thinking and memory time-reversible, becoming time-ordered only through left-brain activity? Only when I think of causation, do I need to order particular events...is that true? Perhaps my right brain functioning requires only more and more neurons to grasp more and more particulars, which the left brain then orders...reflecting Wolfram's requirements on Observers (in his Observer Theory) to get to Q and R, both? Is the incommensurable nature of the Q and R perspectives in physics a reflection of our our minds work (perhaps in turn reflected in our split-brain models of them) of complementarity among conscious agents, alternative perspectives both (all--1000 Brain Theory) providing valuable interaction among complementary perspectives, which is the same as to say, among conscious agents?

column5: from Bernardo Kastrup: To make sense of this conundrum, we don’t need the word games of information realism. Instead, we must stick to what is most immediately present to us: solidity and concreteness are qualities of our experience. The world measured, modeled and ultimately predicted by physics is the world of perceptions, a category of mentation. The phantasms and abstractions reside merely in our descriptions of the behaviorof that world, not in the world itself. Where we get lost and confused is in imagining that what we are describing is a non-mental reality underlying our perceptions, as opposed to the perceptions themselves. We then try to find the solidity and concreteness of the perceived world in that postulated underlying reality. However, a non-mental world is inevitably abstract. And since solidity and concreteness are felt qualities of experience—what else?—we cannot find them there. The problem we face is thus merely an artifact of thought, something we conjure up out of thin air because of our theoretical habits and prejudices.

column10: Tegmark is correct in considering matter—defined as something outside and independent of mind—to be unnecessary baggage. But the implication of this fine and indeed brave conclusion is that the universe is a mental construct displayed on the screen of perception. Tegmark’s “mathematical universe” is inherently a mental one, for where does mathematics—numbers, sets, equations—exist if not in mentation?
column12: As I elaborate extensively in my new book, The Idea of the World, none of this implies solipsism. The mental universe exists in mind but not in your personal mind alone. Instead, it is a transpersonal field of mentation that presents itself to us as physicality—with its concreteness, solidity and definiteness—once our personal mental processes interact with it through observation. This mental universe is what physics is leading us to, not the hand-waving word games of information realism.

column5: Some notes after cross referencing important passages in McIntire across his work and those of his critics, especially McKinney.
column6: McKinney's third type of relativist outcome, with some context and implications: the first type of relativist outcome, or better, relativist moment, is the relativism that endures only until second first language, competency exchange takes place, and adherence of one tradition abandon their perspective for some variety of reasons Which need not be described again here. The second type is something like an abandonment of ship: no assessment of the rational superiority of one tradition over the other is made, but rather some incomplete analysis of it suffices to move inhabitants from one tradition to another. This is plainly not an instance of the dual immersion algorithm at work. The third type is one in which adherence of some modern tradition, unable to recognize or unwilling to recognize, and thus enter into the kind of rigors required to achieve second first language competency in a more tradition-laden perspective, are simply unmotivated by any consideration to which they currently have access to abandon their perspective, or even to explore sufficiently its rival. McKinney builds out such a case, with rival history, told from this modern, tradition – free perspective in which the relative positions of the two traditions seem similar, from its own perspective, or perhaps even its own perspective, rationally preferable, according to its own internal standards of rationality.
column8: McIntyre allows that this sort of situation can obtain. (Check again his response to McKinney.) (This, too, is an instance of MacIntyre's algorithm failing to be applied.) The strategy I want to adopt is to shift to an information processing component, or rather, information processing language that gets at a component of MacIntyre's algorithm, which has two important features. First, it simply has a face appeal to people of our age. There does seem to be a kind of information about what it is like to be a member of a certain kind of tradition, an imaginative participation in a state of affairs we do not adopt; the neuroscience of this gets the logic of counterfactuals, the use of the imagination and planning, is prelinguistic, and very evolutionarily deep. The extension of that imagination to a mature grasp of traditions, has an intuitive appeal to it. But secondly, we are driven to it by the most promising research programs in contemporary science. Levin & Friston, united in cognition all the way down, argue that any complex system will minimize error, promote active inference, maximize available free energy. Does it follow that humankind, as an organism, will be driven toward such information-rich comparisons as MacIntyre's algorithm entails? Does it follow that there are conditions in which individuals can be brought to such full body consideration, under certain circumstances? And if that's the case, then perhaps McIntyre's algorithm requires just the right kind of experimental circumstances  for it to be implemented, circumstances, which might be as hard as those involved in global particle physics, but perhaps not obviously more so.
column10: Even if the circumstances is required to bring individuals into engagement with M's algorithm  are not readily available, the prospect that they are in principle available, necessarily coupled with efforts to produce such circumstances, and thus such comparisons, sheds a different light on intractable disputes than McKinney allows. For is quite possible to understand everything about McKinney's argument, to accept the states of affairs he envisions as leaving incommensurability unaddressed, and yet provide rational grounds, not only internal to a robust tradition, such as Thomism, but also internal to a much broader genus of traditions, which accept the information-processing model, and the deliverances of contemporary science, and thus provide warranted assertability of an asymmetry between the perspectives of the inhabitant of the two types of traditions, which McKinney envisions in his third moment of incommensurability.
column11: So there is a response available to the adherent of MacIntyre's position on incommensurability, understanding MacIntyre's algorithm as requiring the ability to turn the resources of tradition onto the analysis of a nontraditional, modern perspective, as required also by a plausible interpretation of available information relevant to the assessment between traditions. That piece is pure MacIntyre. But adding in important elements from contemporary science can further strengthen that position, as I have alluded to in pointing to Levin and Friston. This might provide a stronger defense for Catholic social teaching, breaking the symmetry of incommensurability that McKinney has identified, with a decided advantage to not only the Thomistic tradition, but to any tradition which embodies in some measure the rationality of traditions as MacIntyre has unfolded it. Here we are close to the point at which MacIntyre closed his seminal work, Whose Justice… We have reached a point in the argument where adherents can move forward only by writing different next chapters. For there are elements common to some traditions which begin to solve certain kinds of problems, including this problem of incommensurability, and yet which don't solve every problem. That foundation has to be built out, adding other components, which address some problems, cause others, and thus characterizes the history of some more particularly embodied tradition. That is work that needs to be done afresh, in every age, for every tradition. If we can conceive of the rationality of traditions as something like the rules of a game, we need to put a specific embodied worldview, analogous to a team on the field, and rival teams can then be fielded, and it's possible that the various teams will bring with them other commitments that enhance or perhaps conflict with those commitments already entailed by the rules of the game. Nothing less could be expected, on McIntyre's account of inseparably theoretical and practical, holistic rational inquiry. Putting such a team on the field, by writing for our time what St. Thomas did with the Summa Theologia in his, is an inseperable next step toward recovery of the rationality of traditions.
column13: Yet such a step would not be far enough. For while there may be advantages laden in MacIntyre's work not yet recognized in his exchange with critics, those critics and MacIntyre agreed entirely on the workload entailed by engaging (may I now call them) computational traditions with one another or with any noncomputational tradition. For to be informed as a mature participant in a computational tradition, one must become a first language speaker, learning from scratch, as it were, as if from youth. This effort amounts to growing up again, a time- and energy-intensive process. (Or as Stephen Wolfram would put it, a computationally irreducible process.) There are no easy ways through, here, though there are and should be efficiencies, as there are with the education of any child in any tradition. There are also empirical questions regarding the extent to which one person's experience in such an upbringing would be indicative of the outcome of another person's experience. The "Herculean" and "strenous" efforts, the "difficult exercise" (IDNL 207, 208, 34) of MacIntyre's own immersions in such traditions turn out to be the norm, not in what is accomplished--far from it--but in what must be accomplished for implementation of MacIntyre's algorithm. This requirement of ardure is common to any science (looking ahead.) So as a practical matter, recovery of the rationality of traditions seems a world-wide effort of unfeasible proportion. Unless, that is, we think about how science deals with such requirements.

column5: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7935032/pdf/rstb.2019.0750.pdf   Lyon P, Keijzer F, Arendt D, Levin M. Reframing cognition: getting down to biological basics. Philos Trans R Soc Lond B Biol Sci. 2021 Mar 15;376(1820):20190750. doi: 10.1098/rstb.2019.0750. Epub 2021 Jan 25. PMID: 33487107; PMCID: PMC7935032.
column6: Taking Shettleworth as the jumping-off point, we offer the following working definition: Cognition comprises the sensory and other information-processing mechanisms an organism has for becoming familiar with, valuing, and interacting productively with features of its environment [exploring, exploiting, evading] in order to meet existential needs, the most basic of which are survival/persistence, growth/ thriving, and reproduction. [21, p. 416] This phyletically neutral definition specifies the adaptive value of cognition for an organism and has the additional virtue of differentiating cognition from metabolic functions, such as respiration, which arguably also employ mechanisms for acquiring, processing and acting on information to meet existential goals but does not involve familiarization, valuing, exploring, exploiting and evading [21]. Information remains uncharacterized but can be explicated in the biological context, as follows: A state of affairs is information for an organism if it triggers a change in physiology or behaviour relative to that state of affairs. Whatever state of affairs induces a change in physiology or interactive potential in an organism is information for that organism. (adapted from [43, pp. 17, 21]) This definition allows for regulatory stochasticity in the absence of a detectable change in environmental conditions. A fairly straightforward entailment of this approach is that cognition is a function necessary for any autonomous biological system’s survival, wellbeing and reproduction, which is an uncomfortable proposition for many. We say that whether one wishes to concede cognition to prokaryotes (for example) remains a matter of personal choice. This is not the issue before us. The issue before us is whether proceeding as though this were the case, in a biologically realistic fashion, is productive. The answer to that question, we believe, is yes.
column10: A major implication of basal cognition is not only the plausibility but also the usefulness of viewing other problems in biology through a ‘cognitive lens’, that is, ‘a strategy using well-established concepts from cognitive and computer science’ in a non-metaphorical sense ‘to complement mechanistic investigation in biology’ [53]. Needless to say, informatic terminology has been used productively in biology for the past half-century. Until now, however, it has been on the general understanding that this terminology implies nothing about cognitive processes. The cognitive lens approach suggests that given evolution—not simply conceptual utility—it makes sense that information-dependent developmental processes in multicellular organisms (e.g. embryogenesis; tissue growth, maintenance and repair; regeneration; immunity) might share fitness-enhancing processes derived from the cognitive endowments of their unicellular ancestors. As a field, then, basal cognition has the potential to bring into productive contact heretofore disparate sectors of the life sciences in order to develop the conceptual and theoretical tools necessary for answering outstanding fundamental questions in a variety of areas of biology, not simply the cognitive sciences. For example, if the tools of cognitive science can be extended to apply to unconventional embodiments, novel applications (e.g. training or motivating tissues for regenerative medicine contexts) become feasible [54].
